question: An alternating voltage of amplitude $40\,V$ and frequency $4\,k\,Hz$ is applied directly across the capacitor of  $12\,\mu F.$  The maximum displacement current between the plates of the capacitor is nearly
explanation:
Given, ${V_0} = 40\,V,$ $f = 4k\,Hz = 4 \times {10^3}\,Hz$ $C = 12 \times {10^{ - 6}}\,F$
Voltage at any time ' $t$ ' is given by,
$V = {V_0}\sin \,(\omega t) = {V_0}\sin \,(2\pi ft)$
$\therefore V = 40\,\sin \left( {2\pi \times 4 \times {{10}^3}} \right)$
$ = 40\,\sin \left( {8\pi \times {{10}^3}t} \right)V$
Displacement current, $I_{d}=$ Conduction current, $I_{C}$
$\therefore I_{d}=C \dfrac{d V}{d t}$
$=12 \times 10^{-6} \times\left[40 \times 8 \pi \times 10^{3} \cos \left(8 \pi \times 10^{3} t\right)\right]$
$=12.05 \cos \left(8 \pi \times 10^{3} t\right)$
$\therefore$ Maximum displacement current,
${\left( {{I_d}} \right)_{\max }} = 12.05\,A\quad \left( {\therefore {\text{For}}{{\left( {{I_d}} \right)}_{\max ,}}\cos \theta = 1} \right)$
So, option (4) is correct
subject: Physics

explanation:

$\therefore \quad \dfrac{{dV}}{{dt}}=\dfrac{{I}_{{D}}}{{C}}=\dfrac{10^{-3}}{4 \times 10^{-6}}=250 {~V} {~s}^{-1}$

question:
Figure shows a capacitor made of two circular plates each of radius $12\,cm$, separated by $5.0\,mm$. The capacitor is being charged by an external source (not shown in the figure). The charging current is constant and equal to $0.15\,A$, then which of the following is correct?
(Given: ${\varepsilon_{0}=8.85 \times 10^{-12} {C}^{2} N^{-1} {~m}^{-2}}$ )
explanation:
Area, ${A=\pi R^{2}=3.14 \times(0.12)^{2} m^{2}}$
${C=\dfrac{\varepsilon_{0} A}{d}=\dfrac{8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times 3.14 \times(0.12)^{2}}{5 \times 10^{-3}}}$
${=80.1 \times 10^{-12} F=80.1 p F}$
${I=C \dfrac{d V}{d t} \quad\left(\because I=\dfrac{d q}{d t}\right)}$
${\Rightarrow \dfrac{d V}{d t}=\dfrac{I}{C}=\dfrac{0.15}{80.1 \times 10^{-12}}}$
${=1.87 \times 10^{9} {~V} {~s}^{-1}}$
Displacement current is equal to conduction current $i.e.,{\rm{ }}0.15{\rm{ }}A$.

options.text: The rate of change of potential difference between the plates is ${1.87 \times 10^{9} {Vs}^{-1}}$
The displacement current across the plates is $0.30\,A$.
The capacitance of the capacitor is $75\,pF$.

subject: Physics

question: A parallel plate capacitor with plate area ${A}$ and seperation ${d}$ is charged by a constant current $i$. Consider a plane surface of area ${A / 4}$, parallel to the plates, and drawn symetrically between the plates. What is the displacement current through this area?
explanation:
Electric field between the plates of the capacitor is given by
${E=\dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\dfrac{q}{A \varepsilon_{0}}}$
Flux through the area,
${\phi=\dfrac{q}{A \varepsilon_{0}} \times \dfrac{A}{4}=\dfrac{q}{4 \varepsilon_{0}}}$
Displacement current, ${i_{d}=\varepsilon_{0} \dfrac{d \phi_{E}}{d t}}$
${=\varepsilon_{0} \times \dfrac{d}{d t}\left(\dfrac{q}{4 \varepsilon_{0}}\right)=\dfrac{i}{4}}$
subject: Physics

question:

question: A parallel-plate capacitor with plate area $A$ and separation between the plates $d$, is charged by a constant current $i$. Consider a plane surface of area $A/2$ parallel to the plates and drawn symmetrically between the plates. Find the displacement current through this area.
explanation:
Suppose the charge on the capacitor at time $$t$$ is $$Q$$. The electric field between the plates of the capacitor is $$E=\dfrac{Q}{\varepsilon_{0} A}$$.
The flux through the area considered is
$$\Phi_{E}=\dfrac{Q}{\varepsilon_{0} A} \cdot \dfrac{A}{2}=\dfrac{Q}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}$$
The displacement current is
$$i_{d}=\varepsilon_{0} \dfrac{d \Phi_{E}}{d t}=\varepsilon_{0}\left(\dfrac{1}{2 \varepsilon_{0}}\right) \dfrac{d Q}{d t}=\dfrac{i}{2}$$
subject: Physics

question: A parallel plate capacitor of plate separation $2\,mm$ is connected in an electric circuit having source voltage $400\,V$. If the plate area is $60\,c{m^2}$, then the value of displacement current for ${10^{ - 6}}\sec $ will be $\left[ {In\,{{10}^{ - 6}}\,s} \right.$ voltage across capacitor becomes 400 volt]
explanation:
$$I_{d}=\varepsilon_{0} \dfrac{d \phi_{E}}{d t}=\varepsilon_{0} \dfrac{E \mathrm{~A}}{t}=\dfrac{\varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{~A}}{t} \dfrac{V}{\mathrm{~d}}$$
${I_d} = \frac{{8.85 \times {{10}^{ - 12}} \times 400 \times 60 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}$
$ = 1.062 \times {10^{ - 2}}amp$
subject: Physics

question:
The Maxwell's four equations are written as
(i) $$\oint_{s} \bar{E} \cdot d \bar{s}=\dfrac{q}{\varepsilon_{o}}$$
(ii) $$\oint_{S} B . d s=0$$
(iii) $$\oint_{S} E \cdot d l=\dfrac{d}{d t} \int_{S} B \cdot d s$$
(iv) $$\int \bar{B} \cdot d \bar{l}=\mu_{0} I+\mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0} \dfrac{d}{d t} \int \bar{E} \cdot \mathrm{d} \bar{s}$$
The correct equations are
explanation: Equation (i) is Gauss's law and equation (iv) is Ampere's law. Here, two equations are Maxwell's equations. For equations (ii) and (iii) vector forms are missing so they are not correct.
subject: Physics

question: The displacement current flows in the dielectric of a capacitor when the potential difference across its plate
explanation:
$$\mathrm{i}_{d}=\varepsilon_{0} \dfrac{\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{dt}}=\varepsilon_{0} \dfrac{\operatorname{Ad} E}{\mathrm{dt}}$$
$$\Rightarrow \mathrm{i}_{d} \neq 0 \text { whenever } \dfrac{\mathrm{d} E}{\mathrm{dt}} \neq 0$$

options.text: Displacement current does not depend on potential difference

subject: Physics

question: To establish an instantaneous displacement current of $2\,A$ in the space between two parallel plate of $$1 \mu F$$ capacitor, the potential difference across the capacitor plates will have to be changed at the rate of
explanation:
${I_d} = {\varepsilon _0}\frac{{d{\phi _E}}}{{dt}} = {\varepsilon _0}\frac{d}{{dt}}(EA) = {\varepsilon _0}\,A\frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {\frac{V}{d}} \right)$
or $\quad {I_d} = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}\,A}}{{\,d}}\frac{{dV}}{{dt}} = C\frac{{dV}}{{dt}}$
$\therefore \frac{{dV}}{{dt}} = \frac{{{I_d}}}{C} = \frac{2}{{{{10}^{ - 6}}}} = 2 \times {10^6}\,V/s$
subject: Physics

question: The diameter of the condenser plate is $4\,cm$. It is charged by an external current of $0.2\,A$ . The maximum magnetic field induced in the gap
explanation: $$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} i}{2 \pi r}=2 \mu T(\mathrm{r}=$$ radius of the plate $$)$$
subject: Physics
