question:
Displacement current goes through the gap between the plates of a capacitor when the charge of the capacitor does not change.
The displacement current arises in the region in which the electric field is constant with time.
explanation: Displacement current goes through the gap between the plates when there is a change in charge on the plates, which causes a change in electric field between the plates. So option (4) is correct.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Physics

question: A parallel plate capacitor consists of two circular plates of radius $0.1\,m$. They are separated by a distance $0.5\,mm$. If electric field between the capacitor plates changes as $\frac{{dE}}{{dt}} = 5 \times {10^{13}}\frac{V}{{m \times s}}$ find displacement current between the plates.
explanation:
Area of plates $A = \pi {r^2} = 3.14 \times {(0.1)^2}\,{m^2}$
$\frac{{dE}}{{dt}} = 5 \times {10^3}\frac{V}{{m \times s}}$
${1_d} = {\varepsilon _0}\,A\left[ {\frac{{dE}}{{dt}}} \right]\quad \left\{ {\because \frac{{d{\phi _E}}}{{dt}} = A\frac{{dE}}{{dt}}} \right\}$
$ = 8.85 \times {10^{ - 12}}\frac{{{C^2}}}{{N{m^2}}} \times 3.14 \times {(0.1)^2}\,{m^2} \times 5 \times {10^{13}}\frac{V}{{m \times s}}$
${1_d} = 13.9\,A$
subject: Physics

question: An electric field of $300\,\,V/m$ is confined to a circular area $10\,cm$ in diameter. If the field is increasing at the rate of $20\,\,V/m - s$, the magnitude of magnetic field at a point $15\,cm$ from the center of the circle will be-
explanation:
$B = \frac{{{\mu _0}{\varepsilon _0}}}{{2\pi R}}\left( {\frac{{\pi {d^2}}}{4}} \right)\frac{{dE}}{{dt}}$
$ = \frac{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 7}} \times 8.85 \times {{10}^{ - 12}} \times 3.14 \times 0.01 \times 20}}{{4 \times 0.15}}$
$ = 1.85 \times {10^{ - 18}}\,T$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Magnetic Field between Plates of a Capacitor

question: A source at rest emits sound waves of frequency $102\,Hz$. Two observers are moving away from the source of sound in opposite directions each with a speed of $10 \%$ of the speed of sound. The ratio of the frequencies of sound heard by the observers is
explanation:
$v_{0_{1}}=v_{0_{2}}=10 \%$ of $v=\dfrac{v}{10}$
$f = 102\,Hz$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958707/preptracker/questions/q_109315_explanation_1764958707451.png For observer 1
$f_{1}=\left(\dfrac{v-v_{01}}{v}\right) f=\left(\dfrac{v-\dfrac{v}{10}}{v}\right) f=\dfrac{9}{10} f$
For observer 2,
$f_{2}=\left(\dfrac{v-v_{02}}{v}\right) f=\dfrac{9}{10} f$
$\therefore \dfrac{f_{1}}{f_{2}}=\dfrac{9 f / 10}{9 f / 10}=1: 1$
subject: Physics

question: A car travelling at a speed of $54\,kmph$ towards an wall sounds horn of frequency $400\,Hz$. The difference in the frequencies of two sounds, one received directly from the car and other reflected from the wall noticed by a person standing between the car and the wall is (speed of sound in air is $335\,m{s^{ - 1}}$)
explanation:
The observer will hear two sounds, one directly from source and other reflected waves of sound. Therefore difference in frequency of two sounds
$=\left(\dfrac{v}{v-v_{s}}\right) n-\left(\dfrac{v}{v-v_{s}}\right) n=0$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Doppler Effect in Case of Reflection of Sound Waves from a Wall /Moving Object

question: A boy standing on a platform observes the frequency of a train horn as it passes by. The change in the frequency noticed as the train approaches and recedes him with a velocity of $108\,kmph$ is (speed of sound in air $ = 330\,m{s^{ - 1}}$)
explanation:
$v=$ velocity of train $ = 108 \times \frac{5}{{18}}\,m/s$
$ = 30\,m/s$
$c=$ speed of sound $ = 330\,m/s$
$\therefore \Delta f=\dfrac{f_{0}}{\left(1-\dfrac{v}{c}\right)}-\dfrac{f_{o}}{\left(1+\dfrac{v}{c}\right)}=\dfrac{2 f_{o} \cdot v / c}{\left(1-\dfrac{v^{2}}{c^{2}}\right)}$
$\therefore$ Fractional change $=\dfrac{\Delta f}{f_{0} /\left(1-\dfrac{v}{c}\right)}$
$=\dfrac{2(v / c)}{(1+v / c)}$
$=\dfrac{2 \times(30 / 330)}{\left(1+\dfrac{30}{330}\right)}$
$\therefore$ Percentage change $=\dfrac{60 \times 100}{360} \%=16.67 \%$
subject: Physics

question: An observer moves towards a stationary source of sound with a speed $\dfrac{1}{5}$ $th$ of sound wave in air. The frequency of the sound emitted by the source is $f$. The apparent frequency recorded by the observer is
explanation:
When an observer moves toward a source which is stationary, then apparent frequency heard by observer increases.
As, $f^{\prime}=\left(\dfrac{v+v_{0}}{v-v_{s}}\right) f$
$v_{s}=0, f^{\prime}=\left(\dfrac{v+v_{0}}{v}\right) f$
Put value of $v_{0}=\dfrac{v}{5}$ (given)
$f^{\prime}=\left(\dfrac{v+v / 5}{v}\right) f$
$f^{\prime}=\dfrac{6}{5} f=1.2 f$
$f^{\prime}=f_{\text {(apparent) }}=1.2 f$
subject: Physics

question: An observer moves towards a stationary source of sound, with a speed of one fifth of the speed of sound. The apparent increase in the frequency heard by the observer is
explanation:
Apparent frequency is given by
$f=f_{0}\left(\dfrac{v_{s}+v_{0}}{v_{s}}\right)=f_{0}\left(\dfrac{v+\dfrac{v}{S}}{5}\right)=\dfrac{6}{5} f_{0}$
Hence, percentage increase in frequency
$=\dfrac{\left(\dfrac{6}{5} f_{0}-f_{0}\right)}{f_{0}} \times 100 \%=20 \%$
subject: Physics

question: A source and an observer move away from each other with same velocity of $10 {~ms}^{-1}$ with respect to the ground. If the observer finds the frequency of sound coming from the source as $1980\,Hz$, then the actual frequency of the source is (speed of sound in air $=340 {~ms}^{-1}$ )
explanation:
According to Doppler's effect formula
$f^{\prime}=f\left(\dfrac{v-v_{0}}{v-v_{s}}\right)$
$1980=f\left(\dfrac{340-10}{340-10}\right)$
$f=\dfrac{1980 \times 350}{330} \Rightarrow f=2100 {~Hz}$
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics

question: A source emitting sound is tied to one end of a string of length $50\,cm$ and is rotated with an angular speed of $40 {rads}^{-1}$ in the horizontal plane. The ratio of the maximum and minimum frequencies of the sound heard by an observer standing at a distance of $10\,m$ from the fixed end of the string is (speed of sound in air $=340 {~ms}^{-1}$)
explanation:
Now maximum frequency is given
${f_{{\rm{max }}}} = {f_{{\rm{standard }}}} \times \left[ {\frac{v}{{{v_s} - v}}} \right]\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
$v=$ speed of source
$v=r w$
$v = \left( {\frac{{50}}{{100}}} \right)\left( {40} \right)$
$v=20 {~m} / {s}$
$v_{s}=$ speed of sound
${f_{{\rm{min }}}} = {f_{{\rm{standard }}}} \times \left[ {\frac{v}{{{v_s} + v}}} \right]\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From Eqs. (1) and (2)
$\dfrac{f_{\text {max }}}{f_{\text {min }}}=\dfrac{v_{s}+v}{v_{s}-v}=\dfrac{340+20}{340-20}=\dfrac{360}{320}=\dfrac{9}{8}$
Hence, the ratio of maximum to minimum frequency is $9: 8$
So, the correct option is (4)
subject: Physics

question: An engine sounding a whistle of frequency $2000\,Hz$ is receding from the stationary observer at $72 {~km} / {h}$. What is the apparent frequency of the observer? The velocity of sound in air is $340 {~m} / {s}$.
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958703/preptracker/questions/q_109230_explanation_1764958703284.png Apparent frequency at observer,
$f^{\prime}=f\left(\dfrac{v}{v+v_{s}}\right)$
$\therefore \quad f^{\prime}=2000\left(\dfrac{340}{340+20}\right)$
Hence, $\quad f^{\prime} \approx 1889 {~Hz}$
subject: Physics

question: A bus moving with a uniform speed of $72 {~km} / {h}$ towards a building blows a horn of frequency $1.7\,kHz$. If the speed of sound in air is $340 {~m} / {s}$, what will be the frequency of echo heard by bus driver?
explanation:
Observed frequency of sound reflected from building is given by
${f^\prime } = f\left( {\frac{{v + {v_b}}}{{v - {v_b}}}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
$f=$ original frequency produced by horn.
$=1.7 {kHz}$.
Hence, putting these value in Eq. (1), we get Observed frequency by bus driver,
$f^{\prime}=1.7\left(\dfrac{340+20}{340-20}\right)=1.9125 {kHz} \approx 1.8 {kHz}$
subject: Physics

question: Two trucks heading in opposite directions each with speed $0.1 u$, approach each other. The speed of the sound is $u$. The driver of first truck sounds his horn of frequency $495\,Hz$. Let $v_{1}$ and $v_{2}$ are the frequencies heard by the driver of second truck, when the trucks approach each other and when the trucks have passed each other. The magnitude of $v_{1}-v_{2}$ is
explanation:
Velocity of sound, $v_{s}=u$
Velocity of trucks, $v_{1}=v_{2}=0.1 u$
In the first case, when two trucks are approaching each other:
The frequency heard by second truck driver,
$v_{1}=\left(\dfrac{v_{s}+v_{2}}{v_{s}-v_{1}}\right) v=\left(\dfrac{u+0.1 u}{u-0.1 u}\right) 495$
$=\dfrac{1.1}{0.9} \times 495=605 {~Hz}$
In the second case, when two trucks are receding each other:
The frequency heard by second truck driver,
$v_{2}=\left(\dfrac{v_{s}-v_{2}}{v_{s}+v_{1}}\right) v=\left(\dfrac{u-0.1 u}{u+01 u}\right) 495$
$=\dfrac{0.9}{1.1} \times 495=405 {~Hz}$
$v_{1}-v_{2}=605-405=200 {~Hz}$
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics

question: A highway truck has two horns $A$ and $B$. When sounded together, the driver records 50 beats in 10 seconds. With horn blowing and the truck moving towards a wall at a speed of $10 {~m} / {s}$, the driver noticed a beat frequency of $5\,Hz$ with the echo. When frequency of $A$ is decreased, the beat frequency with two horns sounded together increases. Calculate the frequency of horn $A$. (Speed of sound in air = $330 {~m} / {s}$ )
explanation:
As given, $n_{A}-n_{B}=\dfrac{50}{10} \Rightarrow n_{A}-n_{B}=5$ beats
When, the horn $B$ is blown while moving towards the wall the apparent frequency,
$n_{B}^{\prime}=n_{B} \dfrac{v+v_{0}}{v-v_{s}}$
Since, both the observer and source are moving with truck.
So, $\quad v_{s}=v_{0}=10 {~m} / {s}$
$n_{B}^{\prime}=n_{B}\left(\dfrac{330+10}{330-10}\right)$
$n_{B}^{\prime}=n_{B} 1.0625 \Rightarrow n_{B}^{\prime}-n_{B}=0.0625 n_{B}$
As given, $n_{B}^{\prime}-n_{B}=5$
$0.0625 n_{B}=5 \Rightarrow n_{B}=80 {~Hz}$
$\because$ Given, that if frequency of $A$ is decreased then beat frequency increases.
So, $n_{B}>n_{A}$
$\therefore n_{A}=n_{B}-5=80-5 \Rightarrow n_{A}=75 {~Hz}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Doppler Effect in Case of Reflection of Sound Waves from a Wall /Moving Object

question: A musician on a moving vehicle plays a tone at $880\,Hz$ note. When the vehicle was approaching a listener, he receives it as $888\,Hz$ tone. The speed of the vehicle is (Assume the velocity of sound is $333 {~m} / {s}$.)
explanation:
We know that, when sound source is in motion and observer is stationary, frequency heard.
$n=\dfrac{n_{0} v}{v-v_{s}} \Rightarrow v_{s}=v\left[1-\dfrac{n_{0}}{n}\right]$
$\Rightarrow \quad v_{s}=333\left[1-\dfrac{880}{888}\right]=3 {~m} / {s}$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: A drone fitted with siren is flying directly away from the drone operator and towards a distant building at a speed of $15 {~m} / {s}$. The siren produces sound of frequency $780\,Hz$. What is the frequency that the operator hears in the echo reflected from the building. [Speed of sound is $340 {~m} / {s}$.]
explanation:
A drone fitted with siren as shown in the figure, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952598/preptracker/questions/q_109221_explanation_1764952598449.png As source is moving towards the building, so the apparent frequency reflected by the building is
$n=\dfrac{n_{0} v}{v-v_{s}}=\dfrac{780 \times 340}{340-15}=816 {~Hz}$
As we know, there is no change in the apparent frequency by the reflection from the building. Hence, the echo heard by observer is $816\,Hz$. So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: A whistle of frequency $660\,Hz$ moves in a circle of radius $1 m$ at an angular speed of $10 {rad} / {s}$. The highest frequency heard by a listener at a long distance and at rest with respect to the center of the circle is (Let speed of sound $=340 {~m} / {s}$.)
explanation:
Since, we know that the maximum frequency heard by listener, if whistle has tangential velocity towards the listener as shown in figure, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952590/preptracker/questions/q_109218_explanation_1764952590044.png linear velocity, $v=\omega r=10 \times 1=10 {~m} / {s}$
As, given that, there is long distance between whistle and listener $i.e.,$ $\theta \approx 0$.
Now, the apparent frequency,
$f=\dfrac{f_{0} v}{v-v_{s}}=\dfrac{660 \times 340}{340-10}=680 {~Hz}$
Hence, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Doppler Effect in Case of Reflection of Sound Waves from a Wall /Moving Object

question: A source of sound whose frequency is $1000\,Hz$ is moving with a speed of $33 {~m} / {s}$. The waves reflected by a fixed obstacle are registered by a receiver that moves together with the source. If the speed of the sound wave is $330 {~m} / {s}$ in air, then the frequency registered by the receiver is
explanation:
Frequency of reflected sound,
$f_{1}=f\left(\dfrac{v}{v-v_{1}}\right)$
$=1000\left(\dfrac{330}{330-33}\right)=\dfrac{1000 \times 330}{297}$
Frequency observed by observer,
$f_{2}=f_{1}\left(\dfrac{v+v_{D}}{v}\right)=\dfrac{1000 \times 330}{297}\left(\dfrac{330+33}{330}\right)$
$=\dfrac{1000 \times 363}{297}=1.2 \times 1000=1.2 {kHz}$
subject: Physics

question: A source of sound having natural frequency $f_{0}=1800 {~Hz}$ is moving uniformly along a line separated from a stationary listener by a distance $l=250 {~m}$. The velocity of source is $n=0.80$ times the velocity of sound. Find the frequency of sound  received by the listener at the moment when the source gets closest to him.
explanation:
As sound waves take finite time in reaching from one position to other position. The sound waves emitted by the source when it is closest to the listener (at $O$ ) does not reach instantaneously. The frequency heard at this instant should be emitted from some previous position of source. Let this sound (or disturbance) was created at position ' $S$ '. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950357/preptracker/questions/q_42233_explanation_1764950356678.png The direction of velocity vector of source makes an angle $\theta$ with the line joining the disturbance position (source position) and observer position as shown in the figure. The time in which sound reaches the listener along $S L$ the source reaches point $O$, closest to the listener. From figure,
$\cos \theta = \frac{{SO}}{{SL}} = \frac{{{v_s}t}}{v} = \frac{{{v_s}}}{v} = n\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Now using the formula for apparent frequency received by observer
$f_{\text {app }}=f_{\text {original }}\left(\dfrac{v-v_{0}}{v-v_{s}}\right)$
As Doppler effect is applicable only along the line joining disturbance position (source position) and observer position,hence we should substitute the component of velocity of the source along this line ' $S L$ '.
Hence the frequency heard by the listener is$\begin{array}{l} {f_{{\rm{app }}}} = {f_o}\left( {\frac{{v - 0}}{{v - {v_s}\cos \theta }}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)\\ \Rightarrow {f_{{\rm{app}}}} = {f_o}\left( {\frac{v}{{v - (nv)n}}} \right) = \frac{{{f_s}}}{{1 - {n^2}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,(3) \end{array}$
On substituting numerical values, we get $f_{\text {app }}=5000 {~Hz}$
subject: Physics

question: Two sound sources are moving away from a stationary observer in opposite directions with velocities ${V_{1}}$ and ${V_{2}\left(V_{1}>V_{2}\right)}$. The frequency of both the sources is ${900 {~Hz} . V_{1}}$ and ${V_{2}}$ are both quite less than speed of sound, ${V=300 {~m} / {sec}}$. Find the value of ${\left(V_{1}-V_{2}\right)}$ so that beat frequency observed by observer is $6\,Hz$.
subject: Physics

question:
A source s emitting sound of $400Hz$ is fixed on block $A$ which is attached to free end of a spring ${S_A}$. The detector $D$ fixed on block $B$ attached to the free end of spring ${S_B}$ detects this sound. The blocks $A$ and $B$ are simultaneously displaced towards each other through a distance of $1.0\,m$ and then left to vibrate. If vibrational frequency of each block is $3Hz$, the maximum frequencies of sound detected by $D$ is
$({V_{{\rm{sound}}}} = 340\,m{\rm{/}}s)$
explanation:
$\omega = 2\pi \nu = 2\pi 3 = 6\pi $
$\therefore \omega=18.85 \,{rads}^{-1}$
Maximum speed of motion of $\left|v_{A}\right|$ and $\left|v_{B}\right|=A \omega$
$f_{\text {max }}=\left(\dfrac{v+v_{B}}{v-v_{A}}\right) f
=\left(\dfrac{340+A \omega}{340-A \omega}\right) f$
$=\left(\dfrac{340+18.85}{340-18.85}\right) 400$
$=\left(\dfrac{358.85}{321.15}\right) \times 400$
$=1.12 \times 400$
$\therefore {f}_{\text {max }}=448 {~Hz}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Doppler Effect in Case of Reflection of Sound Waves from a Wall /Moving Object

question:
A stationary source emitting sound at a fixed frequency $f_{0}$, which is reflected by two cars approaching the source. The difference between the frequencies of sound reflected from the cars is $1.5 \%$ of ${f_0}$The difference in the speeds of cars is
[Note: The cars are moving at constant speeds much smaller than the speed of sound which is $330\,m{s^{ - 1}}$]
explanation:
${f_{app{\text{ }}}} = {f_0}\frac{{c + v}}{{c - v}}$
[Here, $c$: speed of sound, $v$ : speed of car]
$ \Rightarrow df = \frac{{2{f_0}c}}{{{{(c - v)}^2}}}dv \approx \frac{{2{f_0}}}{c}dv\,\,(\because v < < c)\,\,\,(1)$
It is given, $df = \frac{{1.5}}{{100}}{f_0}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
From (1) and (2)
$\frac{{1.5{f_0}}}{{100}} = \frac{{2{f_0}}}{c}dv$
$ \Rightarrow dv = \frac{{1.5 \times 330}}{{100 \times 2}} = 2.48\,m/s$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Doppler Effect in Case of Reflection of Sound Waves from a Wall /Moving Object

question: A source of sound of frequency $450{\text{ cycle}}/sec$ is moving towards a stationary observer with ${34 {~m} / {s}}$ speed. If the speed of sound is ${340 {~m} / {s}}$ then the apparent frequency will be
explanation:
Apparent frequency ${v^{\prime}=v_{S}\left(\dfrac{v+v_{O}}{v-v_{S}}\right)}$
Hence ${v_{S}=450 {~s}^{-1}, v_{S}=34 {~m} {~s}^{-1}}$
${v=340 {~m} {~s}^{-1}, v^{\prime}=}$ ?
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {v^\prime } = \frac{{340 \times 450}}{{(340 - 34)}} = \frac{{450 \times 340}}{{306}} = 500cps$.
So correct option is (3)
subject: Physics

question: A sound source is moving towards stationary listener with ${1 / 10^{\text {th }}}$ of the speed of sound The ratio of apparent to real frequency is
explanation:
Apparent frequency ${v^{\prime}=v\left(\dfrac{v+v_{0}}{v-v_{S}}\right)}$
Where ${v_{0}=0, v_{S}=33 {~m} {~s}^{-1}, v=330 {~m} {~s}^{-1}}$
${\therefore \quad v^{\prime}=v\left(\dfrac{330+0}{330-33}\right)=v\left(\dfrac{330}{297}\right)}$
${\Rightarrow \dfrac{v^{\prime}}{v}=\dfrac{330}{297}=\dfrac{10}{9}}$.
So correct option is (4)
subject: Physics

question: A source of sound of frequency $256\,Hz$ is moving rapidly towards a wall with a velocity of $5\,m{\rm{/}}s$. How many beats per second will be heard if sound travels at a speed of $330\,m{\rm{/}}s$ by an observer behind the source.
explanation:
When the source $S$ is between the wall ($W$) and the observer ($O$).
For direct sound the source is moving away from the observer, therefore the apparent frequency
${n^{\prime \prime }} = \frac{v}{{v + {v_s}}}n = \frac{{330}}{{330 + 5}} \times 256$
and frequency of reflected sound
${n^\prime } = \frac{v}{{v - {v_s}}}n = \frac{{330}}{{330 - 3}} \times 256 = 259.9$
Number of beats/ ${{\rm{sec}} = {{\rm{n}}^\prime } - {{\rm{n}}^{\prime \prime }} = 259 - 252.2}$
${ = 7.7}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Doppler Effect in Case of Reflection of Sound Waves from a Wall /Moving Object

question: A vehicle with horn of frequency $v$ is moving with a velocity of $30\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ in a direction perpendicular to the line joining the observer and the vehicle. The observer perceives the sound to have a frequency $\left(v+v_{1}\right)$. If the sound velocity in air is $300\,m{s^{ - 1}},$  then
explanation:
Actual frequency of horn $=v$
Apparent frequency perceived by observer, $v=v+v_{1}$
Velocity of vehicle, ${v_s} = 30\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
Velocity of sound in air, $v = 300\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
Doppler's effect of sound is valid only if source and observer are both in relative motion. As the source is moving in a direction perpendicular to the straight line which joins the observer, hence there is no relative motion between them.
Therefore, Doppler's effect will not occur, thus, $v_{1}=0$
subject: Physics

question: A car is moving towards a high cliff. The car driver sounds a horn of frequency $f$. The reflected sound heard by the driver has a frequency $2 f$. If $v$ be the velocity of sound, then the velocity of the car in the same velocity units, will be
explanation:
When the sound is reflected from the cliff, it approaches the driver of the car. Therefore, the driver acts as an observer and both the source (car) and observer are moving.Hence, apparent frequency heard by the observer (driver) is given by
$\begin{equation*}f^{\prime}=f\left(\dfrac{v+v_{o}}{v-v_{s}}\right) \tag{1}\end{equation*}$
where, $v_{s}=$ velocity of sound
and $\quad \nu_{o}=$ velocity of car
Thus, Eq. (1) becomes
$2f = f\left( {\frac{{v + {v_o}}}{{v - {v_o}}}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,2v - 2{v_o} = v + {v_o}$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,3{v_o} = v$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,{v_o} = \frac{v}{3}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Doppler Effect in Case of Reflection of Sound Waves from a Wall /Moving Object

question:
A sounding source of frequency $500\,Hz$ moves towards a stationary observer with a velocity $30\,m{s^{ - 1}}.$ If the velocity of sound in air is $330\,m{s^{ - 1}},$ then find the frequency heard by the observer.
None of these
subject: Physics

question: Two cars are moving on two perpendicular roads towards a crossing with uniform speeds of $72\,km\,{h^{ - 1}}$ and $36\,km\,{h^{ - 1}}.$ If first car blows horn of frequency $280\,Hz,$ then the frequency of horn heard by the driver of second car when line joining the car makes angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the roads, will be
explanation:
The component of velocity of source along line joining,
$\begin{aligned}v_{s} & =v_{1} \cos 45^{\circ} \\& =36 \times \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} k m h^{-1}=5 \sqrt{2} m s^{-1}\end{aligned}$
Component of velocity of observer (second car) along line joining
${v_o} = {v_2}\cos 45^\circ = 72 \times \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}km\,{h^{ - 1}}$
$ = 10\sqrt 2 \,m{s^{ - 1}}$
$n' = \frac{{v + {v_o}}}{{v - {v_s}}}n = \frac{{330 + 10\sqrt 2 }}{{330 - 5\sqrt 2 }} \times 280$
$ = \frac{{344}}{{323}} \times 280 = 298\,Hz$
subject: Physics

question: Two tuning forks $A$ and $B$ having a frequency of  $500\,Hz$  each are placed with $B$ to the right of $A$. An observer is between the forks and is moving towards $B$ with a speed of $25\,m{s^{ - 1}}.$ The speed of sound is $345\,m{s^{ - 1}}.$ and the wind speed is $5\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ from $A$ to $B$. Calculate the difference in the two frequencies heard by the observer.
explanation:
Let the fork at $A$ be source $S_{1}$ and the fork at $B$ be source $S_{2}$.
For the sound from source $S_{1}$,
Apparent frequency,
$\begin{equation*}f_{1}=f\left(\dfrac{c-v_{o}}{c-v_{s}}\right) \tag{1}\end{equation*}$
where, $c = 345 + 5 = 350\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ as wind and sound are in same direction.
${v_o} = + 25\,m{s^{ - 1}},{v_s} = 0$
Put these values in Eq. (1), we get
$\Rightarrow f_{1}=500\left(\dfrac{350-25}{350-0}\right)$
$ = 464.3\,Hz$
For the sound from source $S_{2}$,
$c = 345 - 5 = 340\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ as wind is opposite to sound ${v_s} = 0\,m{s^{ - 1}},$
${v_o} = - 25\,m{s^{ - 1}},$ then
$f_{2}=f\left(\dfrac{c-v_{o}}{c-v_{s}}\right)=500\left(\dfrac{c-(-25)}{c-0}\right)$
$ = \frac{{500 \times 365}}{{340}} = 536.80\,Hz$
$ \Rightarrow {f_2} - {f_1} = (536.80 - 464.30) = 72.5\,Hz$
As the difference is very large, the observer will not hear the beats.
subject: Physics

question: A source of sound of sound is approaching an observer with speed of $30\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ and the observer is approaching the source with a speed of $60\,m{s^{ - 1}}.$ Then, the dfractional change in the frequency of sound is (take, speed of sound in air $ = 330\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ )
explanation:
Given, ${v_s} = 30\,m{s^{ - 1}},{v_o} = 60\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
Apparent frequency,
$\begin{aligned}v^{\prime} & =v\left[\dfrac{v+v_{o}}{v-v_{s}}\right]=v\left[\dfrac{330+60}{330-30}\right] \\v^{\prime} & =v\left[\dfrac{390}{300}\right] \\\Rightarrow \quad v^{\prime} & =\dfrac{39}{30} v\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Fractional change in frequency
$=\dfrac{\dfrac{39}{30} v-v}{v}=\dfrac{9}{30}=\dfrac{3}{10}$
subject: Physics

question: A band playing music at a frequency $f$ is moving towards a wall at a speed $v_{b}$. A motorist is following the band with a speed $v_{m}$. If $v$ be the speed of the sound, the expression for beat frequency heard by motorist is
explanation:
According to Doppler's effect, When observer is moving behind the source (band), apparent frequency heard
$n^{\prime}=n\left[\dfrac{v+v_{o}}{v+v_{s}}\right]$
Here, $v_{o}=v_{m} \Rightarrow v_{s}=v_{b}$
$\therefore \quad n_{1}=\left[\dfrac{v+v_{m}}{v+v_{b}}\right] f$
The other sound is the echo reaching the observer from the wall and can be regarded as coming from the image of source formed by reflection at the wall.This image is approaching the observer in the direction of sound.Hence, for reflected sound, frequency heard by motorist
$\begin{aligned}n_{2} & =n\left[\dfrac{v+v_{o}}{v+v_{s}}\right] \\\Rightarrow \quad n_{2} & =f\left[\dfrac{v+v_{m}}{v+v_{b}}\right]\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Doppler Effect in Case of Reflection of Sound Waves from a Wall /Moving Object

question: A train is moving at $30\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ in still air. The frequency of the locomotive whistle is $500\,Hz$ and the speed of sound is $345\,m{s^{ - 1}}.$ The apparent wavelength of sound in front of and behind the locomotive are respectively
explanation:
Apparent wavelength in front of locomotive,
$\lambda^{\prime}=\dfrac{\lambda\left(v-v_{s}\right)}{v}$
or $\lambda^{\prime}=\dfrac{v-v_{s}}{v}=\dfrac{345-30}{500}\left(\because v=\dfrac{v}{\lambda}\right)$
$=\dfrac{315}{500}=0.63 m$
Behind locomotive,
$\lambda '' = \frac{{\lambda \left( {v + {v_s}} \right)}}{v} = \frac{{v + {v_s}}}{v}$
$ = \frac{{345 + 30}}{{500}} = \frac{{375}}{{500}} = 0.75\,m$
subject: Physics

question: A motor-cyclist moving towards a huge cliff with a speed of ${18 {kmh}^{-1}}$, blows a horn of source frequency 325 Hz . If the speed of the sound in air is ${330 {~ms}^{-1}}$, the number of beats heard by him is
explanation:
Using Doppler's effect, the apparent frequency is
${f_{a}=\left(\dfrac{V+V_{0}}{V-V_{S}}\right) f_{0}=\left(\dfrac{330+5}{330-5}\right) 325}$
${f_{a}=335 {~Hz}}$
Number of Beats, ${\Delta f=f_{a}-f_{0}}$
$=335-325=10 \text {. }$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Doppler Effect in Case of Reflection of Sound Waves from a Wall /Moving Object

question: A person observes two moving trains, ' $A$ ' reaching the station and ' $B$ ' leaving the station with equal speed of $30\,m/s$. If both trains emit sounds with frequency $300\,Hz$, (Speed of sound: $330\,m/s$) approximate difference of frequencies heard by the person will be:
explanation:
Train $A$ reaching the station and train $B$ leaving the station with equal speed of $30\,m/s$.
Frequency of $\operatorname{train} A$ heard by the observer
$f_{A}=f_{0}\left[\dfrac{v_{S}+v_{A}}{v_{S}}\right]$
${f_A} = 300\left[ {\frac{{330 + 30}}{{330}}} \right] = 327.27\,Hz$
Frequency of $\operatorname{train} B$ heard by the observer
$f_{B}=f_{0}\left[\dfrac{v_{S}-v_{B}}{v_{S}}\right] ; f_{B}=300\left[\dfrac{330-30}{330}\right]$
$f_{B}=272.73 H z$
So, beats $=f_{A}-f_{B}=(327.27-272.73) H z$
or $\Delta f \approx 55\,Hz$
subject: Physics

question: The engine of a train moving with speed $10\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ towards a platform sounds a whistle at frequency $400\,Hz$. The frequency heard by a passenger inside the train is (Neglect air speed. Speed of sound in air is $330\,m{s^{ - 1}}$)
explanation: $400\,Hz$, as passenger is inside the train relative velocity between the source and the passenger B zero. So, passenger will hear the sound of same frequency as generated by train.
subject: Physics

question: A car $P$ travelling at $20\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ sounds its horn at a frequency of $400\,Hz$. Another car $Q$ is travelling behind the first car in the same direction with a velocity $40\,m{s^{ - 1}}$. The frequency heard by the passenger of the car $Q$ is approximately [Take, velocity of sound $ = 360\,m{s^{ - 1}}$]
explanation:
Velocity of car $P\left( {{v_s}} \right) = 20\,m/s$
Frequency of emitted sound $(v) = 400\,Hz$
Velocity of car $Q\left( {{v_0}} \right) = 360\,m/s$
From Doppler's effect $v^{\prime}=v\left(\dfrac{v \pm v_{0}}{v \pm v_{s}}\right)$
Since source and observer both are moving in the same direction,
$v^{\prime}=400\left(\dfrac{360+40}{360+20}\right) H z$.
${v^\prime } = 400\left( {\frac{{400}}{{380}}} \right) = 421.05\,Hz$
${v^\prime } = 421\,Hz$
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: When observer moves away from the source the frequency of sound appears to decrease.

Reason: The apparent frequency of sound depends on, whether the observer is moving towards source or away from source.
explanation: The apparent frequency ( $\left.f^{\prime}\right)$ is determined by the observer's velocity $\left(v_{0}\right)$ and the source's velocity $\left(v_{s}\right)$ relative to the speed of sound $(v)$. When the observer moves away (positive $v_{0}$ ), the apparent frequency decreases, and when moving towards (negative $v_{0}$ ), the apparent frequency increases. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question: A police car moving at $22\,m{\rm{/}}s$ chases a motorcyclist. The police man sounds his horn at $176\,Hz$, while both of them move towards a stationary siren of frequency $165\,Hz$. Calculate the speed of the motorcycle. If it is given that the motorcyclist does not observe any beats: (Given velocity of sound $330\,m{\rm{/}}s$ )
explanation:
As no beats are observed $f_{1}=f_{2}$
$176\left(\dfrac{330-v_{0}}{330-22}\right)=165\left(\dfrac{330+v_{0}}{330}\right)$
${v_0} = 22\,m{\rm{/}}\sec $
subject: Physics

question: A person $P$ is $600\,m$ away from the station when train is approaching station with $72\,km{\rm{/}}h$, it blows a whistle of frequency $800\,Hz$ when $800\,m$ away from the station. Find the frequency heard by the person. Speed of sound $ = 340\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
explanation: https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956995/preptracker/questions/q_41267_explanation_1764956995066.png  $v_{a p p}=\dfrac{v}{v-v_{s} \cos \theta} v=\dfrac{340}{340-16} \times 800=839.5$
subject: Physics

question: A source of sound frequency $256\,Hz$ is moving towards a wall with a velocity of $5\,m{\rm{/}}s$. Velocity of sound is $330\,m{\rm{/}}s$. The number of beats heard by an observer standing behind the source is nearly
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956207/preptracker/questions/q_41263_explanation_1764956206641.png $n_{D}=256\left(\dfrac{330}{330+5}\right)$
$n_{R}=256\left(\dfrac{330}{330-5}\right)$
$\Delta n=n_{R}-n_{D}=\dfrac{256 \times 330}{325}-\dfrac{256 \times 330}{335}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Doppler Effect in Case of Reflection of Sound Waves from a Wall /Moving Object

question: Velocity of sound is $v$. Source and observer move towards each other with velocities $v_{s}$ and $v_{0}$ respectively. Wind is blowing with a velocity $v_{m}$ in the direction opposite to the propagation of sound; $n$ is the pitch of the sound. The apparent pitch of the sound heard by the observer is
explanation: https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950185/preptracker/questions/q_41259_explanation_1764950185535.png $\therefore n^{\prime}=n\left(\dfrac{\left(v-v_{m}\right)+v_{0}}{\left(v-v_{m}\right)-v_{s}}\right)$.
subject: Physics

question: A stationary sound source $S$ of frequency $334\,Hz$ and a stationary observer $O$ are placed near reflecting surface moving away from the source with velocity $2\,m{\rm{/}}s$ as shown in figure. Velocity of sound waves in air $v = 330\,m{\rm{/}}s$. The apparent frequency of echo is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950183/preptracker/questions/q_41251_explanation_1764950183452.png ${F_{{\rm{echo }}}} = {f_{ac}}\left[ {\frac{{V - {V_0}}}{{V + {V_s}}}} \right] = 334\left[ {\frac{{330 - 2}}{{330 + 2}}} \right]$
$ = 334 \times \frac{{328}}{{332}} = 330\,Hz$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Doppler Effect in Case of Reflection of Sound Waves from a Wall /Moving Object

question: A vehicle, with a horn of frequency $n$ is moving with a velocity of $30\,m{\rm{/}}s$ in a direction perpendicular to the straight line joining the observer and the vehicle. The observer perceives the sound to have a frequency $\left(n+n_{1}\right)$. If the velocity of sound in air is $300\,m{\rm{/}}s$, then
explanation: https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956205/preptracker/questions/q_41248_explanation_1764956205381.png  Since the source is moving at right angles to direction of propagation of sound $n_{1}=0$.
subject: Physics

question: Two trains ' $A$ ' and ' $B$ ' are moving away from one another with a speed of $720\,kmph$. The apparent frequency of the whistle blown by as heard by the passenger of the train ' $B$ ' is (velocity of sound in air is $350\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ ) (given whistle frequency $1100\,Hz$)
subject: Physics

question: A man fired a bullet in front of a mountain and he heard its echo after 2 seconds. After traveling a distance of $85\,m$ towards the mountain, he fired another bullet and heard its echo after 1.5 seconds. The velocity of sound and distance between the mountain and the man when the first bullet was fired are
explanation:
$2 D=v \times 2$
$2[D-85]=v \times \dfrac{3}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{D}{D-85}=\dfrac{2}{3 / 2}=\dfrac{4}{3}$
$\Rightarrow 3 D=4 D-340$
$ \Rightarrow D = 340\,m$
$ \Rightarrow v = 340\,m{s^{ - 1}}$.
subject: Physics

question: A person is in between two vertical mountains. The person fires a gun then hears the first echo from the nearby mountain in a time $3 s$ and hears second echo from far mountain in a time $4 s$. The person hears the third echo after
explanation:
Let $x$ and $y$ be the distances between mountains and person as shown in the figure. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950171/preptracker/questions/q_41224_explanation_1764950171192.png First echo will be heard from nearer hill
$ \Rightarrow {t_1} = \frac{{2x}}{v} = 3\,s$
$2^{\text {nd }}$ echo will be heard from farther hill
$ \Rightarrow {t_2} = \frac{{2y}}{v} = 4\,s$
$\Rightarrow$ Third echo will be heard after $(3 + 4) = 7\,\sec .$
subject: Physics

question:
A person in an open car is approaching a vertical mountain with uniform velocity $90\,kmph$. The person blows horn, then hears its echo after a time of $4\,s$. The distance of the car from the mountain when the horn was blown is
(Velocity of sound in air is $335\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ )
explanation:
Let ' $D$ ' be the distance the car and mountain
$ \Rightarrow D + \left( {D - {v_C}t} \right) = vt$
$ \Rightarrow D = \frac{{\left( {v + {v_C}} \right)t}}{2}$
$ = \frac{{(335 + 25)}}{2} \times 4$
$ = 180 \times 4 = 720\,m$
subject: Physics

question: A person is in front of a wall. The person can hear echo of sound produced by him when minimum distance between person and wall is $16.75\,m$. The velocity of sound in air is
explanation:
$V=\dfrac{2 d}{t}=\dfrac{2 \times 16.75}{0.1}$
$ \Rightarrow V = 335\,m{\rm{/}}s$
subject: Physics

question: Two sources of sound $S_{1}$ and $S_{2}$ are moving towards and away from a stationary observer with the same speed respectively. Observer detects 3 beats per second. Find the value of speed of sources (approximately) (take, ${f_1} = {f_2} = 500\,Hz$ and speed of sound in air $ = 330\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ )
explanation:
Given, frequency of sound produced source $S_{1}$ and $S_{2}$ i.e.,
${f_1} = {f_2} = 500\,Hz$
Speed of sound in air, $v = 330\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
Beats $ = 3\,Hz$
If $v$, be the speed of source, when first source is moving towards the observer and $f_{1}^{\prime}$ be apparent frequency heard by the observer, then
$f_{1}^{\prime}=\dfrac{v}{v-v_{s}} \times f_{1}=\dfrac{330}{330-v_{1}} \times 500$
When second source is moving away from the observer and $f_{2}^{\prime}$ be apparent frequency heard by the observer, then
$f_{2}^{\prime}=\dfrac{v}{v+v_{s}} \times f_{2}=\dfrac{330}{330+v_{s}} \times 500$
$\therefore$ Beats $=f_{1}^{\prime}-f_{2}^{\prime}=3$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{330}{330-v_{s}} \times 500-\dfrac{330}{330+v_{s}} \times 500=3$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{500 \times 2 v_{s} \times 330}{(330)^{2}-v_{s}^{2}}=3$
Since, $v_{s}^{2} < < (330)^{2} \quad$ (we have)
$\dfrac{500 \times 2 v_{s} \times 330}{(330)^{2}}=3$
$ \Rightarrow {v_s} = 0.99 \approx 1\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics

question: The driver of a car travelling with speed $30\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ towards a hill sounds a horn of frequency $600\,Hz$. If the velocity of sound in air is $330\,m{s^{ - 1}}$, the frequency of reflected sound as heard by driver is
explanation:
When the sound is reflected from the cliff, it approaches the driver of the car. Therefore, the driver acts as an observer and both sources (car) and observer are moving.
In this case, the apparent frequency heard by the driver (observer),
$f^{\prime}=f\left(\dfrac{v+v_{0}}{v-v_{0}}\right)$
Given, $f = 600\,Hz,v = 330\,\,m{s^{ - 1}},{v_0} = 30\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
$f^{\prime}=600\left(\dfrac{330+30}{330-30}\right)=720 H z$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Doppler Effect in Case of Reflection of Sound Waves from a Wall /Moving Object

question: A source of sound $S$ is moving with a velocity of $50\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ towards a stationary observer. The observer measures the frequency of the source as $1000\,Hz$. What will be the apparent frequency of the sound has heard by observer when the source is moving away from the observer after crossing him? (the velocity of the sound in the medium is $350\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ ).
explanation:
When the source is coming to the stationary observer,
$n^{\prime}=\left(\dfrac{v}{v-v_{s}}\right) n$
$\Rightarrow 1000=\left(\dfrac{350}{350-50}\right) n$
or $n = \frac{{1000 \times 300}}{{350}}\,Hz$
When the source is moving away from the stationary observer.
$n^{\prime \prime}=\left(\dfrac{v}{v+v_{s}}\right) n$
$ = \left( {\frac{{350}}{{350 + 50}}} \right)\left( {\frac{{1000 \times 300}}{{350}}} \right) = 750\,Hz$
subject: Physics

question: A toy-car, blowing its horn, is moving with a steady speed of $5\,m/s$, away from a wall. An observer, towards whom the toy car is moving, is able to hear 5 beats per second. If the velocity of sound in air is $340\,m/s$, the frequency of the horn of the toy car is close to :
explanation:
From Doppler's effect
$f\left( {{\text{direct}}} \right){\text{ }} = f\left( {\frac{{340}}{{340 - 5}}} \right) = {f_1}$
Frequency received the wall $=\dfrac{340}{340+5}$
Frequency received by the person from the wall is $f($ from wall $)=f\left(\dfrac{340}{340+5}\right)=f_{2}$
Beats $=\left(f_{1}-f_{2}\right)$
$5 = f\left( {\frac{{340}}{{340 - 5}} - \frac{{340}}{{340 + 5}}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} f = 170\,Hz$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Doppler Effect in Case of Reflection of Sound Waves from a Wall /Moving Object

question: Two engines pass each other moving in opposite directions with uniform speed of $30\,m/s$. One of them is blowing a whistle of frequency $540\,Hz$. The apparent frequency of the whistle observed by the person in another engine is (speed of sound is $330\,m/\sec $)
explanation:
We know that the apparent frequency
$f^{\prime}=\left(\dfrac{v+v_{o}}{v-v_{S}}\right) f$ from Doppler's effect
Speed of sound $v = 330\,m/s$
Frequency of whistle $(f) = 540\,Hz$.
$\therefore f^{\prime}=\dfrac{330+30}{330-30} \times 540=648 H z \text {. }$
subject: Physics

question: A source of sound emits sound waves at frequency $f_{0}$. It is moving towards an observer with fixed speed $v_{s}\left(v_{s} < v\right.$, where $v$ is the speed of sound in air). If the observer were to move towards the source with speed $v_{0}$, one of the following two graphs ($A$ and $B$) will given the correct variation of the frequency $f$ heard by the observer as $v_{0}$ is changed.
explanation:
According to Doppler's effect,
Apparent, frequency $f=\left(\dfrac{v-v_{0}}{v-v_{s}}\right) f_{0}$
Now, $f=\left(\dfrac{-f_{0}}{v-v_{s}}\right) v_{0}+\dfrac{v f_{0}}{v-v_{s}}$
So, slope $=\dfrac{-f_{0}}{v-v_{s}}$
subject: Physics

question: A bat moving at $10\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ towards a wall sends a sound signal of $8000\,Hz$ towards it. On refelection it hears a sound of frequency $f$. The value of $f$ in $Hz$ is close to (speed of sound $ = 320\,m{s^{ - 1}}$)
explanation:
Reflected frequency of sound reaching bat
$ = \left[ {\frac{{v - \left( { - {v_0}} \right)}}{{v - {v_s}}}} \right]f = \left[ {\frac{{v + {v_0}}}{{v - {v_s}}}} \right]f = \frac{{v + 10}}{{v - 10}}f$
$ = \left( {\frac{{320 + 10}}{{320 - 10}}} \right) \times 8000 = 8516\,Hz$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Doppler Effect in Case of Reflection of Sound Waves from a Wall /Moving Object

question: Two factories are sounding their sirens at $800\,Hz$. A man goes from one factory to other at a speed of $2\,m/s$. The velocity of sound is $320\,m/s$. The number of beats heard by the person in one second will be
explanation:
Let $f_{1} \& f_{2}$ be the frequencies received by the pearson from two factories.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956174/preptracker/questions/q_40976_explanation_1764956174379.png
${f_1} = {f_o}\left( {\frac{{v - {v_o}}}{v}} \right) = 800\left( {\frac{{320 - 2}}{{320}}} \right)$
${f_2} = {f_o}\left( {\frac{{v + {v_o}}}{v}} \right) = 800\left( {\frac{{320 + 2}}{{320}}} \right)$
${f_2} - {f_1} = 10\,Hz$
subject: Physics

question: A source of sound A emitting waves of frequency $1800\,Hz$ is falling towards ground with a terminal speed $v$. The observer $B$ on the ground directly beneath the source receives waves of frequency $2150\,Hz$. The source A receives waves, refelected from ground of frequency nearly ( speed of sound $ = 343\,m/s$)
explanation:
Given ${f_A} = 1800\,Hz{\text{ }}\,\& \,{f_B} = 2150\,Hz$
$f_{B}=f_{A}\left(\dfrac{u}{u-v}\right)$
here, $u = 343\,m/s$
$v = 55.8372\,m/s$
Now, for the reflected wave,
$\therefore f_A^\prime = \left( {\frac{{u + {v_t}}}{{u - {v_t}}}} \right){f_A}$
$ = \left( {\frac{{343 + 55.83}}{{343 - 55.83}}} \right) \times 1800$
$ = 2499.44 \approx 2500\,Hz$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Doppler Effect in Case of Reflection of Sound Waves from a Wall /Moving Object

question: An engine approaches a hill with a constant speed. When it is at a distance of $0.9\,km$, it blows a whistle whose echo is heard by the driver after 5 seconds. If the speed of sound in air is $330\,m/s$, then the speed of the engine is:
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956173/preptracker/questions/q_40968_explanation_1764956172765.png
Let after $5 \mathrm{sec}$ engine reaches point $C$
$t = \frac{{AB}}{{330}} + \frac{{BC}}{{330}}$
$5 = \frac{{0.9 \times 1000}}{{330}} + \frac{{BC}}{{330}}$
$\therefore BC = 750\,m$
Distance travelled by engine in $5 \mathrm{sec}$
$ = 900\,m - 750\,m = 150\,m$
Therefore velocity of engine $ = \frac{{150\,m}}{{5\sec }} = 30\,m/s$
subject: Physics

question: $A$ and $B$ are two sources generating sound waves. $A$ listener is situated at $C$. The frequency of the source at $A$ is $500\,Hz$. A, now, move towards $C$ with a speed $4\,m/s.$ the number of beats heard at $C$ is 6 . When $A$ moves a way from $C$ with speed $4\,m/s$, the number of beats heard at $C$ is 18 . The speed of sound is $340\,m/s$. The frequency of the source at $B$ is:
explanation:
$f=500 H z$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950129/preptracker/questions/q_40964_explanation_1764950129594.png
Case 1: When source is moving towards stationary listener
apparent frequency $f^{\prime}=f\left(\dfrac{v}{v-v_{s}}\right)$
$ = 500\left( {\frac{{340}}{{336}}} \right) = 506\,Hz$
Case 2: When source is moving away from the stationary listener
${f^{\prime \prime }} = f\left( {\frac{v}{{v + {v_s}}}} \right) = 500\left( {\frac{{340}}{{344}}} \right) = 494\,Hz$
In case 1 number of beats heard is 6 and in case 2 number of beats heard is 18 therefore frequency of the source at $B = 512\,Hz$
subject: Physics

question: Source and observer, both start moving simultaneously from origin, one along \(x\) - axis and the other along \(y\)-axis with speed of source equal to twice the speed of observer. The correct graph between frequency and time is
explanation:
Let speed of observer be $v$ along $y$-aixs and speed of source be $v_{S}=2 v$ along $x$-axis.
https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2023_09_25_b33ca960859c43be16a8g-05.jpg?height=370&width=521&top_left_y=1623&top_left_x=411
$O P=v t, O S=2 v t$
$\cos \alpha = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 5 }}\,\& \,\cos \beta = \frac{2}{{\sqrt 5 }}$
Now, apparent frequency $f^{\prime}$ is given by
$f^{\prime}=f\left(\dfrac{v-v_{o} \cos \alpha}{v+v_{s} \cos \beta}\right)$
Where $v$ is velocity of sound. $f^{\prime}=f\left(\dfrac{v-\dfrac{v}{\sqrt{5}}}{v+\dfrac{4 v}{\sqrt{5}}}\right)$
Clearly, $f^{\prime}$ is constant but $f^{\prime} < f$. This is shown is curve (3).
subject: Physics

question: An observer starts moving with uniform acceleration a towards a stationary sound emitting a whistle of frequency \[n\], As the observer approaches sources, the apparent frequency, heard by the observer varies with time as
explanation: For observer approaching a stationary source $n^{\prime}=\dfrac{v+v_{0}}{v} \cdot n$ and given $v_{0}=a t \Rightarrow n^{\prime}=\left(\dfrac{a n}{v}\right) t+n$ this is the equation of straight line with positive intercept $n$ and positive slope $\left(\dfrac{n}{v}\right)$. So the correct graph is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Doppler Effect when there is a Relative Acceleration between Source and Observer

question: A source of sound of frequency \(f_{1}\) is placed on the ground. A detector placed at a height is released from rest on this source. The observed frequency \[f(\;Hz)\] is plotted against time \[t(\sec )\]. The speed of sound in air is \[300\;m/s\]. Find \(f_{1}\left(g=10 m / s^{2}\right)\)
explanation:
$f=\left(\dfrac{v+v_{\mathrm{o}}}{v}\right) f_{1}=f_{1}+f_{1} \dfrac{v_{\mathrm{o}}}{v}$
So, $f=f_{1}+\left(\dfrac{f_{1} g}{v}\right) t \quad \quad\left(v_{o}=g t\right)$
Slope of graph $=\dfrac{f_{1} g}{v} \Rightarrow \dfrac{(2)\left(10^{3}\right)-f_{1}}{30}=\dfrac{\left(f_{1}\right)(10)}{300}$
$ \Rightarrow {f_1} = {10^3}\,Hz$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Doppler Effect when there is a Relative Acceleration between Source and Observer

question: A source $S$ and an observer $O$, moving with speeds $v_{1}$ and $v_{2}$ respectively, are moving away from each other. When they are separated by distance $a(t=0)$, a pulse is emitted by the source. This pulse is received by $O$ at time ${{\text{t}}_1}$  then $t_{1}$, is equal to (velocity of sound is $v_{s}$ )
explanation: $\begin{aligned}& t=\dfrac{\text { Relative separation }}{\text { Relative velocity }} \\& t=\dfrac{a}{v_{s}-v_{2}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: In the given arrangement a source is placed at the fixed end of the spring with spring constant $100\,N/m$ and the receiver is attached to the moving end of the spring, which is connected to a box of mass $4\,kg$, which is oscillating horizontally with an amplitude of oscillation $2\,m$. If the source emits a frequency of $990\,Hz$ then (speed of sound $ = 330\,m/s$)
explanation:
The maximum velocity of the observer
${\text{ (block) is }}{v_o} = A\omega = A\sqrt {\frac{k}{m}} $
${v_o} = 2 \times \sqrt {\frac{{100}}{4}} = 10\,m/s$
The maximum and minimum frequency is received by the receiver when it is at the mean position
$\begin{gathered}f_{\text {max }}=\left(\dfrac{v+v_{0}}{v}\right) f_{0}=1020 H z \\f_{\text {min }}=\left(\dfrac{v-v_{0}}{v}\right) f_{0}=960 H z\end{gathered}$
Frequency band width received by the receiver ranges between $960\,Hz - 1020\,Hz$

options.text: Maximum frequency received by the receiver is $990\,Hz$
Minimum frequency received by the receiver is $990\,Hz$
Frequency band width received by the receiver is $960\,Hz - 1020\,Hz$
Maximum and minimum frequencies are received at the extreme positions

subject: Physics

question: A train has just completed a U-curve in a track which is a semicircle. The engine is at the forward end of the semi circular part of the track while the last carriage is at the rear end of the semicircular track. The driver blows a whistle of frequency $200\,Hz$. Velocity of sound is 340 $m/\sec $. Then the apparent frequency as observed by a passenger in the middle of a train when the speed of the train is $30\,m/\sec $ is
explanation:
The situation is shown in the fig. Both the source (engine) and the observer (Person in the middle of the train) have the same speed, but their direction of motion is right angles to each other. The component of velocity of observer towards source is $v \cos 45^{\circ}$ and that of source along the line joining the observer and source is also $v \cos 45^{\circ}$. There is no relative motion between them, so there is no change in frequency heard. So frequency heard is $200Hz$.
https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2023_09_25_b33ca960859c43be16a8g-03.jpg?height=489&width=365&top_left_y=1701&top_left_x=526
$\begin{aligned}& f^{\prime}=f\left(\dfrac{v+v_{t} \cos 45^{\circ}}{v+v \cos 45^{\circ}}\right) \\& f^{\prime}=f\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A sound wave of frequency $n$ travels horizontally to the right. It is reflected from a large vertical plane surface moving to the left with speed $v$. The speed of the sound in the medium is $c$. Then,
explanation:
Large vertical plane acts as listener.
The frequency received by the listener is
$\therefore \quad n^{\prime}=\dfrac{(c+v) n}{c}$
This is the number of waves striking the surface per second.

options.text:
The wavelength of the reflected wave is
$\left[\dfrac{c}{n}\right]\left[\dfrac{c+v}{c-v}\right]$

The frequency of the reflected wave is
$\left[\dfrac{c+v}{c-v}\right] n$
The number of beats heard by a stationary listener to the left to the reflecting surface is $\dfrac{n v}{c-v}$
The number of waves striking the surface per second is $\left[\dfrac{c+v}{c}\right] n$

subject: Physics
subtopic: Doppler Effect in Case of Reflection of Sound Waves from a Wall /Moving Object

question: A source $S$ of acoustic wave of the frequency $1700\,Hz$ and a receiver $R$ are located at the same point. At the instant $t=0$, the source starts from rest to move away from the receiver with a constant acceleration $\alpha$. The velocity of sound in air is $v = 340\,m/s$. If $\alpha  = 10\,m/{s^2}$, the apparent frequency that will be recorded by the stationary receiver at $t = 10\,s$ will be
explanation:
$v_{S}=u+a t=0+10(10)=100$
Apparent frequency
$=\left(\dfrac{v}{v+v_{s}}\right) f=\left(\dfrac{340}{340+100}\right) 1700=1313.6 H z \text {. }$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Doppler Effect when there is a Relative Acceleration between Source and Observer

question: When the observer moves towards the stationary source with velocity, $v_{1}$ the apparent frequency of emitted note is $f_{1}$. When the observer moves away from the source with velocity $v_{1}$, the apparent frequency is $f_{2}$. If $\mathrm{v}$ is the velocity of sound in air and $\dfrac{f_{1}}{f_{2}}=2$ and $\dfrac{v}{v_{1}}=$ ?
explanation:
According to the question
$\dfrac{f_{1}}{f_{2}}=2 \quad \text { [given] }$
[ $f_{1}=$ apparent frequeny when velocity $v_{1}$ is towards the observer]
[ $f_{2}=$ apparent frequency when velocity $v_{1}$ is away from the observer]
Now, the apparent frequency of sound when observer moves towards the source is given by
${f_1} = \left( {\frac{v}{{v - {v_1}}}} \right){f_o}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
[symbols have their usual meanings]
Similarly when observer moves away from the source, apparent frequency is given by
${f_2} = \left( {\frac{v}{{v + {v_1}}}} \right){f_o}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From eq.(1) and eq.(2), we get
$\begin{gathered}\dfrac{f_{1}}{f_{2}}=\dfrac{\left(\dfrac{v}{v-v_{1}}\right) f_{o}}{\left(\dfrac{v}{v+v_{1}}\right) f_{o}}=\dfrac{v+v_{1}}{v-v_{1}} \\\Rightarrow \dfrac{v+v_{1}}{v-v_{1}}=2 \Rightarrow 2 v-2 v_{1}=v+v_{1}\end{gathered}$
$\Rightarrow v=3 v_{1} \Rightarrow \dfrac{v}{v_{1}}=3$
subject: Physics

question: A bat flies at a steady speed of $4\,m\,{s^{ - 1}}$ emitting a sound of $f=90 \times 10^{3}$. It is flying horizon tally towards a vertical wall. The frequency of the reflected sound as detected by the bat will be (Take velocity of sound in air as $330\,m\,{s^{ - 1}}$ )
explanation:
Here, the bat is a source of sound and the wall is an observer at rest.
$\therefore$ Frequency of sound reaching the wall is
${f^\prime } = \frac{{vf}}{{v - {v_s}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
where $v$ is the velocity of sound in air and ${v_s}$ is velocity of source.
On reflection, the wall is the source of sound of frequency $f^{\prime}$ at rest and bat is an observer approaching the wall with speed
${v_0} = {v_s} = 4\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
$\therefore$ Frequency heard by the bat is
${f^{\prime \prime }} = \frac{{{f^\prime }\left( {v + {v_0}} \right)}}{v} = f\left( {\frac{{v + {v_0}}}{{v - {v_s}}}} \right)\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} [\operatorname{Using} (1)]$
$90 \times {10^3}\left( {\frac{{330 + 4}}{{330 - 4}}} \right) = \frac{{90 \times {{10}^3} \times 334}}{{326}}$
$ \approx 92.2 \times {10^3}\,Hz.$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Doppler Effect in Case of Reflection of Sound Waves from a Wall /Moving Object

question: The observer is moving with velocity ' $v_{0}$ ' towards the stationary source of sound and then after crossing moves away from the source with velocity $v_{0}$. Assume that the medium through which the sound wave travel is at rest. If $v$ is velocity of sound and $n$ is the frequency emitted by the source, then the difference between apparent frequencies heard by the observer is
explanation:
When the observer is moving towards the source and source is stationary, then the apparent frequency is given as,
${n^\prime } = n\left( {\frac{{v + {v_0}}}{v}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
When the observer is moving away from the source and the source is stationary, then the apparent frequency is given as
${n^{,,}} = n\left( {\frac{{v - {v_0}}}{v}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From eq.(1) and eq.(2), we get
$\begin{gathered}n^{\prime}-n^{\prime \prime}=\dfrac{n}{v}\left(v+v_{0}-v+v_{0}\right) \\=\dfrac{2 n v_{0}}{v}\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics

question: With what velocity should an observer approach a stationary sound source so that the apparent frequency of sound should appear double the actual frequency? ($v$ is velocity of sound).
explanation:
When the actual frequency is $f$, the apparent frequency of the sound to the observer is
$f^{\prime}=f \times \dfrac{\left(v+v_{0}\right)}{v}$
Here, $v=$ speed of sound and $v_{0}=$ speed of observer.
When $f^{\prime}=2 f, \dfrac{\left(v+v_{0}\right)}{v}=2 \Rightarrow v_{0}=v$
$\therefore$ When the observer approaches the stationary source at the speed of sound, the apparent frequency is double the actual frequency.
subject: Physics

question: A person with vibrating tuning fork of frequency $338\,Hz$ is moving towards a vertical wall with a speed of $2\,m{s^{ - 1}}$. Velocity of sound in air is 340 $m{s^{ - 1}}$. The number of beats heard by that person per second is
explanation:
Frequency of tuning fork (source), $f = 338\,Hz$
Velocity of sound, $v = 340\,m\,{s^{ - 1}}$
When the person is moving towards the wall, the frequency of sound reaching the wall is
${f^\prime } = \frac{{fv}}{{v - {v_S}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Where ${v_s} = $ speed of the person $ = 2\,m{s^{ - 1}}$.
On reflection the wall acts as source and the person is the listener moving towards the source. So the frequency heard by the person after reflection from the wall is
${f^{\prime \prime }} = \left[ {\frac{{v + {v_L}}}{v}} \right]{f^\prime }\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Substituting the value of $f^{\prime}$ from eq. (1) in (2), we get
${f^{\prime \prime }} = \left[ {\frac{{v + {v_L}}}{{v - {v_S}}}} \right]f = \left( {\frac{{v + {v_S}}}{{v - {v_S}}}} \right)f\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \left[ {{\text{ }}As{\text{ }}{v_L} = {v_S}} \right]$
$ = \left[ {\frac{{340 + 2}}{{340 - 2}}} \right] \times 338 = 342\,Hz$
$\therefore$ Number of beats heard per second ${f^{\prime \prime }} - f = 342 - 338 = 4$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Doppler Effect in Case of Reflection of Sound Waves from a Wall /Moving Object

question: An engine is moving towards a wall with a velocity $50\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ emits a note of $1.2kHz$. Speed of sound in air $ = 350\,m{s^{ - 1}}$. The frequency of the note after reflection from the wall as heard by the driver of the engine is
explanation:
The reflected sound travels in the direction opposite to that of the original sound of engine. Thus the source and the observer can be presumed to approach each other with same velocity. If the original frequency is $f$ then apparent frequency heard by the driver
${{\text{f}}^\prime } = \frac{{{\text{f}}\left( {v + {v_0}} \right)}}{{\left( {v - {v_S}} \right)}} = {\text{f}}\left( {\frac{{v + {v_S}}}{{v - {v_S}}}} \right)\left[ {\because {v_o} = {v_S}} \right]$
$ \Rightarrow {f^\prime } = 1.2\left( {\frac{{350 + 50}}{{350 - 50}}} \right) = \frac{{1.2 \times 400}}{{300}} = 1.6kHz$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Doppler Effect in Case of Reflection of Sound Waves from a Wall /Moving Object

question: A source of sound is moving with a velocity of $50\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ towards a stationary observer. The observer measures the frequency of sound as $500\,Hz$. The apparent frequency of sound as heard by the observer when source is moving away from him with the same speed is (Speed of sound at room temperature $350\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
explanation:
Speed of source ${v_S} = 50\,m{s^{ - 1}}$,
apparent frequency ${f^\prime } = 500\,Hz$, speed of sound $v = 350\,m{s^{ - 1}}$.
When source is moving towards stationary observer
${f^\prime } = \left( {\frac{v}{{v - {v_S}}}} \right)f\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Where $f = $ actual frequency of the source.
When source is moving away from stationary observer,
apparent frequency
${f^{\prime \prime }} = \left( {\frac{v}{{v + {v_S}}}} \right)f\,$
${\text{or, }}{f^{\prime \prime }} = \left( {\frac{{v - {v_S}}}{{v + {v_S}}}} \right){f^\prime }\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} [{\text{Using}}\,{\text{eqn}}.({\text{1}})]$
$ = \left( {\frac{{350 - 50}}{{350 + 50}}} \right) \times 500 = 375\,Hz$
subject: Physics

question: When source of sound moves towards a stationary observer, the wavelength of sound received by him
explanation:
When source of sound moving towards to stationary observer
Apparent frequency $\left(n_{a}\right)=n\left[\dfrac{v}{v-v_{s}}\right]$
Hence, the wavelength of sound received by him decreases while frequency increases

options.text: Remains the same, whereas frequency increases
Decreases while frequency remains the same

subject: Physics

question:
Frequency of sound wave does not change when the listener moves towards or away from stationary source.
The motion of source causes the apparent change in wavelength.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
When a source of sound passes us, the pitch we hear goes from high to low.
According to Doppler's effect, there is apparent change in the frequency of sound observed due to relative motion between the oberver and the source of sound.
explanation:
If $f_{0}$ is the frequency of sound emitted by the source then
$\begin{aligned}& f_{\text {approach }}=f_{0}\left(\dfrac{v}{v-v_{s}}\right) \\& f_{\text {receding }}=f_{0}\left(\dfrac{v}{v+v_{s}}\right) \\& f_{\text {approach }}>f_{\text {receding }}\end{aligned}$

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Physics

question: A train whistling at constant frequency is moving towards a station at a constant speed $v$. The train goes past a stationary observer on the station. The frequency $n^{\prime}$ of the sound as heard by the observer is plotted as a function of time $t$ (figure). Identify the expected curve.
explanation:
Whistling train is the source of sound, $v_{s}$.
Before crossing a stationary observer on station, frequency heard is $n^{\prime}=\dfrac{v n}{\left(v-v_{s}\right)}=$ constant and $n^{\prime}>n$.
Here, $v$ is velocity of sound in air and $n$ is actual frequency of whistle.
After crossing the stationary observer, frequency heard is $n^{\prime \prime}=\dfrac{v n}{\left(v+v_{s}\right)}=$ constant and $n^{\prime \prime} < n$.
Therefore, the expected curve is (4).
subject: Physics

question: A stationary observer receives a sound of frequency \[2000\;Hz\]. The variation of apparent frequency and time is shown. Find the speed of source. If velocity of sound is \[300\;m/s\].
explanation:
As the apparent frequency is changing non linearly with time so it must be a two dimensional Doppler effects as shown in the figure.
For AP, $f=f_{0}\left(\dfrac{v}{v-v_{S} \cos \theta}\right)$
For PB $f=f_{0}\left(\dfrac{v}{v+v_{S} \cos \theta}\right)$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956989/preptracker/questions/q_40222_explanation_1764956988902.png
$\begin{aligned}& f_{\text {min }}=1800=f_{0}\left(\dfrac{v}{v+v_{s}}\right)=2000\left(\dfrac{v}{v+v_{s}}\right) \\& f_{\max }=2250=f_{0}\left(\dfrac{v}{v-v_{s}}\right)=2000\left(\dfrac{v}{v-v_{s}}\right)\end{aligned}$
On solving $ = 33.3\,m/s.$
subject: Physics

question: An observer approaches towards a stationary source of sound at constant velocity and recedes away at the same speed. the graph of wavelength observed with time is
explanation: Wavelength remains same during approach and recede.
subject: Physics

question: A stationary observer recevies a sound from a source of frequency \(f_{0}\) moving with a constant velocity \[{v_s} = 30\;m/s\]. The apparent frequency varies with time as shown in the figure. Velocity of sound \[v = 303\;m/s\]. Then which of the following is incorrect?
explanation:
This frequency-time curve corresponds to a source moving at an angle to a stationary observer.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956129/preptracker/questions/q_40202_explanation_1764956128797.png
In the region $SN$, the source is moving towards the observer, i.e., the apparent frequency
$\begin{aligned}& f^{\prime}=f_{0}\left(\dfrac{v}{v-v_{s} \cos \theta}\right) \\& f^{\prime}=f_{0}\left(\dfrac{300}{300-30 \cos \theta}\right)\end{aligned}$
When $\theta = \pi /2,i.e.,\,{\text{at }}\,N$,
${f^\prime } = {f_0} = 1000\,Hz,$
When $\theta=0$, i.e., $\cos \theta=1$,
${f_{{\text{max}}}} = {f_0}\frac{v}{{v - {v_s}}} = \frac{{(1000\,Hz)(300\,m/s)}}{{(300\,m/s - 30\,m/s)}}$
$ = \frac{{10}}{9} \times 1000\,Hz = 1111\,Hz$
${f_{{\text{min}}}} = {f_0}\frac{v}{{v + {v_s}}} = \frac{{1000 \times 300}}{{330}} = 909\,Hz$
Option (1) is Correct

options.text: The minimum value of apparent frequency is $889\,Hz.$
The natural frequency of source is $1000\,Hz.$
The frequency-time curve corresponds to a source moving at an angle to the stationary observer.
The maximum value of apparent frequency is $1111\,Hz.$

subject: Physics

question: A tuning fork of frequency $380\,Hz$ is moving towards a wall with a velocity of $4\,m/s$. Then the number of beats heard by a stationary listener between direct and reflected sounds will be:(velocity of sound in air is $340\,m/s$ )
explanation: On reflection frequency does not change so not beats are heard.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Doppler Effect in Case of Reflection of Sound Waves from a Wall /Moving Object

question: A source of sound and an observer are approaching each other with the same speed, which is equal to $\dfrac{1}{10}$ times the speed of sound. The apparent relative change in the frequency of the source is
explanation:
Speed of sound $=v$ (say)
Speed of source $=$ speed of observer $=\dfrac{v}{10}$
Let natural frequency of sound $=n$
Apparent frequency of sound,
$n^{\prime}=n\left(\dfrac{v+v_{o}}{v-v_{s}}\right)$
Since source is moving in the direction of sound and observer moving towards source, then
$v_{o}=\dfrac{v}{10} \text { and } v_{s}=\dfrac{v}{10}$
$\therefore {n^\prime } = n\frac{{\left( {v + \frac{v}{{10}}} \right)}}{{\left( {v - \frac{1}{{10}}} \right)}} = \left( {\frac{{\frac{{11v}}{{10}}}}{{\frac{{9v}}{{10}}}}} \right)$
$ = \frac{{11}}{9}n \Rightarrow \frac{{{n^\prime } - n}}{n} = \frac{2}{9}$
$\therefore \frac{{{n^\prime } - n}}{n} \times 100\% = \frac{2}{9} \times 100\% = \frac{{200}}{9}\% = 22.22$
subject: Physics

question: A listener is at rest with respect to the source of sound. A wind starts blowing along the line joining the source and the observer. Which of the following quantities do not change?
explanation:
$\because f^{\prime}=\left(\dfrac{v_{\text {sound }} \pm v_{0}+v_{\text {wind }}}{v_{\text {sound }} \mp v_{\text {source }}+v_{\text {wind }}}\right) f$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {f^\prime } = f.$
$\left( {{v_o} = {v_{{\text{source }}}} = 0} \right)$
So time period remain same.
subject: Physics

question: A boy is walking away from a wall at a speed of $1.0\,m/s$ as shown in the figure. The boy blows a whistle steadily. An observer towards whom the boy is moving hears 4 beats. If the speed of sound is $340\,m/s$, the frequency of whistle is:
explanation:
Direct frequency received by the observer is
${f_1} = f\left( {\frac{{340}}{{340 - 1}}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Frequency received by the wall is
${f_2} = f\left( {\frac{{340}}{{340 + 1}}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Frequency received by the observer after reflection from the wall is
$f_{3}=f_{2}$
Given that
${f_1}\, \sim {f_3} = 4$
$f\left[ {\frac{{340}}{{339}} - \frac{{340}}{{341}}} \right] = 4$
$ \Rightarrow f = 680\,Hz$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Doppler Effect in Case of Reflection of Sound Waves from a Wall /Moving Object

question: Two sound sources emitting sound each of wavelength $\lambda$ are fixed at a given distance apart. A listener moves with a velocity $u$ along the line joining the two sources. The number of beats heard by him per second is :
explanation:
$f_{1}=\left(\dfrac{v+u}{v}\right) f_{0}$ and $f_{2}=\left(\dfrac{v-u}{v}\right) f_{0}$
Number of beats per second
$\begin{aligned}f_{1}-f_{2} & =\left(\dfrac{v+u}{v}\right) f_{0}-\left(\dfrac{v-u}{v}\right) f_{0} \\& =\left(\dfrac{v+u-v+u}{v}\right) f_{0} \\& =2 u\left(\dfrac{f_{0}}{v}\right)=\dfrac{2 u}{\lambda}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A man is standing between two parallel cliffs and fires a gun. If he hears first and second echoes after $1.5\,s$ and $5\,s$ respectively, the distance between the cliffs is (Velocity of sound in air $ = 340\,m{s^{ - 1}})$
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950992/preptracker/questions/q_39923_explanation_1764950991813.png
$2 d_{1}+2 d_{2}=v \times t_{1}+v \times t_{2}$
$\Rightarrow 2\left(d_{1}+d_{2}\right)=v\left(t_{1}+t_{2}\right)$
${d_1} + {d_2} = \frac{{v\left( {{t_1} + {t_2}} \right)}}{2} = \frac{{340 \times (1.5 + 3.5)}}{2} = 850\,m$
subject: Physics

question: A motor car blowing a horn of frequency $124\,Hz$ moves with a velocity $72\,km/hr$  towards a tall wall. The frequency of the refelected sound heard by the driver will be (velocity of sound in air is $330\,m/s)$
explanation:
The frequency of reflected sound heard by the driver
The frequency received by the wall is
$f_{1}=f\left(\dfrac{v}{v-v_{s}}\right)$
The frequency received by the car from wall is
${f_2} = {f_1}\left( {\frac{{v + {v_s}}}{v}} \right) \Rightarrow {f_2} = f\left( {\frac{{v + {v_s}}}{{v - {v_s}}}} \right)$
$ = 124\left[ {\frac{{330 + (72 \times 5/18)}}{{330 - (72 \times 5/18)}}} \right] = 140\,Hz$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Doppler Effect in Case of Reflection of Sound Waves from a Wall /Moving Object

question: A band playing music at frequency $f$ is moving towards a wall at a speed $v_{b}$. A motorist is following the band with a speed $v_{m}$. If $v$ be the speed of the sound, the expression for beat frequency heard by motorist is
explanation:
Motorist will hear two frequencies:
(1) directly from the band and (2) reflected from the wall. The frequency heard directly by the motorist from band
${f_1} = f\left[ {\frac{{v + {v_m}}}{{v + {v_b}}}} \right]\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
The frequency of sound reaching the wall,
${f^{\prime \prime }} = f\left[ {\frac{v}{{v - {v_b}}}} \right]\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
The frequency of reflected sound from the wall, heard by motorist,
${f_2} = {f^{\prime \prime }}\left[ {\frac{{v + {v_m}}}{v}} \right]$
${f_2} = f\left( {\frac{{v + {v_m}}}{{v - {v_b}}}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left[ {{\text{from}}\,\,\left( 2 \right)} \right]$
Hence the frequency heard by the motorist
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta f=f_{2}-f_{1} \\& \Rightarrow \Delta f=f\left(\dfrac{v+v_{m}}{v-v_{b}}\right)-f\left(\dfrac{v+v_{m}}{v+v_{b}}\right) \\& \Rightarrow \Delta f=f\left(\dfrac{v+v_{m}}{v^{2}-v_{b}^{2}}\right) \times 2 v_{b} .\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A source of sound $S$ emitting waves of frequency $100\,Hz$ and an observer $O$are located at some distance from each other. The source is moving with a speed of $19.4\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with the source observer line as shown in the figure. The observer is at rest. The apparent frequency observed by the observer (velocity of sound in air $330\,m{s^{ - 1}}$) is
explanation:
The frequency received by the observer is
${f^\prime } = f\left( {\frac{v}{{v - {v_s}\cos 60^\circ }}} \right) = 100\left( {\frac{{330}}{{330 - 19.4\cos 60^\circ }}} \right)$
${f^\prime } = 100\left( {\frac{{330}}{{330 - 9.7}}} \right) = 103\,Hz$
subject: Physics

question: A detector away from the source of a wave is detecting pulses of that wave every 0.2 second. If the detector starts to move towards the sources at a speed of $6.0\,km/h$, then it would detect a total of 18200 pulses per hour. What is the speed of the wave?
explanation:
From doppler effect.
The frequency received by the detector is
$f^{\prime}=f\left(\dfrac{v+v_{D}}{v}\right)$
Given that $f=5$ ( 5 - waves per second )
${f^\prime } = \frac{{18200}}{{3600}} = \frac{{91}}{{18}}$
${v_D} = \frac{5}{3}\,m/s$
By keeping these values in the above equation we get $v = 150\,m/s$
subject: Physics

question: A small source of sound moves on a circle (in anti clockwise) as shown in figure and an observer is sitting at $O$. Let $f_{A}, f_{B}, f_{C}$ be the frequencies heard when the source is at $A$, $B$ and $C$ respectively.
explanation:
Frequency heard by observer (stationary) will be maximum, if the source is moving towards the observer, which is the case when the source is at $B$. Frequency heard by observer (stationary) will be mininum, if the source is moving away from the observer, which is the case when the source is at $A$.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950956/preptracker/questions/q_39902_explanation_1764950955884.png
For point $C$, frequency heard is equal to the original frequency
subject: Physics

question: Consider two sound sources $S_{1}$ and $S_{2}$ having same frequency $100\,Hz$ and the observer $O$ located between them. All the three are moving with same velocity in same direction. The beat frequency as heard by the observer is
explanation: Apparent frequency is equal to the frequency since relative velocity is zero. So number of beats will be zero.
subject: Physics

question: When a source moves away from stationary observer with velocity $v_{o}$ then apparent change in frequency is $\Delta n_{1}$. When an observer approaches the stationary source with same velocity $v$ then change in frequency is $\Delta n_{2}$ then
explanation:
$f_{1}=f\left(\dfrac{v}{v+v_{o}}\right)$
$\begin{aligned}& \Delta n_{1}=f-f_{1}=\dfrac{f v_{o}}{v+v_{o}} \\& f_{2}=f\left(\dfrac{v+v_{o}}{v}\right) \\& \Delta n_{2}=f_{2}-f=f \dfrac{v_{o}}{v} \Rightarrow \Delta n_{2}>\Delta n_{1}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: Two cars $A$ and $B$ approach a stationary observer from opposite sides as shown in fig. Observer hears no beats. If the frequency of the horn of the car $B$ is $504\,Hz$, the frequency of horn of car A will be:
explanation:
Since, the person heard no beats therefore their apparent frequency are the same.
${\text{So, }}n \times \frac{{330}}{{330 - 15}} = 504 \times \frac{{330}}{{330 - 30}}$
$n = \frac{{504 \times 315}}{{300}} = 529.2\,Hz$
subject: Physics

question: A source of sound is moving with constant velocity of $20\,m/s$ emitting a note of frequency $1000\,Hz$. The ratio of frequencies observed by a stationary observer while the source is approaching him and after it crosses him will be
explanation:
When source is approaching the observer, the frequency heard ${n_a} = \left( {\frac{v}{{v - {v_s}}}} \right) \times n = \left( {\frac{{340}}{{340 - 20}}} \right) \times 1000 = 1063\,Hz$
When source is receding, the frequency heard
$\begin{aligned}& n_{r}=\left(\dfrac{v}{v+v_{s}}\right) \times n=\dfrac{340}{340+20} \times 1000=944 \\& \Rightarrow n_{a}: n_{r}=9: 8\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: An observer moves towards a stationary source of sound, with a velocity one-fifth of the velocity of sound. What is the percentage increase in the apparent frequency?
explanation:
Let $v$ be the velocity of the sound and be the velocity of the observer.
$\begin{aligned}& n^{\prime}=n\left[\dfrac{v+v_{0}}{v}\right]=n\left[\dfrac{v+\dfrac{v}{5}}{v}\right]=n\left[\dfrac{6}{5}\right] \\& \dfrac{n^{\prime}}{n}=\dfrac{6}{5} ; \dfrac{n^{\prime}-n}{n}=\dfrac{6-5}{5} \times 100=20 \%\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A train moving at a speed of  $200\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ towards stationary object emits a sound of frequency $1000\,Hz$. Some of the sound reaching the object gets reflected back to the train as echo. The frequency of the echo as detected by the driver of the train is : (speed of sound in air is $300\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ )
explanation:
From Doppler's shift, we know for this case
${f^\prime } = {f_0}\left( {\frac{{v + {v_s}}}{{v - {v_s}}}} \right) = 1000\left( {\frac{{330 + 220}}{{330 - 220}}} \right)$
$ = 1000\left( {\frac{{550}}{{110}}} \right) = 5000\,Hz.$
subject: Physics

$f=f_{\mathrm{ap}_{1}}-f_{\mathrm{ap}_{2}}=96 H z$

question:
The magnetic field of plane
electromagnetic wave is given by
${B_y} = 2 \times {10^{ - 7}}\sin \left( {0.5 \times {{10}^3}x + 1.5 \times {{10}^{11}}t} \right)T$.
This electromagnetic wave is
explanation:
We have,
${B_y} = 2 \times {10^{ - 7}}\sin \left( {0.5 \times {{10}^3}x + 1.5 \times {{10}^{11}}t} \right)$
Comparing with the standard equation
${B_y} = {B_0}\sin (kx + \omega t)$, we get
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,k = 0.5 \times {10^3}$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\lambda = \frac{{2\pi }}{k}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = \frac{{2\pi }}{{0.5 \times {{10}^3}}}\,$
${\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, = 0.01256\,m$
The wavelength range of microwaves is
${10^{ - 3}}$ to $0.1\,m.$ The wavelength of this
wave lies between ${10^{ - 3}}$ to $0.1\,m$, so the
equation represents a microwave.

question: One requires $11eV$ of energy to dissociate a carbon monoxide molecule into carbon and oxygen atoms. The minimum frequency of the appropriate electromagnetic radiation to achieve the dissociation lies in
explanation:
Given, energy required to dissociate a carbon monoxide molecule into carbon and oxygen atoms.
$E = 11e\,V$
We know that, $E = h\upsilon $, where
$h = 6.62 \times {10^{ - 34}}J - s;\upsilon = {\rm{ frequency }}$
$ \Rightarrow 11e\upsilon = h\upsilon $
$ \Rightarrow \quad \upsilon = \frac{{11 \times 1 \cdot 6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}{h}\,J$
$E = h\upsilon $
$ = \frac{{11 \times 1 \cdot 6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}{{6 \cdot 62 \times {{10}^{ - 34}}}}\,J = 2.65 \times {10^{15}}\,Hz$
This frequency belongs to ultraviolet region.
subject: Physics

question: A plate of mass 10 $g$ is in equilibrium in air due to the force exerted by a light beam on the plate. Calculate power of the beam (in $MW$). Assume that the plate is perfectly absorbing.
explanation:
For equilibrium, the force exerted by the light beam should balance the weight of plate.
${F_{{\rm{photon }}}} = mg$
$\left. {\left( {{F_{{\text{photon}}}} = \frac{{IA}}{c} = \frac{P}{c}} \right.,{\text{where}}\,\,{\text{power}}\,\,P = IA} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{P}{c} = 10 \times {10^{ - 3}} \times 10$
$ \Rightarrow P = 30 \times {10^6}\,W = 30\,\,MW$
subject: Physics

question: A totally reflecting, small plane mirror placed horizontally faces a parallel beam of light as shown in the figure. The mass of the mirror is $20\,g$. Assume that there is no absorption in the lens and that ${30 \%}$ of the light emitted by the source goes through the lens. The power of the source  needed to support the weight of the mirror is
explanation:
As ${30 \%}$ light is incident and reflected by mirror, force due to reflection of light on mirror is
$F = 2 \times \frac{{0.3P}}{c} = \frac{{0.6\,P}}{c}$
To support the weight of mirror, we use ${\dfrac{0.6 P}{c}=m g}$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} P = \frac{{mgc}}{{0.6}}$
$ = \frac{{20 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 10 \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{0.6}} = {10^8}{\rm{ watt }}$
$=100\,MW$
subject: Physics

explanation: Electromagnetic waves carry energy when travel through space and this energy is equal divided by electric field vectors and magnetic field vectors. Electromagnetic waves are  composed of photons which have momentum and energy when photon is incident on a surface, its momentum changes and hence it exerts force on the surface giving rise to pressure. So, correct option is (3).

options.text: Statement A is correct but statement B is 	  incorrect.
