question: In a plane $E M$ wave, the electric field oscillates sinusoidally at a frequency of $5 \times {10^{10}}\,Hz$ and have an amplitude of  $50\,V{m^{ - 1}}.$ The total average energy density of the electromagnetic field of the wave is [Use  ${\varepsilon _0} = 8.85 \times {10^{ - 12}}{C^2}/N{m^2}\,J\,]$
explanation:
Given,
$f = 5 \times {10^{10}}\,Hz,{E_0} = 50\,V/m$
Total average density of $EM$ wave $u_{T}=\dfrac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0}$
$E_{0}^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{B_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}}$
Here, we will use, $u_{T}=\dfrac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}$
$ = \frac{1}{2} \times 8.85 \times {10^{ - 12}} \times 2500 = 1.106 \times {10^{ - 8}}J{m^{ - 3}}$
subject: Physics

question: If the total energy transferred to a surface in time $t$ is  $6.48 \times {10^5}\,J,$ then the magnitude of the total momentum delivered to this surface for complete absorption will be
explanation:
Total energy transferred to a surface,
$E = 6.4 \times {10^5}\,J$
Total momentum transferred to a surface for absorption is given by
$p=\dfrac{E}{c}$
$p = \frac{{6.48 \times {{10}^5}\,J}}{{3 \times {{10}^8}\,m{s^{ - 1}}}}$
$ \Rightarrow p = 2.16 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,kg\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics

question: The electric field of an electromagnetic wave in free space is represented as $\vec{E}=E_{0} \cos (\omega t-k z) \hat{i}$. The corresponding magnetic induction vector will be
explanation:
Given: $\vec{E}=E_{0} \cos (\omega t-k z) \hat{i}$
From the equation of $\vec{E}$, we can see the direction of $EM$ wave is $+Z$ - direction
$\dfrac{E_{0}}{B_{0}}=c \Rightarrow B_{0}=\dfrac{E_{0}}{c}$
$\vec{E} \times \vec{B}=\hat{k} \Rightarrow \hat{i} \times \vec{B}=\hat{k} \Rightarrow \vec{B}$ is along $+\hat{j}$
$\therefore \quad \vec{B}=\dfrac{E_{0}}{c} \cos (\omega t-k z) \hat{j}$
subject: Physics

question: A plane electromagnetic wave of frequency $35\,M\,Hz$ travels in free space along the $x$ - direction.  At a particular point (in space and time)  $\vec E = 9.6\,\hat j\,\,V/m.$ The value of magnetic field at this point is
explanation:
Given : $f = 35M\,Hz,\vec E = 9.6\hat j\,\,V/m$
Wave is travelling along $x$-axis, $\vec{n}=\vec{E} \times \vec{B}$
So, direction of $\vec{B}$ is along $\hat{k}$.
$\vec{B}=\dfrac{\vec{E}}{c}=\dfrac{9.6}{3 \times 10^{8}}=3.2 \times 10^{-8} ; \vec{B}=3.2 \times 10^{-8} \hat{k}$
subject: Physics

question:
Match the Column I and Column II

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
explanation:
All the mentioned laws and respective equations are given by
Ampere - Maxwell law is given by,
$\oint \vec{B} d \vec{l}=\mu_{0} i_{c}+\mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0} \dfrac{d \phi_{E}}{d t}$
Gauss's law for electricity is given by,
$\oint \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{A}=\dfrac{Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}$
Gauss's law for magnetism is, $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{A}=0$
Faraday's law is $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{l}=\dfrac{d \phi_{B}}{d t}$
So, correct option is (3).

options.text: A – Q,     B – R, 	C – P, 	D – S
A – P,      B – Q, 	C – R, 	D – S
A – S,      B – R, 	C – P, 	D – Q
A – S,      B – P, 	C – R, 	D – Q

subject: Physics

question: If frequency of an electromagnetic wave is $60\,M\,Hz$ and it travels in air along $z$ direction then the corresponding electric and magnetic field vectors will be mutually perpendicular to each other and the wavelength of the wave  $({\rm{in}}\,\,m)$  is
explanation:
Frequency, $f = 60M\,Hz$
Wavelength, $\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{60 \times {{10}^6}}} = 5\,m$
So, correct option is (2).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Wave Equation and its Properties of EM Waves

question:
The magnetic field in a plane electromagnetic wave is
${B_y} = \left( {3.5 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}} \right)\sin \left( {1.5 \times {{10}^3}x + 0.5 \times {{10}^{11}}t} \right)T.$
The corresponding electric field will be
explanation:
The magnetic field of an $EM$ wave propagating along $x$-axis is given by, ${B_y} = {B_0}\sin \,(kx + \omega t)\,T.$ On comparing the above equation with the given electromagnetic wave equation we get, ${B_0} = 3.5 \times {10^{ - 7}}\,\,J.$ The electric field equation is given by,
$E=E_{0} \sin (k x+\omega t) V / m$ Also,
Relation between $E$ and $B$ is given by $\dfrac{E}{B}=c$
$\therefore {E_0} = {B_0}c = 3 \times 3.5 \times {10^8} \times {10^{ - 7}} = 105\,V/{m^{ - 1}}$
$E = {E_0}\sin \left( {1.5 \times {{10}^3}x + 0.5 \times {{10}^{11}}t} \right)V/{m^{ - 1}}$
${E_z} = 105\sin \left( {1.5 \times {{10}^3}x + 0.5 \times {{10}^{11}}t} \right)V/{m^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics

question: A plane $E M$ wave is propagating along $x$ direction. It has a wavelength of  $4\,mm.$ If electric field is in $y$ direction with the maximum magnitude of  $60\,V{m^{ - 1}},$  the equation for magnetic field is
explanation:
Given that, $\lambda = 4\,mm,{E_0} = 60\,V/m$
Amplitude magnetic field is given by
$B_{0}=\dfrac{E_{0}}{c}=\dfrac{60}{3 \times 10^{8}}=2 \times 10^{-7}$
Since wave is travelling in $+x$ direction and electric field is along $+y$ axis. So, $B$ is along $z$ - axis.
General equation of $\vec{B}$ is given by
$\vec{B}=B_{0} \sin [k(x-v t] \hat{k} T$
$f=\dfrac{c}{\lambda}=\dfrac{3 \times 10^{8}}{4 \times 10^{-3}}=\dfrac{3}{4} \times 10^{11}$
$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times \frac{3}{4} \times {10^{11}} = \frac{\pi }{2} \times {10^3}c$
and $k=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda}=\dfrac{2 \pi}{4 \times 10^{-3}}=\dfrac{\pi}{2} \times 10^{3}$
So, $\overrightarrow{B_{z}}=2 \times 10^{-7} \sin \left(\dfrac{\pi}{2} \times 10^{3}\left(x-3 \times 10^{8} t\right)\right) \hat{k} T$
subject: Physics

question: Average force exerted on a non-reflecting surface at normal incidence is  $2.4 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,N.$  If  $360\,W/c{m^2}$  is the light energy flux during span  of 1 hour 30 minutes, then the area of the surface is
explanation:
Force on a non-reflecting surface at normal incidence is given by $F=\dfrac{I A}{c}$
$\therefore A = \frac{{Fc}}{I} = \frac{{2.4 \times {{10}^{ - 4}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8} \times {{10}^{ - 4}}}}{{360}}$
$ = 0.02\,{m^2}$
subject: Physics

question:
In the given electromagnetic wave $E_{y}=600$ sin $(\omega t-k x) V m^{-1}$, intensity of the associated light beam is $({\text{in}}\,\,W/{m^2})$
(Given ${\varepsilon _0} = 9 \times {10^{ - 12}}{C^2}{N^{ - 1}}\,{m^{ - 2}})$
explanation:
Given, $E_{y}=600 \sin (\omega t-k x)$ and
${\varepsilon _0} = 9 \times {10^{ - 12}}{C^2}/N{m^2}$
Intensity of $EM$ wave is given by,
$I=\dfrac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2} c=\dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{B_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}} c$
Intensity, $I=\dfrac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2} c$
$ = \frac{1}{2} \times 9 \times {10^{ - 12}} \times {600^2} \times 3 \times {10^8} = 486\,W/{m^2}$
So, correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: Electromagnetic waves travel in a medium with speed of  $1.5 \times {10^8}\,m{s^{ - 1}}.$  The relative permeability of the medium is 2.0 . The relative permittivity will be
explanation:
Let the relative permittivity be $\varepsilon_{r}$.
Speed of an EM wave in a medium is given by
$v=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_{r} \varepsilon_{r}} \sqrt{\mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}}}$
$\Rightarrow v=\dfrac{c}{\sqrt{\mu_{r} \varepsilon_{r}}}$
$\therefore \,\,\,\,\,\,{\mu _r}{\varepsilon _r} = {\left( {\frac{c}{v}} \right)^2}$
$\Rightarrow \mu_{r} \varepsilon_{r}=\left(\dfrac{3 \times 10^{8}}{1.5 \times 10^{8}}\right)^{2}$
$ \Rightarrow {\varepsilon _r} = \frac{4}{{{\mu _r}}} = \frac{4}{2} = 2\quad (\therefore {\mu _r} = 2.0\,\,\,\,\,\,({\text{given}}))$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Wave Equation and its Properties of EM Waves

question: The electric field in an electromagnetic wave is given by $\vec{E}=\hat{i} 40 \cos \omega\left(t-\dfrac{z}{c}\right) N C^{-1}$. The magnetic field induction of this wave is (in $S I$ unit)
explanation:
$\vec{E}=\hat{i} 40 \cos \omega\left(t-\dfrac{z}{c}\right) N / C$
$\vec{E}$ is along $+X$ axis: $\vec{v}$ is along $+Z$ axis ;
Direction of $E M$ wave is given by
$\vec{v}=\vec{E} \times \vec{B}$
So, $\vec{B}$ is along $+Y$ axis, $\dfrac{E}{B}=c$
$\Rightarrow B=\dfrac{E}{c}=\dfrac{40}{3 \times 10^{8}}$
$\therefore \vec{B}=\dfrac{40}{c} \hat{j} \cos \omega\left(t-\dfrac{z}{c}\right)$
subject: Physics

question: A parallel-plate capacitor made of circular plates, each of radius ${R=6.0 {~cm}}$, has a capacitance $C = 100\,pF$. The capacitor is connected to a $230\,V\,a.c.$ supply with a (angular) frequency of  $300\,rad/s.$  Determine the amplitude of ${B}$ (in ${10^{-11} {~T}}$ ) at a point $3.0\,cm$ from the axis between the plates.
explanation:
We are given that, $C = 100\,pF = 100 \times {10^{ - 12}}\,F = {10^{ - 10}}\,F,$
${V_{rms}} = 230\,\,V,\omega = 300\,rad/s.$
Capacitive reactance, ${X_{C}=\dfrac{1}{\omega C}=\dfrac{1}{300 \times 10^{-10}}=\dfrac{10^{8}}{3} \Omega}$
If ${I_{{rms}}}$ is the $rms$ value of the conduction current,
$I_{{rms}}=\dfrac{V_{{rms}}}{X_{C}}=\dfrac{230}{10^{8} / 3}=6.9 \times 10^{-6} {~A}=6.9 \mu {A}$
The conduction current is equal to the displacement even in case of $ac$.
Let ${I_{0}}$ be the peak value (amplitude) of the conduction current $(I)$ Clearly, ${I_{{rms}}=\dfrac{I_{0}}{\sqrt{2}}}$
Or ${\quad I_{0}=I_{\text {rms }} \times \sqrt{2}=(6.9 \mu A) \times 1.414=9.76 \mu {A}}$
Obviously, peak value (amplitude) of ${I_{d}}$ is also the same, $i.e.,$ ${9.76 \mu {A}}$.
Consider a circular path of radius ${3.0 {~cm}(=0.03 {~m})}$ parallel to the plates of the capacitor and with its centre ${(O)}$ on the axis of the plates.
According to the Ampere's law,
$\oint {\vec B} \cdot \overrightarrow {d\ell } = {\mu _0}\left( {I + {I_d}} \right){\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Now ${\oint \vec{B} \cdot \overrightarrow{d \ell}=B \times}$ circumference of the circle of radius $0.03\,m = B(2\pi \times 0.03)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
As there is no conduction current in the plates,
$I = 0{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
Further, ${I_{d}=\dfrac{9.76(\mu {A})}{\pi(0.06)^{2}} \times \pi(0.03)^{2}}$
$ = 2.44\,\mu A = 2.44 \times {10^{ - 6}}\,A\quad \quad (4)$
From Eqs. (1), (2), (3) and (4),
$B(2\pi \times 0.03) = {\mu _0}\left( {2.44 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} \right)$
Or $B = \left( {\frac{{{\mu _0}}}{{2\pi }}} \right)\frac{{2.44 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{0.03}}$
$ = \left( {2 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}} \right)\left( {81.3 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} \right)$
$ = 1.63 \times {10^{ - 11}}\,T$
subject: Physics

question: A  $27{\mkern 1mu} \,mW$  laser beam has a cross-sectional area of ${10 {~mm}^{2}}$. Find the magnitude of the maximum electric field   in this electromagnetic wave is given by [Given permittivity of space ${\varepsilon_{0}=9 \times 10^{-12}}$ $SI$ units, Speed of light ${\left.c=3 \times 10^{8} {~m} / {s}\right]}$ :
explanation:
Intensity of $EM$ wave is given by
$I = \frac{{{\rm{ Power }}}}{{{\rm{ Area }}}} = \frac{1}{2}{\varepsilon _0}E_0^2C = \frac{{27 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{10 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}$
$ = \frac{1}{2} \times 9 \times {10^{ - 12}} \times {E^2} \times 3 \times {10^8}$
$E = \sqrt 2 \times {10^3}kV/m = 1.4\,kV/m$
subject: Physics

question: Sea water at frequency $v=4 \times 10^{8} {~Hz}$ has permittivity  $\varepsilon  \approx 80\,{\varepsilon _0},$  permeability $\mu \approx \mu_{0}$ and resistivity $\rho=0.25\, \Omega-{m}$. Imagine a parallel plate capacitor immersed in sea water and driven by an alternating voltage source ${V}({t})={V}_{0} \sin (2 \,\pi {vt}$ ). What is the ratio of amplitude of the conduction current density to the displacement current density?
explanation:
$V(t) = {V_0}\sin 2\pi vt$
Let distance between the plates $={d}$.
Electric field $=\dfrac{{V}({t})}{{d}}=\dfrac{{V}_{0}}{{~d}} \sin 2 \pi {vt}$
Conducting current density
$J_{c}=\dfrac{E}{\rho}=\dfrac{V_{0}}{\rho d} \,\sin 2 \pi v t=J_{0 c} \sin 2 \pi v t$
where, ${J}_{0 {c}}$ is maximum conduction current density.
Displacement current density,
${J}_{{d}}=\varepsilon \dfrac{{d}}{{dt}}\left[\dfrac{{V}_{0}}{{~d}} \sin (2 \pi {vt})\right]=\dfrac{2 \pi v \varepsilon}{{d}} {V}_{0} \cos (2 \pi v {t})$
$J_{d}=J_{0_{d}} \cos (2 \pi v t)$
$\dfrac{J_{0_{d}}}{J_{0_{c}}}=\dfrac{\dfrac{2 \pi \vee \varepsilon V_{0}}{d}}{\dfrac{V_{0}}{\rho d}}$
$=2 \pi v \varepsilon \rho$
$=2 \pi \times 4 \times 10^{8} \times 80 \,\varepsilon_{0} \times 0.25$
$ = \frac{{4 \times {{10}^9}}}{{9 \times {{10}^9}}} = \frac{4}{9}\quad \left( {\frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} = 9 \times {{10}^9}N{m^2}/{C^2}} \right)$
$=0.44$
subject: Physics

question:
The electric field part of an electromagnetic wave in vacuum is,
${E}=3.1 \cos \left[(1.8) {y}+\left(5.4 \times 10^{8}\right) {t}\right] \hat{{i}} \dfrac{{N}}{{C}}$
What is the wavelength of this part of electromagnetic wave?
explanation:
Comparing the given equation with,
$E = {E_0}\cos \left( {ky + \omega t} \right)$
We get, ${k}=1.8 \,{rad}\, {m}^{-1}$,
Now, $\lambda = \frac{{2\pi }}{k} = \frac{{2 \times 22}}{{1.8 \times 7}} = 3.49\,m$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Wave Equation and its Properties of EM Waves

question: In an electromagnetic wave, the average energy density associated with magnetic field is
subject: Physics

question: The amplitude of an electromagnetic wave in vacuum is doubled with no other changes made to the wave. As a result of this doubling of the amplitude, which of the following statement is correct?
explanation:
The speed of an electromagnetic wave in vacuum is
$c = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{\mu _0}{\varepsilon _0}} }} = 3 \times {10^8}\,m\,{s^{ - 1}}$
It is independent of amplitude, frequency and wavelength of the electromagnetic wave.

options.text: The frequency of the wave changes only.
The wavelength of the wave changes only.
The speed of wave propagation changes
None of the above is correct.

subject: Physics

question: Beyond which frequency, the ionosphere bends any incident electromagnetic radiation but do not reflect it back towards the earth?
explanation: The ionosphere can reflect electromagnetic waves of frequency less than  $40\,MHz$ but do not reflect electromagnetic waves of frequency more than $40MHz$.
subject: Physics

question: A plane electromagnetic wave is propagating along the $z$ direction. If the electric field component of this wave is in the direction $(\hat{i}+\hat{j})$, then which of the following is the direction of the magnetic field component?
explanation:
The magnetic field component is perpendicular to the direction of propagation and the direction of electric field.
Using vector algebra, $\vec{E} \times \vec{B}$ should be in $z$-direction
$\therefore \quad(\hat{i}+\hat{j}) \times(-\hat{i}+\hat{j})=2 \hat{k}$
subject: Physics

question: A plane electromagnetic wave travels in free space along $x$-axis. At a particular point in space, the electric field along $y$-axis is $9.3\,V\,{m^{ - 1}}$. The magnetic induction ($B$) along $z$-axis is
explanation:
$c = \frac{E}{B}$
$ \Rightarrow B = \frac{E}{c} = \frac{{9.3}}{{3 \times {{10}^8}}} = 3.1 \times {10^{ - 8}}\,T$
subject: Physics

question: A plane electromagnetic wave is incident on a material surface. If the wave delivers momentum $p$ and energy $E$, then
explanation:
An electromagnetic wave has both energy and momentum.
$\therefore p \ne 0,E \ne 0$
subject: Physics

question: Which of the following is not a property of light?
explanation: Light waves do not require a material medium for their propagation. Also, they travel through vacuum. A metallic plate kept in the path of light rays gets heated up, showing that light involves transportation of energy. Also, speed of light is $3 \times {10^8}\,m{s^{ - 1}},$ which is finite.

options.text: It requires a material medium for propagation.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Wave Equation and its Properties of EM Waves

question:
If ${v_\gamma },{v_X}$ and ${v_M}$ are the speeds of $\gamma $-rays, $X$-rays
and microwaves respectively in vacuum, then
explanation:
$\gamma $-rays, $X$-rays and microwaves
respectively are electromagnetic
waves. They travel with the speed of
light in vacuum.
Hence, $\,\,\,\,\,{v_\gamma } = {v_X} = {v_M}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Wave Equation and its Properties of EM Waves

question:
The average magnetic energy density of an
electromagnetic wave of wave length $\lambda $ travelling in free space is given by
explanation:
Energy density of an electromagnetic wave,
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,U = \frac{1}{2}{\varepsilon _0}{E^2} + \frac{1}{2}\frac{{{B^2}}}{{{\mu _0}}}$
So, the magnetic energy density is
$\frac{{{B^2}}}{{2{\mu _0}}}.$
subject: Physics

question:
In a free space, electron is placed in the path of a
plane electromagnetic wave, it will start moving along
explanation:
The electron placed in the path of
electromagnetic wave will experience
force due to electric field vector and
not due to magnetic field vector.
Hence, it will move along the electric field.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Wave Equation and its Properties of EM Waves

question:
The electric field of an electromagnetic wave in free space is given by:
${E=10 \cos \left(10^{7} t+k x\right) V / m}$
where ${t}$ and ${x}$ are in seconds and metres, respectively. It can be inferred that
(a) the wavelength ${\lambda}$ is $188.4\,m$
(b) the wave number ${k}$ is $0.33\,rad/m$
(c) the wave amplitude is $10\,V/m$
(d) the wave is propagating along ${+x}$-direction.
Which one of the following pairs of statements is correct?
explanation:
${A=10 {~V} / {m} ; \omega=10^{7} \Rightarrow \omega=2 \pi f=\dfrac{2 \pi c}{\lambda}}$
${\Rightarrow \lambda=\dfrac{2 \pi c}{\omega}=188.4 {~m}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Wave Equation and its Properties of EM Waves

question: A plane electromagnetic wave in a non-magnetic dielectric medium is given by ${\vec{E}=\vec{E}_{0} \sin \left(4 \times 10^{-7} x-50 t\right)}$ with distance being in meter and time in seconds. The refractive index of medium is:
subject: Physics
subtopic: Wave Equation and its Properties of EM Waves

question: The property which is not of an electromagnetic wave travelling in free space is that:
explanation: The EM waves are originated from accelerating charge. The charge moving with uniform velocity produces steady state magnetic field. Correct option is (4).

options.text: The energy density in electric field is equal to energy density in magnetic field
They travel with a speed equal to ${\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}}}}$
They originate from charges moving with uniform speed

subject: Physics
subtopic: Wave Equation and its Properties of EM Waves

question: Electromagnetic waves are incident normally on a perfectly reflecting surface having surface area A . If ${I}$ is the intensity of the incident electromagnetic radiation and ${c}$ is the speed of light in vacuum, the force exerted by the electromagnetic wave on the reflecting surface is
explanation: For perfectly reflecting surface, force is given by ${F=\dfrac{2 I A}{c}}$
subject: Physics

question:
If a source of electromagnetic radiation having power $15\,kW$ produces $10^{16}$ photons per second, the radiation belongs to a part of spectrum is
(Take Planck constant $h = 6 \times {10^{ - 34}}\,Js\,)$
explanation:
Power, $P=15 {~kW}$
$P=\dfrac{E}{t}=\dfrac{n h c}{\lambda}$
$\lambda=\dfrac{n h c}{P}=\dfrac{10^{16} \times 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{15 \times 10^{3}}$
$\lambda=1.32 \times 10^{-13} {~m}$
$\lambda < 10^{-12}$ lies in range of Gamma rays
subject: Physics

question: The energy of an electromagnetic wave contained in a small volume oscillates with
explanation:
Let the electric field is $E=E_{0} \sin (\omega t-k x)$
The energy density due to electric field is
${U_E} = \frac{1}{2}{\varepsilon _0}{E^2} = \frac{1}{2}{\varepsilon _0}E_0^2{\sin ^2}(\omega t - kx)$
$U_{E}=\dfrac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}[1-\cos 2(\omega t-k x)]$
$\left[\cos 2 \theta=1-2 \sin ^{2} \theta\right]$
Again, $B=B_{0} \sin (\omega t-k x)$.
Thus, $U_{B}=\dfrac{1}{4} \dfrac{B_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}}[1-\cos 2(\omega t-k x)]$
So, the frequency of $U_{E}$ and $U_{B}$ is double the frequency of electromagnetic wave.

options.text: double the frequency of the wave
half the frequency of the wave

subject: Physics

question: The ratio of average electric energy density and total average energy density of electromagnetic wave is
explanation:
Average electric energy density = average magnetic energy density $=\dfrac{1}{2}$ total average energy density
So, the ratio of,
$\dfrac{\text { average electric energy density }}{\text { total average energy density }}=\dfrac{1}{2}$
subject: Physics

question:
The electric field in an electromagnetic wave is given as $\vec{E}=20 \sin \omega\left(t-\dfrac{x}{c}\right) \hat{j} N C^{-1}$.
Where $\omega$ and $c$ are angular frequency and velocity of electromagnetic wave respectively. The energy contained in a volume of $5 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,{m^3}$ will be
(Given ${\varepsilon _0} = 8.85 \times {10^{ - 12}}{C^2}/N{m^2}$)
explanation:
$\vec{E}=20 \sin \omega\left(t-\dfrac{x}{c}\right) \hat{j} N / C$
From the given equation, $E_{0}=20 {~N} / {C}$
Volume $=5 \times 10^{-4} {~m}^{3}$
Average energy density of an em wave
$=\dfrac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}$
Energy stored $=\left(\dfrac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}\right)($ volume $)$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times(20)^{2} \times\left(5 \times 10^{-4}\right) J$
$=8.85 \times 10^{-13} {~J}$
subject: Physics

question: The amplitude of magnetic field in an electromagnetic wave propagating along $y$-axis is $6.0 \times {10^{ - 7}}\,T$. The maximum value of electric field in the electromagnetic wave is
explanation:
For a electromagnetic wave, amplitude of electric and magnetic field are related by,
$\dfrac{E_{m}}{B_{m}}=c$
$\therefore E_{m}=B_{m} \times C$
$=6 \times 10^{-7} \times 3 \times 10^{8}=18 \times 10 ; E=180 {Vm}^{-1}$
subject: Physics

question: The energy density associated with electric field $\vec{E}$ and magnetic field $\vec{B}$ of an electromagnetic wave in free space is given by $\left(\varepsilon_{0}\right.$ - permittivity of free space, $\mu_{0}$ - permeability of free space)
explanation: In free space, the energy density of a static field $E$ is  ${u_E} = \frac{1}{2}{\varepsilon _0}{E^2},$  and the energy density of magnetic field is  ${u_B} = \frac{1}{{2{\mu _0}}}{B^2}$
subject: Physics

question: A point source of $100\,W$ emits light with $5 \%$ efficiency. At a distance of $5\,m$ from the source, the intensity produced by the electric field component is
explanation:
Effective power emitted by the source
$P_{e}=100 \times \dfrac{5}{100}$ Watt $=5$ Watt.
Intensity at a distance of $5 {~m}$ form the source is given by
$I = \frac{{{P_e}}}{{4\pi {r^2}}} = \frac{5}{{4\pi {{(5)}^2}}}W/{m^2} = \frac{1}{{20\pi }}.$
$50 \%$ of this energy is carried by electric field and $50 \%$ is carried by magnetic field.
$\therefore$ Intensity produced by electric field component is given by ${I_E} = \frac{1}{2},{I_e} = \frac{1}{{40\pi }}W/{m^2}.$
subject: Physics

question: A small object at rest, absorbs a light pulse of power $20\,mW$ and duration $300\,ns$. Assuming speed of light as $3 \times {10^8}\,m/s$, the momentum of the object becomes equal to
explanation:
$U$ ( energy $)=$ Power $\times$ time
$ = \left( {20 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right) \times \left( {300 \times {{10}^{ - 9}}\,s} \right)$
$ = 6 \times {10^{ - 9}}{\rm{ joules}}$
Momentum gained by the object is,
$p=\dfrac{U}{c}=\dfrac{6 \times 10^{-9}}{3 \times 10^{8}}$$=2 \times 10^{-17} {~kg} {~ms}^{-1}$
subject: Physics

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options given below:
Electromagnetic waves are not deflected by electric and magnetic field.
The amplitude of electric field and the magnetic field in electromagnetic waves are related to each other as $E_{0}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu_{0}}{\varepsilon_{0}}} B_{0}$.
explanation:
Statement A: True, as the electromagnetic waves are stream of photons which are not charged particles, so they are not deflected by electric and magnetic fields.
Statement B : False, as $E_{0}=\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}}} \cdot B_{0}$

options.text: Both Statement A and  Statement B are incorrect.
Both Statement A and  Statement B are correct.
Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is in correct but Statement B is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Wave Equation and its Properties of EM Waves

question:
Which of the following are true?
(A) Speed of light in vacuum is dependent on the direction of propagation.
(B) Speed of light in a medium is independent of the wavelength of light.
(C) The speed of light is independent of the motion of the source.
(D) The speed of light in a medium is independent of intensity.
Choose the connect answer from the options given below.
explanation:
C and D only.The speed of light is always independent of the motion of source as $c=1 / \sqrt{\mu_{0} \in_{0}}$.
The speed of light in a medium is independent of intensity as $v=c / n$, where $n$ is the refractive index of the medium.

question:
Match Column I with Column II. Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
A. Gauss's law in Electrostatics
B. Faraday's law
C. Gauss's law nagnetism
D. Ampere Maxwell Law
P. $\oint {\vec E.{\mkern 1mu} \,d\vec l} = - \frac{{d{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\phi _B}}}{{dt}}$
Q. $\oint {\vec B.\,d\vec A} = 0$
R. $\oint {\vec B.\,d\vec l = } {\mu _0}{i_c} + {\mu _0}{\varepsilon _0}\frac{{d{\phi _E}}}{{dt}}$
S. $\oint {\vec E.\,d\vec s = \frac{{{q_{net}}}}{{{{\rm{\varepsilon }}_0}}}} $
explanation:
Gauss's law in electrostatics
$\Rightarrow \phi=\oint \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{s}=\dfrac{q_{\text {net }}}{\varepsilon_{0}}$
Faraday's law $\Rightarrow \oint \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{l}=-\dfrac{d \phi_{B}}{d t}$
Gauss's law in magnetism, $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{A}=0$, as the net magnetic pole strength enclosed by the Gaussion surface is zero.
Ampere's Maxwell law,
$\oint {\vec B} \cdot d\vec l = {\mu _0}{i_c} + {\mu _0}{\varepsilon _0}\frac{{d{\phi _E}}}{{dt}}$
${\rm{A}} - {\rm{S}},{\rm{B}} - {\rm{P}},{\rm{C}} - {\rm{Q}},{\rm{D}} - {\rm{R}}$
subject: Physics

question: An electromagnetic wave is transporting energy in the negative $z$ direction. At a certain point and certain time the direction of electric field of the wave is along positive $y$ direction. What will be the direction of the magnetic field of the wave at that point and instant?
explanation:
Direction of wave propagation, $\vec{v}=v(-\hat{k})$ direction of electric field, $\vec{E}=E \hat{j}$
As $\vec{v}=\vec{E} \times \vec{B}$
So, $\vec v\,( - \hat k) = \vec E\,\hat j \times \vec B\,\hat i$
So, direction of $\vec{B}$ is along $+x$ axis.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Wave Equation and its Properties of EM Waves

question: In an electromagnetic wave, at an instant and at a particular position, the electric field is along the negative $z$-axis and magnetic field is along the positive $x$-axis. Then the direction of propagation of electromagnetic wave is
explanation:
For an electromagnetic wave, the direction of propagation is given by, the direction of the vector $\vec{E} \times \vec{B}$.
$e=E \times B$
As, $\vec{E}$ is in $-z$ direction and $\vec{B}$ is in $+x$ direction. So, $\vec{E} \times \vec{B}=(-E \hat{K}) \times(B \hat{i})$
$=E B(-\hat{j})$
Thus, it is evident that electromagnetic wave will propagate in negative $y$-axis.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Wave Equation and its Properties of EM Waves

question: Which of the following Maxwell's equation is valid for time varying conditions but not valid for static conditions?
explanation:
For time varying conditions, the Maxwell equation is given by
$\oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=-\dfrac{\partial \phi_{B}}{\partial t}=$ Induced $emf$ in a loop and $\vec{E}$ is the induced non-electrostatic electric field produced by variable magnetic field.
subject: Physics

question: A plane electromagnetic wave of frequency $20\,MHz$ propagates in free space along positive $x$-direction. At a particular space and time, $\vec{E}=6.6 \hat{j} V / m$. What is $\vec{B}$ at this point?
explanation:
The magnitude of $B$ is,
$B=\dfrac{E}{c}=\dfrac{6.6}{3 \times 10^{8}}=2.2 \times 10^{-8} {~T}$
Direction of $\vec{B}$ is along $+\hat{z}$ direction.
So, $\vec{B}=2.2 \times 10^{-8} \hat{k} T$
subject: Physics

question:
The electric field and magnetic field components of an electromagnetic wave going through vacuum is described by
$E_{x}=E_{0} \sin (k z-\omega t) ; B_{y}=B_{0} \sin (k z-\omega t)$
then the correct relation between $E_{0}$ and $B_{0}$ is given by
explanation:
Given, $E_{x}=E_{0} \sin (k z-\omega t)$$B_{x}=B_{0} \sin (k z-\omega t)$
The magnitude of $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{B}$ are related as, $\dfrac{E}{B}=c \Rightarrow \dfrac{E_{0}}{B_{0}}=c=$ velocity of E.M wave,
$c=f \lambda=\left(\dfrac{\omega}{2 \pi}\right)\left(\dfrac{2 \pi}{k}\right)=\dfrac{\omega}{k}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{E_{0}}{B_{0}}=\dfrac{\omega}{k} \Rightarrow k E_{0}=\omega B_{0}$
So, correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: If $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{K}$ represent electric field and propagation vectors of the EM waves in vacuum, then magnetic field vector is given by ($\omega$ - angular frequency)
explanation:
Here, the magnetic field vector will be along $\hat{K} \times \vec{E}$, Where $\hat{K}$ is the unit vector along the direction of propagation.
Also, $|\vec{B}|=\dfrac{|\vec{E}|}{c} \Rightarrow \vec{B}=\left(\dfrac{E}{c}\right)
\dfrac{\hat{K} \times \vec{E}}{E}$
$=\dfrac{\hat{K} \times \vec{E}}{c}=\dfrac{\hat{K} \times \vec{E}}{\omega / K} \quad\left[\because c=\dfrac{\omega}{K}\right]$
$=\dfrac{K \hat{K} \times \vec{E}}{\omega}=\dfrac{\vec{K} \times \vec{E}}{\omega} \quad[\because K \hat{K}=\vec{K}]$
So option (1) is correct.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Wave Equation and its Properties of EM Waves

question: A plane electromagnetic wave propagating in $x$-direction is described by ${E_y} = \left( {200\,V{\mkern 1mu} {m^{ - 1}}} \right)\sin \left[ {1.5 \times {{10}^7}t - 0.05x} \right];$ Theintensity of the wave is :<br>(Use ${ \in _0} = 8.85 \times {10^{ - 12}}{C^2}\,{N^{ - 1}}\,{m^{ - 2}}$)
explanation:
Average intensity of $E M$ wave in terms of Electric field is given by
$I=\dfrac{1}{2} c \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}$
where $c = $Speed of $EM$ wave in vaccum
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{E_0} = $Amplitude of electric field
$ = \frac{1}{2} \times 3 \times {10^8} \times 8.85 \times {10^{ - 12}} \times {(200)^2}$
$Z = 53.1\,W{\rm{/}}{m^2}$
subject: Physics

question: An object is placed in a medium of redfractive index 3. An electromagnetic wave of intensity $6 \times {10^8}\,W{\rm{/}}{m^2}$ falls normally on the object and it is absorbed completely. The radiation pressure on the object would be (speed of light in free space $ = 3 \times {10^8}\,m{\rm{/}}s$):
explanation:
$P=\dfrac{\Delta P_{\text {momentum }}}{\Delta t \times A}=$ pressure
$P=\dfrac{E}{V} \times \dfrac{1}{A \times \Delta t}=\dfrac{l}{V} \quad\left[E=m v^{2}\right]$
$P=\dfrac{6 \times 10^{6}}{c / \mu} \quad\left[V=\dfrac{c}{\mu}\right]$
$P=\dfrac{6 \times 10^{6}}{3 \times 10^{8}} \times 3$
$ = 6\,N{\rm{/}}{m^2}$
subject: Physics

question: The speed of electromagnetic wave in a medium of dielectric constant 2.25 and relative permeability 4 is:
explanation:
The speed of electromagnetic wave in a medium is-
$v = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {\mu \varepsilon } }} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{\mu _0}{\varepsilon _0}{\mu _r}{\varepsilon _r}} }} = \frac{c}{{\sqrt {{\mu _r}{\varepsilon _r}} }}$
$v = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{\sqrt {4 \times 2.25} }} = 1 \times {10^8}\,m{\rm{/}}s.$

question: A plane electromagnetic wave of frequency $25 \mathrm{MHz}$ travel in free space along the $x$ direction. At a particular point in space and time, the electric vector is $\vec{E}=6.3 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m} \hat{j}$. What is the $\vec{B}$ at this point?
explanation:
As we know
$B = \frac{E}{C} = \frac{{6.3}}{{3 \times {{10}^8}}} = 2.1 \times {10^{ - 8}}\,T$
As $\vec{E}$ is along $y$-direction and wave is travelling along $x$-direction, therefore $\vec{B}$ is along $z$ direction because in propagation of $e m$ wave $E\,\,\& \,\,B$ fields are $\perp$ to each other.
$\vec B = 2.1 \times {10^{ - 8}}\hat k\,T$

question:

$B_{z}=5 \times 10^{-9} T \sin \omega(t-Y / C) .$
What is the maximum electric and magnetic forces on a charge $i.e$., $\alpha$-particle moving along $y$-axis with a speed of $3 \times {10^8}\,m{\rm{/}}s$. (charge on electron $ = 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}}C$).
explanation:
$q=$ Charge on $\alpha$-particle $(q)=2 \times e$.
Maximum force on $\alpha$-particle due to electric field
$F_{E}=q \times E_{0} .$
$=3.2 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.5$
${F_E} = 4.80 \times {10^{ - 19}}\,N$
Maximum force on $\alpha$ - particle due to magnetic field $F_{B}=q v B_{0}$
$ = 3.2 \times {10^{ - 19}} \times 3 \times {10^8} \times {10^{ - 9}} \times 5$
${F_B} = 4.80 \times {10^{ - 19}}\,N.$

question:
The electric field part of an electromagnetic wave in vacuum is $E=3.1 N C^{-1} \cos [(1.8 \mathrm{rad} /$ $\left.\left.m^{-1}\right) y+\left(5.4 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}^{-1}\right) t\right] i$.
The wavelength of this part of electromagnetic wave is:
explanation:
$E=3.1 \cos \left[\left(1.8 y+5.4 \times 10^{8}\right] i N / C\right.$
Standard equation:
$\begin{aligned}& E=E_{0} \cos [k x-\omega t] N / C \\& \therefore k=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda}=1.8 \\& \lambda=\dfrac{2 \pi}{1.8}=3.49 \simeq 3.5 .\end{aligned}$

explanation:

question: The pressure exerted by an electromagnetic wave of intensity, $I\left( {W{m^{ - 2}}} \right)$ on a non-reflecting surface is
explanation:
When a surface intercepts electromagnetic radiation, a force and a pressure are exerted on the surface. As the surface is non-reflecting, so it is completely absorbed and in such case the
force is, $F=\dfrac{I A}{c}$
The radiation pressure is the force per unit area,
$p=\dfrac{F}{A}=\dfrac{I}{c}$
where, $c=$ speed of light

question: A light beam travelling in the $$x$$-direction is described by the electric field  ${E_y} = (300\,V/m)\sin \omega \,(t - x/c)$. An electron is constrained to move along the $$y$$-direction with a speed of $2.0 \times {10^7}\,m/s$. Find the maximum magnetic force on the electron.

question: Predict the correct relation between average energy density in electric field ${\mu _E}$ magnetic field $$u_{B}$$
explanation:
Average energy density of $$E$$
$$u_{E}=\dfrac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}$$
Average energy density of $B$
$$\begin{gathered}u_{B}=\dfrac{1}{4} \dfrac{B_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}} \\\because \mathrm{c}=\dfrac{\mathrm{E}_{0}}{\mathrm{~B}_{0}} \mathrm{So}, \\u_{E}=\dfrac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0}\left(c B_{0}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} \dfrac{1}{\left(\mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}\right)} \times B_{0}^{2}=\dfrac{1}{4} \dfrac{\mathrm{B}_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}} \\u_{E}=u_{B}\end{gathered}$$
subject: Physics

question: Light with an energy flux of $18\,W/c{m^2}$ falls on a non- reflecting suface at normal incidence. If the surface has an area of $20\,c{m^2}$, find the average force exerted on the suface during a 30 minute time span.
explanation:
The total energy falling on the surface is
$U = \left( {18\,W/c{m^2}} \right) \times 20\,c{m^2} \times (30 \times 60)\infty $
$ = 6.48 \times {10^5}\,J$
Therefore, the total momentum delivered (for complete absorption) is
$p = \frac{U}{c} = \frac{{6.48 \times {{10}^5}\,J}}{{3 \times {{10}^8}\,m/s}} = 2.16 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,kg/ms$
The average force exerted on the surface is
$F = \frac{p}{t} = \frac{{2.16 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{0.18 \times {{10}^4}}} = 1.2 \times {10^{ - 6}}\,N$
subject: Physics

question: A long straight wire of resistance $R$, radius ' $$a$$ ' and length ' $$l$$ ' carries a constant current ' $$I$$ '. The poynting vector for the wire will be-
explanation:
If $$V$$ is the potential difference across the wire, then $$\mathrm{E}=\dfrac{\mathrm{V}}{\ell}=\dfrac{\mathrm{IR}}{\ell}$$ and magnetic field at the surface of wire $$B=\dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi a}$$. Hence pointing vector, directed radially inwards, is given by
$$S=\dfrac{E B}{\mu_{0}}=\dfrac{I R}{\mu_{0} \ell} \times \dfrac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi a}=\dfrac{I^{2} R}{2 \pi a l}$$
Hence the correct answer will be (4).
subject: Physics

question: A plane electromagnetic wave propagating in the $$x$$ direction has a wavelength of $5.0\,mm$. The electric field is in the y-direction and its maximum magnitude is $30\,V/m$. Find equation for the electric field as a function of $$x$$ and $t.$
explanation:
The equation for the electric field is
$$E=E_{0} \sin \omega\left(t-\dfrac{x}{c}\right)$$
We have, $$\omega=2 \pi v=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda} c$$
Thus, $$E=E_{0} \sin \left[\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda}(c t-x)\right]$$
$ = (30\,V/m)\sin \left[ {\frac{{2\pi }}{{5\,mm}}(ct - x)} \right]$
$ = 30\,V/m\sin \left[ {12\pi \times {{10}^{10}}t - 400\pi x} \right]$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Wave Equation and its Properties of EM Waves

$ = {p_{{\rm{rad }}}} \times c4\pi {r^2}$
$ = 9 \times {10^{ - 6}} \times 3 \times {10^8} \times 12.56 \times 25$
$ = 8.5 \times {10^5}\,W$

question: An electromagnetic wave travels in vacuum alongz-direction $$E=\left(E_{1} \hat{i}+E_{2} \hat{j}\right) \cos (k z-\omega t)$$. Choose the correct options from the following:
explanation:
Here, in electromagnetic wave, the electric field vector is given as,
$$\bar{E}=\left(E_{1} \hat{i}+E_{2} \hat{j}\right) \cos (k z-\omega t)$$
In electromagnetic wave, the associated magnetic field vector, $$B_{o}=\dfrac{E_{o}}{c}$$
where $$E_{0}=\sqrt{E_{1}^{2}+E_{2}^{2}}, \vec{B}=\dfrac{E_{2} \hat{i}+E_{1} \hat{j}}{\mathrm{c}} \cos (k z-\omega t)$$
As $$\bar{E} \& \bar{B}$$ are perpendicular to each other.
$$\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}_{2}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}=0$$
So option (3) is correct.

options.text:
The associated magnetic field is given as
$$B=\dfrac{1}{c}\left(E_{1} \hat{i}-E_{2} \hat{j}\right) \cos (k z-\omega t)$$

The associated magnetic field is given as
$B{\rm{ }} = \frac{1}{c}\left( {{E_1}\hat i - {E_2}\hat j} \right)\cos (kz - \omega t){\rm{ }}$

subject: Physics

question: Light with an energy flux of $20\,W/c{m^2}$ falls on a non-reflecting surface at normal incidence. If the surface has an area of $30\,c{m^2}$, the total momentum delivered (for complete absorption) during $30\,\min $ is
explanation:
$F = {P_{{\rm{rad }}}} \times {\rm{Area}}$
$ = \frac{I}{c} \times {\rm{ Area }}$
$ = \frac{{20}}{{3 \times {{10}^8}}} \times 30 = 2 \times {10^{ - 6}}\,N$
$$=$$ Change in momentum in $1\,\sec $
$$\therefore$$ Momentum delivered to the surface in $30\,\min $
$ = 2 \times {10^{ - 6}} \times 30 \times 60 = 36 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,kg\,m\,{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics

question: In an electromagnetic wave in free space the root mean square value of the electric field is ${E_{rms}} = 6\,V/m$. The peak value of the magnetic field is :-

question: For a plane electromagnetic wave propagating in $x$-direction, which one of the following combination gives the correct possible direction for electric field ($E$) and magnetic field ($B$) respectively?
explanation:
$\overrightarrow E \times \overrightarrow B $ should be along x axis
(1) $$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 0 & -1 & 1 \\ 0 & -1 & -1\end{array}\right|=2 i$$
(2) $$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & -1 & -1\end{array}\right|=0$$
(3) $$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 0 & -1 & 1 \\ 0 & -1 & 1\end{array}\right|=0$$
(4) $$\left|\begin{array}{lll}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 1\end{array}\right|=0$$
Option (1) is correct.
subject: Physics

question:
The elecric field part of an electromagnetic wave in a medium is represented by $$E_{X}=0$$;
${E_y} = 2.5\frac{N}{C}\cos \left[ {\left( {2\pi \times {{10}^6}\frac{{rad}}{m}} \right)t} \right.$
$\left. { - \left( {\pi \times {{10}^{ - 2}}\frac{{rad}}{s}} \right)x} \right];\quad {E_z} = 0.{\rm{ The wave is }}$
explanation:
Comparing the given equation
$$E_{y}=2.5 \dfrac{N}{C} \cos \left[\left(2 \pi \times 10^{6} \dfrac{\mathrm{rad}}{\mathrm{m}}\right) t-\left(\pi \times 10^{-2} \dfrac{\mathrm{rad}}{\mathrm{sec}}\right) x\right]$$
With the standard equation
$$E_{y}=E_{0} \cos (\omega t-k x)$$
we get
$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times {10^6}$
$\therefore \quad f = {10^6}\,Hz$
Moreover, we know that
$$\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda}=k=\pi \times 10^{-2} m^{-1} \Rightarrow \lambda=200 m$$
As the direction of field of electromagnetic wave is in $y$-direction so, the wave is moving along positive $x$-direction with frequency ${10^6}HZ$ and wavelength $200\,m$.

options.text: Moving along-$x$-direction with frequency ${10^6}HZ$ and wavelength $100\,m$
Moving along $$y$$-direction with frequency $2\pi  \times {10^6}HZ$ and wavelength $200\,m$
Moving along $$x$$-direction with frequency ${10^6}HZ$ and wavelength $200\,m$
Moving along $$x$$-direction with frequency ${10^6}HZ$ and wavelength $300\,m$

question: A light beam of intensity $20\,W\,c{m^{ - 2}}$ is incident normally on a perfectly reflecting surface of sides $25\,cm \times 15\,cm$. The momentum imparted to the surface by the light per second is
explanation:
Given that, $I = 20\,\,W\,c{m^{ - 2}},$
Area $ = 25\,cm \times 15\,cm$
For a perfectly reflecting surface,
$\dot P = \frac{{2\dot E}}{c}$
$\dot E = I \times A = 20 \times 25 \times 15 = 7500\,W$
$\therefore \quad \dot P = \frac{{2 \times 7500}}{{3 \times {{10}^8}}} = 5 \times {10^{ - 5}}\,kg\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics

question: The electric field associated with an electromagnetic wave in vacuum is given by $\overrightarrow E  = \hat i40\cos \left( {kz - 6 \times {{10}^8}t} \right)$, where $$E, z$$ and $$t$$ are in volt $/m$, metre and second respectively. The value of wave vector $$k$$ is
explanation:
Electromagnetic wave equation is given by
$E = {E_0}\cos (kz - \omega t)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Speed of electromagnetic wave, $$v=\dfrac{\omega}{k}$$
Given, equation is
$E = \hat i40\cos \left( {kz - 6 \times {{10}^8}t} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Comparing Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$$\omega=6 \times 10^{8} \text { and } E_{0}=40 \hat{i}$$
Here, wave factor, $k = \frac{\omega }{v} = \frac{{6 \times {{10}^8}}}{{3 \times {{10}^8}}} = 2\,{m^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Wave Equation and its Properties of EM Waves

question: A plane electromagnetic wave of frequency 20 $MHz$ travels through a space along $x$ direction. If the electric field vector at a certain point in space is $6\,\,V\,{m^{ - 1}}$, what is the magnetic field vector at that point?
explanation:
The magnitude of magnetic field is
$B = \frac{E}{c} = \frac{{6V{m^{ - 1}}}}{{3 \times {{10}^8}\,m{s^{ - 1}}}} = 2 \times {10^{ - 8}}\,T$
subject: Physics

question:

question: Suppose that the electric field amplitude of electromagnetic wave is ${E_0} = 120\,N{C^{ - 1}}$ and its frequency if $f = 50MHz$. Then which of the following value is incorrectly computed?
explanation:
Magnetic field amplitude $$B_{0}=\dfrac{E_{0}}{c}=\dfrac{120}{3 \times 10^{8}}$$
$$=400 n T$$
Angular frequency $$\omega=2 \pi f=2 \pi \times 50 \times 10^{6}$$
$$=\pi \times 10^{8} \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$$
Wave length $\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{50 \times {{10}^6}}} = 6\,m$
Angular wave number $$k=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda}=\dfrac{2 \pi}{6}$$
$ \approx 2.1\,rad/m$
Hence option (3) is correct.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Wave Equation and its Properties of EM Waves

question: Given the wave function (in SI units) for a wave to be $$\psi_{(x, t)}=10^{3} \sin \pi\left(3 \times 10^{6} x-9 \times 10^{14} t\right)$$. The speed of wave is
explanation:
We have,
$$\begin{gathered}\psi_{(x, t)}=10^{3} \sin \pi\left(3 \times 10^{6} x-9 \times 10^{14} t\right) \\=10^{3} \sin 3 \times 10^{6} \pi\left(x-3 \times 10^{8} t\right)\end{gathered}$$
Comparing it with the relation,
${\psi _{(x,t)}} = a\sin \frac{{2\pi }}{\lambda }(x - ct);$
We note that,
$c = 3 \times {10^8}\,m{s^{ - 1}}$

question: A plane electromagnetic wave travelling along the $x$-direction has a wavelength of $3\,mm$. The variation in the electric field occurs in the $y$ direction with an amplitude $66\,V{m^{ - 1}}$. The equation for the electric and magnetic fields as a function of $$x$$ and $$t$$ are respectively
explanation:
Here, $\lambda = 3\,mm = 3 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,m,\,{E_0} = 66\,V{m^{ - 1}}$
$\therefore {B_0} = \frac{{{E_0}}}{c} = \frac{{66}}{{3 \times {{10}^8}}} = 2.2 \times {10^{ - 7}}\,T$
As, electromagnetic wave is propagating along $x$-axis and electric field oscillation is long $y$ direction using the relation for harmonic wave
$$\begin{aligned}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{y}} & =\mathrm{E}_{0} \cos \dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda}(\mathrm{ct}-\mathrm{x}) \\E_{y} & =E_{0} \cos \dfrac{2 \pi c}{\lambda}(t-x / c) \\E_{y} & =66 \cos \dfrac{2 \pi \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{3 \times 10^{-3}}(t-x / c) \\& =66 \cos 2 \pi \times 10^{11}(t-x / c) \\B_{z} & =B_{0} \cos \dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda}(t-x / c)\end{aligned}$$
$$=2.2 \times 10^{-7} \cos 2 \pi \times 10^{11}(t-x / c)$$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Wave Equation and its Properties of EM Waves

question: An $EM$ wave from air enters a medium. The electric fields are $$\overrightarrow{E_{1}}=E_{01} \hat{x} \cos \left[2 \pi v\left(\dfrac{z}{c}-t\right)\right]$$ in air and $$\vec{E}_{2}=E_{02} \hat{x} \cos [k(2 z-c t)]$$ in medium, where the wave number $k$ and frequency $$v$$ refer to their values in air. The medium is nonmagnetic. If $$\varepsilon_{r 1}$$ and $$\varepsilon_{r 2}$$ refer to relative permittivity of air and medium respectively, which of the following options is correct?
explanation:
Velocity of $EM$ wave is given by $$\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu \varepsilon}}$$
From the given wave eq's velocity in air $$\dfrac{\omega}{k}=c$$
velocity in medium $$\dfrac{c}{2}$$
$$\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_{0} \varepsilon_{r 1}}}}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_{r 2}}}=\dfrac{c}{\left(\dfrac{c}{2}\right)}=2 \Rightarrow \dfrac{\varepsilon_{r 1}}{\varepsilon_{r 2}}=\dfrac{1}{4}$$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Wave Equation and its Properties of EM Waves

question:
A cube of edge $$a$$ has its edges parallel to $x,\,y$ and $z$-axis of rectangular co-ordinate system. A uniform electric field $E$ is parallel to $y$-axis and a uniform magnetic field is parallel to $x$- axis. The rate at which energy flows through each face of the cube is
$$z=$$ constant
explanation: $$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}}=\dfrac{1}{\mu_{0}}[\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}]$$ gives the clue that energy flowing per second $$=\dfrac{a^{2} \mathrm{~EB}}{2 \mu_{0}}$$ in faces parallel to $xy$ plane and zero in all others.

options.text: $\frac{{{a^2}\,EB}}{{2{\mu _0}}}$ parallel to $xy$ plane face and zero in others
$\frac{{{a^2}\,EB}}{{2{\mu _0}}}$ parallel to $xy$ plane face and zero in others

subject: Physics

question: Calculate the amplitude of electric field produced by the radiation coming from a $100\,\,W$ bulb at a distance of $3\,m$. Assume that the efficiency of the bulb is $$2.5 \%$$ and it is a point source.
explanation:
The bulb, as a point source, radiates light in all directions uniformly. At a distance of $3\,cm$, the surface area of the surrounding sphere is
$A = 4\pi {r^2} = 4\pi {(3)^2} = 113\,{m^2}$
The intensity at this distance is
$I = \frac{{{\rm{ Power }}}}{{{\rm{ Area }}}} = \frac{{100\,W \times 2.5\% }}{{113\,{m^2}}}$
$ = \frac{{100\,W}}{{113\,{m^2}}} \times \frac{{2.5}}{{100}} = 0.022\,\,W/{m^2}$
$I = \frac{1}{2}{\varepsilon _o}E_o^2c \Rightarrow {E_o} = \sqrt {\frac{{2I}}{{{\varepsilon _o}c}}} {\rm{ }}$
${\rm{or }}\quad {E_o} = \sqrt {\frac{{2 \times 0.022}}{{8.85 \times {{10}^{ - 12}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}} = 4.07\,V{m^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics

question: A bank of overhead arc lamps can produce a light intensity of $2500\,W{m^{ - 2}}$ in the $25\,ft$ space stimulator facility at NASA. Find the average momentum density of a total absorbing surface.
explanation:
$$\mathrm{I}=\dfrac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} \mathrm{E}_{0}^{2} \mathrm{c}$$ and energy density $ = \frac{I}{c}$, momentum density $ = \frac{I}{{{c^2}}} = \frac{{\Delta P}}{V}$br>Where $$\Delta P$$ - change in momentum of $EM$ waves present in the volume $V$.
$ = \frac{{2500}}{{9 \times {{10}^{16}}}} = 2.78 \times {10^{ - 14}}\,kg{m^{ - 2}}\,{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics

question: ${E_0} = 100\,V{m^{ - 1}}$. Find the Poynting vector magnitude.
subject: Physics

question: An $EM$ wave radiates outwards from a dipole antenna, with $$E_{0}$$ as the amplitude of its electric field vector. The electric field $$E_{0}$$ which transports significant energy from the source falls off as
explanation:
From a diode antenna, the electromagnetic waves are radiated outwards. Intensity is directly proportional to square of amplitude $$I \alpha E_{o}^{2} \alpha \dfrac{1}{r^{2}}$$.
The amplitude of electric field vector $$\left(E_{0}\right)$$ which transports significant energy from the source falls off inversely as the distance $$(r)$$ from the antenna, i.e., $$E_{0} \propto \dfrac{1}{r}$$.
subject: Physics

question: Light with an energy flux of  $20\,\,W/c{m^2}$ falls on a non-reflecting surface at normal incidence. If the surface has an area of $30\,c{m^2},$ the total momentum delivered (for complete absorption) during $30\,\min $ is
explanation:
$$F=P_{\text {rad }} \times$$ Area
$ = \frac{I}{c} \times {\rm{ Area }}$
$ = \frac{{20}}{{3 \times {{10}^8}}} \times 30 = 2 \times {10^{ - 6}}\,N$
$$=$$ Change in momentum in $$1 \mathrm{sec}$$
$$\therefore$$ Momentum delivered to the surface in $$30 \mathrm{~min}$$
$ = 2 \times {10^{ - 6}} \times 30 \times 60 = 36 \times {10^{ - 4}}\,kg\,m\,{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics

question: A lamp emits monochromatic green light uniformly in all directions. The lamp is 3% efficient in converting electrical power to electromagnetic waves and consumes $100\,\,W$ of power. The amplitude of the electric field associated with the electromagnetic radiation at a distance of $5\,m$ from the lamp will be nearly :-
explanation:
$\frac{P}{{4\pi {r^2}}} = \frac{1}{2}{\varepsilon _o}E_o^2c\quad \left[ {\because \frac{I}{c} = \frac{1}{2}{\varepsilon _o}E_o^2} \right]E_0^2 = \frac{P}{{2\pi {r^2}{\varepsilon _0}C}}$
${E_0} = \sqrt {\frac{P}{{2\pi {r^2}{\varepsilon _0}c}}} = \sqrt {\frac{3}{{2\pi {{(5)}^2} \times 8.85 \times {{10}^{ - 12}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}} $
${E_0} = 2.68\,{\rm{volt/metre}}$
subject: Physics

question:
An electromagnetic wave of frequency $$1 \times 10^{14}$$ hertz is propagating along $z$ - axis. The amplitude of electric field is $4\,\,V/m$.
If ${\varepsilon _0} = 8.8 \times {10^{ - 12}}{C^2}/N - {m^2}$, then average energy density of electric field will be :-
explanation:
Only due to electric field, ave. energy density
$ = \frac{1}{4}{\varepsilon _o}E_o^2 = \frac{1}{4} \times 8.8 \times {10^{ - 12}} \times 16 = 35.2 \times {10^{ - 12}}\left( {\frac{J}{{{m^3}}}} \right)$
subject: Physics

question: For plane electromagnetic waves propagating in the $z$-direction, which one of the following combination gives the correct possible direction for $\overrightarrow E $ and $\overrightarrow B $ field respectively?
explanation:
As we know, $$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}=0 \quad \because[\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \perp \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}]$$ and $$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}$$ should be along $Z$-direction
As $$(-2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}) \times(-3 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j})=5 \hat{k}$$
Hence option (2) is the correct option.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Wave Equation and its Properties of EM Waves

question: The electric field component of a monochromatic radiation is given by $$\vec{E}=2 E_{0} \hat{i} \cos k z \cos \omega t$$. Its magnetic field $$\vec{B}$$ is then given by :
explanation:
$$\dfrac{d E}{d z}=-\dfrac{d B}{d t}$$
$$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{d E}{d z}=-2 E_{0} K \sin k z \cos \omega t=-\dfrac{d B}{d t} \\& d B=+2 E_{0} K \sin k z \cos \omega t d t\end{aligned}$$
$B = + 2{E_0}K\sin kz\int {\cos } \omega tdt$
$ = + 2{E_0}\frac{k}{\omega }\sin kz\sin \omega t$
$\frac{{{E_0}}}{{{B_0}}} = \frac{\omega }{k} = c$
$B = \frac{{2{E_0}}}{c}\widehat j\sin kz\sin \omega t$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Wave Equation and its Properties of EM Waves

question: Magnetic field in plane electromagnetic wave is given by $$\vec{B}=B_{0} \sin (k x+\omega t) \hat{j} T$$. Expression for corresponding electric field will be
explanation:
$$c=\dfrac{E_{0}}{B_{o}} \Rightarrow E=c B_{0}=c B_{0}$$
phase difference between two fields is zero.
$$E=c B_{0} \sin (k x+\omega t) \hat{k}$$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Wave Equation and its Properties of EM Waves

question:
A laser light of wavelength $660\,\,nm$ is used to weld Retina detachment. If a laser pulse of width $60\,ms$ and power $0.5\,kW$ is used the approximate number of photons in the pulse are -
[Take planck's constant $$h=6.62 \times 10^{-34} J_{S}$$ ]
explanation:
$$P=\dfrac{n E}{t}=\dfrac{n h c}{\lambda t} \Rightarrow n=\dfrac{p \lambda t}{h c}$$
$$\begin{aligned}& =0.5 \times 10^{3} \times \dfrac{660 \times 10^{-9} \times 60 \times 10^{-3}}{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}} \\& =100 \times 10^{18}=10^{20}\end{aligned}$$
subject: Physics

question: A monochromatic beam of light has a frequency $v = \frac{3}{{2\pi }} \times {10^{12}}\,Hz$ and is propagating along the direction $$\dfrac{\hat{i}+\hat{j}}{\sqrt{2}}$$. It is polarized along the $$\hat{k}$$ direction. The acceptable form for the magnetic field is:
explanation:
$$\widehat{E} \times \widehat{B}$$ should give direction of wave propagation
$$\Rightarrow \widehat{K} \times \widehat{B} I I \dfrac{\hat{i}+\hat{j}}{\sqrt{2}}$$
option-1 $$\widehat{K} \times\left(\dfrac{\hat{i}+\hat{j}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)=\dfrac{\hat{j}-(-\hat{i})}{\sqrt{2}}=\dfrac{\hat{i}+\hat{j}}{\sqrt{2}} \| \dfrac{\hat{i}+\hat{j}}{\sqrt{2}}$$
option-2 and 4 do not satisfy this.
wave propagation vector $$\widehat{K}$$ should be along $$\dfrac{\hat{i}+\hat{j}}{\sqrt{2}}$$
So correct option is 3 .
subject: Physics
subtopic: Wave Equation and its Properties of EM Waves

question: The mean intensity of radiation on the surface of the Sun is about ${10^8}\,W/{m^2}$. The rms value of the corresponding magnetic field is closest to
explanation:
Mean intensity $$=\dfrac{B_{\text {rms }}^{2}}{\mu_{0}} c$$
$ \Rightarrow B_{rms}^2 = \frac{{{{10}^8} \times 4\pi \times {{10}^{ - 7}}}}{{3 \times {{10}^8}}} \Rightarrow {B_{rms}} \approx {10^{ - 4}}\,T$
subject: Physics

question:
If the magnetic field in a plane electromagnetic wave is given by
$$\vec{B}=3 \times 10^{-8} \sin \left(1.6 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{x}+48 \times 10^{10} t\right) \hat{j} T$$, then what will be expression for electric field?
explanation:
$$E_{0}=B_{0} C$$
$ = \left( {3 \times {{10}^{ - 8}}} \right)\left( {3 \times {{10}^8}} \right)\sin (\omega t + kx)$
${E_0} = 9\sin \left( {1.6 \times {{10}^3}x + 48 \times {{10}^{10}}t} \right)$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Wave Equation and its Properties of EM Waves

question: Relation between energy density of electric field $$\left(U_{E}\right)$$ and magnetic field $$\left(U_{B}\right)$$ will be:
explanation: Average energy density of electric field and magnetic field are same.
subject: Physics

question: If a $EM$ wave traveling in vacuum in $y$-direction has magnetic field  $\vec B = 8 \times {10^{ - 8}}(\hat K)$. Then value of electric field $$\vec{E}$$ is:
explanation:
$$\mathrm{E}_{0}=\mathrm{cB}_{0}$$
$$\Rightarrow \mathrm{E}_{0}=8 \times 10^{-8} \times\left(3 \times 10^{8}\right)=24$$
The direction of propagation for $EM$ wave is same as $$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}$$.
$$\because$$ The wave is travelling in $ + y$ direction and $$\vec{B}$$ is in $$+z$$ direction, and as $$(-\hat{i}) \times \hat{k}=+\hat{j}, \vec{E}$$ is in $x$ direction.
subject: Physics

question: An electric bulb is rated as $200\,W$. What will be the peak magnetic field at $4\,m$ distance produced by the radiations coming from this bulb? Consider this bulb as a point source with $$3.5 \%$$ efficiency.
explanation:
We know that, power $P$ energy density and efficiency $$\eta$$ can be related by:
$$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{\eta P}{4 \pi r^{2}}=\dfrac{c B_{\max }^{2}}{2 \mu_{0}} \\& \Rightarrow B_{\max }=\dfrac{1}{r} \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \dfrac{\eta P}{c}}\end{aligned}$$
Here $$\eta=0.35$$
${\mu _0} = 4\pi \times {10^{ - 7}}H/m\quad {\rm{ }}({\rm{or}}){\rm{ }}N/A{{\rm{ }}^2}.$
$ = \frac{1}{4}\sqrt {\frac{{{{10}^{ - 7}} \times 2 \times 0.35 \times 200}}{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}} $
$ = 1.71 \times {10^{ - 8}}\,T$
subject: Physics

question:
Match the Column I and Column II

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
explanation:
All the mentioned laws and respective equations are given by
Ampere - Maxwell law is given by,
$\oint \vec{B} d \vec{l}=\mu_{0} i_{c}+\mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0} \dfrac{d \phi_{E}}{d t}$
Gauss's law for electricity is given by,
$\oint \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{A}=\dfrac{Q}{\varepsilon_{0}}$
Gauss's law for magnetism is, $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{A}=0$
Faraday's law is $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{l}=\dfrac{d \phi_{B}}{d t}$
So, correct option is (3).

options.text: A – Q,     B – R, 	C – P, 	D – S
A – P,      B – Q, 	C – R, 	D – S
A – S,      B – R, 	C – P, 	D – Q
A – S,      B – P, 	C – R, 	D – Q

subject: Physics

question: Sea water at frequency $v=4 \times 10^{8} {~Hz}$ has permittivity  $\varepsilon  \approx 80\,{\varepsilon _0},$  permeability $\mu \approx \mu_{0}$ and resistivity $\rho=0.25\, \Omega-{m}$. Imagine a parallel plate capacitor immersed in sea water and driven by an alternating voltage source ${V}({t})={V}_{0} \sin (2 \,\pi {vt}$ ). What is the ratio of amplitude of the conduction current density to the displacement current density?
explanation:
$V(t) = {V_0}\sin 2\pi vt$
Let distance between the plates $={d}$.
Electric field $=\dfrac{{V}({t})}{{d}}=\dfrac{{V}_{0}}{{~d}} \sin 2 \pi {vt}$
Conducting current density
$J_{c}=\dfrac{E}{\rho}=\dfrac{V_{0}}{\rho d} \,\sin 2 \pi v t=J_{0 c} \sin 2 \pi v t$
where, ${J}_{0 {c}}$ is maximum conduction current density.
Displacement current density,
${J}_{{d}}=\varepsilon \dfrac{{d}}{{dt}}\left[\dfrac{{V}_{0}}{{~d}} \sin (2 \pi {vt})\right]=\dfrac{2 \pi v \varepsilon}{{d}} {V}_{0} \cos (2 \pi v {t})$
$J_{d}=J_{0_{d}} \cos (2 \pi v t)$
$\dfrac{J_{0_{d}}}{J_{0_{c}}}=\dfrac{\dfrac{2 \pi \vee \varepsilon V_{0}}{d}}{\dfrac{V_{0}}{\rho d}}$
$=2 \pi v \varepsilon \rho$
$=2 \pi \times 4 \times 10^{8} \times 80 \,\varepsilon_{0} \times 0.25$
$ = \frac{{4 \times {{10}^9}}}{{9 \times {{10}^9}}} = \frac{4}{9}\quad \left( {\frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}} = 9 \times {{10}^9}N{m^2}/{C^2}} \right)$
$=0.44$
subject: Physics

question: The amplitude of an electromagnetic wave in vacuum is doubled with no other changes made to the wave. As a result of this doubling of the amplitude, which of the following statement is correct?
explanation:
The speed of an electromagnetic wave in vacuum is
$c = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{\mu _0}{\varepsilon _0}} }} = 3 \times {10^8}\,m\,{s^{ - 1}}$
It is independent of amplitude, frequency and wavelength of the electromagnetic wave.

options.text: The frequency of the wave changes only.
The wavelength of the wave changes only.
The speed of wave propagation changes
None of the above is correct.

subject: Physics

question: Beyond which frequency, the ionosphere bends any incident electromagnetic radiation but do not reflect it back towards the earth?
explanation: The ionosphere can reflect electromagnetic waves of frequency less than  $40\,MHz$ but do not reflect electromagnetic waves of frequency more than $40MHz$.
subject: Physics

question:
If a source of electromagnetic radiation having power $15\,kW$ produces $10^{16}$ photons per second, the radiation belongs to a part of spectrum is
(Take Planck constant $h = 6 \times {10^{ - 34}}\,Js\,)$
explanation:
Power, $P=15 {~kW}$
$P=\dfrac{E}{t}=\dfrac{n h c}{\lambda}$
$\lambda=\dfrac{n h c}{P}=\dfrac{10^{16} \times 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{15 \times 10^{3}}$
$\lambda=1.32 \times 10^{-13} {~m}$
$\lambda < 10^{-12}$ lies in range of Gamma rays
subject: Physics

question:
Match Column I with Column II. Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
A. Gauss's law in Electrostatics
B. Faraday's law
C. Gauss's law nagnetism
D. Ampere Maxwell Law
P. $\oint {\vec E.{\mkern 1mu} \,d\vec l} = - \frac{{d{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\phi _B}}}{{dt}}$
Q. $\oint {\vec B.\,d\vec A} = 0$
R. $\oint {\vec B.\,d\vec l = } {\mu _0}{i_c} + {\mu _0}{\varepsilon _0}\frac{{d{\phi _E}}}{{dt}}$
S. $\oint {\vec E.\,d\vec s = \frac{{{q_{net}}}}{{{{\rm{\varepsilon }}_0}}}} $
explanation:
Gauss's law in electrostatics
$\Rightarrow \phi=\oint \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{s}=\dfrac{q_{\text {net }}}{\varepsilon_{0}}$
Faraday's law $\Rightarrow \oint \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{l}=-\dfrac{d \phi_{B}}{d t}$
Gauss's law in magnetism, $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d \vec{A}=0$, as the net magnetic pole strength enclosed by the Gaussion surface is zero.
Ampere's Maxwell law,
$\oint {\vec B} \cdot d\vec l = {\mu _0}{i_c} + {\mu _0}{\varepsilon _0}\frac{{d{\phi _E}}}{{dt}}$
${\rm{A}} - {\rm{S}},{\rm{B}} - {\rm{P}},{\rm{C}} - {\rm{Q}},{\rm{D}} - {\rm{R}}$
subject: Physics

question: Which of the following Maxwell's equation is valid for time varying conditions but not valid for static conditions?
explanation:
For time varying conditions, the Maxwell equation is given by
$\oint \vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d l}=-\dfrac{\partial \phi_{B}}{\partial t}=$ Induced $emf$ in a loop and $\vec{E}$ is the induced non-electrostatic electric field produced by variable magnetic field.
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Sound waves require a material medium for propagation.

Reason: Sound waves are not electromagnetic waves.
explanation: Sound waves, as mechanical waves, need a physical medium for propagation, disturbing particles to transmit energy. In contrast, electromagnetic waves, including light and radiation, can travel through a vacuum without relying on a material medium, showcasing distinctive propagation characteristics. So correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: $X$-ray astronomy is possible only from satellites orbiting the earth.

Reason: Efficiency of $X$-ray telescope is large as compared to terre-strail telescope.
explanation: $X$-rays are absorbed by Earth's atmosphere, if used in ground-based observatories. Placing $X$ - ray telescopes in space satellites eliminates atmospheric interference, enabling discoveries in $X$-ray astronomy. So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question: A point source of sound is located somewhere along the $x$-axis. Experiments show that the same wave front simultaneously reaches listeners at $x=-8 {~m}$ and $x=+2.0 {~m}$. A third listener is positioned along the positive $y$-axis. What is her $y$-coordinate (in $m$ ) if the same wave front reaches her at the same instant as it does the first two listeners?
explanation: Source should be located midway $x=-8 {~m}$ and $x=2 {~m}$. That is at $x=-3 {~m}$. For the same wavefront to reach at $C, S C=S A=S B$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950361/preptracker/questions/q_42265_explanation_1764950361249.png  $5=\sqrt{3^{2}+y^{2}} \Rightarrow y=4 {~m}$
subject: Physics

question:
An ant with mass $m$ is standing peacefully on top of a horizontal, stretched rope. The rope has mass per unit length $\mu$ and is under tension $F$. Without warning, a student starts a sinusoidal transverse wave of wavelength $\lambda$ propagating along the rope. The motion of the rope is in a vertical plane. What minimum wave amplitude will make the ant feel weightless momentarily? Assume that $m$ is so small that the presence of the ant has no effect on the propagation of the wave.
[Given: $\lambda=0.5 {~m}, \mu=0.1 {~kg} / {m}, F=3.125 {~N}$, take $g=\pi^{2}$ ]
explanation:
$a_{\max }=\omega^{2} A=g$
$\begin{aligned}& \omega=\dfrac{2 \pi v}{\lambda}, \quad v=\sqrt{\dfrac{F}{\mu}} \\& A_{\min }=\dfrac{g \lambda^{2} \mu}{4 \pi^{2} F}=\dfrac{\lambda^{2} \mu}{4 F}=2 \times 10^{-3} {~m}=2 {~mm}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A source of sound ${S}$ and a detector ${D}$ are placed at some distance from one another. A big cardboard is placed near the detector and perpendicular to the line ${S D}$ as shown in figure. It is gradually moved away and it is found that the intensity changes from a maximum to a minimum as the board is moved through a distance of $20\,cm$.  Find the frequency of the sound emitted is  Velocity of sound in air is ${336 {~m} / {s}}$.
explanation:
When board is moved through $20\,cm$, path difference will be $40\,cm$.
Phase difference ${\phi=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times 0.4=\pi}$
Wave frequency ${n=\dfrac{v}{\lambda}=\dfrac{336}{0.8}=420 {~Hz}}$
subject: Physics

question: An aluminium wire is clamped at each end and under zero tension at room temperature. Reducing the temperature, the tension in the wire is found to be increased (as length decreases). The wire is plucked. The resulting strain was found to be ${\rho ^a}{v^b}{Y^c}$ in the vibrating wire; where $\rho $: density, $v$ : velocity of wave in the string, $y$ : Young's modulus. The value $\frac{{ac}}{b} + \frac{{bc}}{a} = $
explanation:
$v = \sqrt {\frac{T}{\mu }} ;\frac{{\Delta L}}{L} = \frac{{\frac{F}{A}}}{Y}$
$\mu=\dfrac{{m}}{{L}}=\dfrac{\rho {AL}}{{L}} \rho {A}$
Using it in
$v = \sqrt {\frac{T}{\mu }} = \sqrt {\frac{F}{{\rho A}}} $
$\Rightarrow \rho v^{2}=\dfrac{F}{A}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\Delta L}{L}=\dfrac{\dfrac{F}{A}}{Y}=\dfrac{\rho v^{2}}{Y}$
$\Rightarrow {a}=1, {~b}=2, {c}=1$
$\therefore \quad \dfrac{{ac}}{{b}}+\dfrac{{bc}}{{d}}=\dfrac{(1)(1)}{2}+\dfrac{(2)(1)}{(1)}=2.5$
subject: Physics

question:
A source emitting sound of frequency $100\,Hz$ is placed in front of a wall at a distance of $2\,m$ from it. A detector is also placed in front of the wall at the same distance from it. The minimum distance between the source and the detector for which detector detects a maximum of sound is
$\left( {{v_{sound{\rm{ }}}} = 360\,m/s} \right)$
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950318/preptracker/questions/q_41916_explanation_1764950318341.png $a.$ Direct wave received from the source travels a distance of ' $x$ ' metre.
$b.$ Waves reaching after reflection travels a distance of $\left[(2)^{2}+\left(\dfrac{x^{2}}{4}\right)\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}$ metre.
Path difference,
$\Delta x = \left\{ {2{{\left[ {{{(2)}^2} + \left( {\frac{{{x^2}}}{4}} \right)} \right]}^{\frac{1}{2}}} - x} \right\}m$
Constructive interference will takes place when $\Delta x = \lambda ,2\lambda , \ldots $
For minimum,
$\Delta x = \lambda \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$\lambda = \frac{v}{v} = \frac{{360}}{{100}} = 3.6\,m$
From (1)
$2{\left[ {{{(2)}^2} + \frac{{{x^2}}}{4}} \right]^{\frac{1}{2}}} - x = 3.6$
$ \Rightarrow {\left( {4 + \frac{{{x^2}}}{4}} \right)^{\frac{1}{2}}} = 1.8 + \frac{x}{2}$
$ \Rightarrow 4 + \frac{{{x^2}}}{4} = 3.24 + \frac{{{x^2}}}{4} + 1.8x$
$\therefore \quad x = \frac{{0.76}}{{1.8}} = 0.42\,m$
subject: Physics

question: Waves produced in the prongs and the stem of a vibrating tuning fork are
explanation:
Longitudinal waves are produced in the stems and transverse waves are produced in the prong of a vibrating tuning fork.
So correct option is (3)

options.text: transverse in both prongs and stem
longitudinal in both prongs and stem
transverse in prongs, longitudinal in stem
longitudinal in prongs, transverse in stem.

question:
A pendulum vibrates with a time period of
1 second. The sound produced in it is in the range of
explanation:
Frequency ${=\dfrac{1}{t}=\dfrac{1}{1}=1}$ hertz
${\therefore}$ Infrasonic waves have range less than 20 $Hz$ . Therefore, sound will be in range of infrasonic.
So correct option is (4)

question:
Assertion: Soldiers are asked to break steps while crossing the bridge.

Reason: The frequency of marching may be equal to the natural frequency of bridge and may lead to resonance which can break the bridge.
explanation: This is done to prevent the frequency of marching from being equal to the natural frequency of the bridge, which could lead to structural damage. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

question:
Assertion: Resonance is a special case of forced vibration in which the nature and frequency of vibration of the body is the same as the impressed frequency and the amplitude of forced vibration is maximum.

Reason: The amplitude of forced vibrations of a body, increases with an increase in the frequency of the externally impressed periodic force.
explanation:
Driving force $F=F_{0} \sin \omega t$
Impressed frequency $=\omega$
Amplitude $A=\dfrac{F_{0}}{\left[m\left(\omega^{2}-\omega_{0}^{2}\right)\right]^{2}}$$\omega_{0}=\sqrt{\dfrac{K}{m}}$https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956222/preptracker/questions/q_41443_explanation_1764956221909.png $f_{0}=$ resonance frequency
$\omega_{0}=2 \pi f_{0}$
$\Rightarrow$ Both assertion and reason are true and reason explains assertion. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: Transverse waves are not produced in liquids and gases.

Reason: Light waves are transverse waves.
explanation: Transverse waves propagate as crests and troughs, causing a change in the shape of the medium. Since liquids and gases lack elasticity of shape, they cannot support transverse waves. Light waves are an example of transverse waves. So correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: Beats can also be observed by two laser light sources as in sound.

Reason: Laser light sources have constant phase difference.
explanation: Beats are typically not observed with usual light sources in the same way they are with sound sources. However, the reason is true; Two coherent light sources may have constant phase difference, But laser light is highly coherent and intense. Two laser sources of very nearly equal frequencies can produce beats. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: Two arms of a tuning fork vibrate in different phase.

Reason: Both arms in a tuning fork have same frequency of vibration.
explanation: The assertion is false because in a standard tuning fork, the two arms vibrate in the same phase. The reason is true as both arms indeed have the same frequency of vibration. So correct option is (4).

Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

question:
Assertion: A vibrating tuning fork sounds louder when its stem is pressed against a desk top.

Reason: When a wave reaches another denser medium, part of the wave is reflected.
explanation: A tuning fork, when struck, produces a pure tone by setting air particles into vibrational motion. If tuning fork is vibrated on a desktop makes the desktop vibrate at the same frequency. This forced vibration amplifies the sound as the larger desktop surface area affects more air particles, increasing amplitude and loudness. So correct option is (3).

question:
Assertion: Longitudinal waves cannot be polarised.

Reason: Electromagnetic waves can be polarised.
explanation: Longitudinal waves, (such as sound waves,) and cannot be polarized. The ability of electromagnetic waves to be polarized is due to vibrations of $\vec{E}, \vec{B}$ field which can be without medium also. So correct option is (2).

question:
Explosions on other planets are not heard on Earth.
Sound waves cannot travel to a far off distance
explanation: There is no material medium over a long distance between earth and other planets. So explosions on other planets are not heard on earth.
