question: The elevation of a cloud is $60^{\circ}$ above the horizon. A thunder is heard $8\,s$ after the observation of lighting. The speed of sound is $330\,m{s^{ - 1}}$. The vertical height of cloud from ground is:
explanation:
Here velocity of light is very high incomparision with velocity of sound. So time taken for the light is very small and can be neglected.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951065/preptracker/questions/q_40081_explanation_1764951065381.png
$OQ = 8 \times 330 = 2640\,m$
$\therefore PQ = OQ\sin 60^\circ $
$PQ = 2640 \times \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} = 2286\,m$
subject: Physics

question: A person wears a hearing aid that uniformly increases the sound level of all audible frequencies of sound by 30.0 $dB$.The hearing aid picks up sound having a frequency of 250 $Hz$ at an intensity of $3.0 \times {10^{ - 11}}\,W/{m^2}.$ What is the intensity delivered to the eardrum ?
explanation:
In terms of their intensities, the difference in the decibel level of two sounds is
${L_2} - {L_1} = (10\,dB)\log \left( {\frac{{{I_2}}}{{{I_0}}}} \right) - (10\,dB)\left( {\frac{{{I_1}}}{{{I_0}}}} \right)$
$ = (10\,dB)\log \left( {\frac{{{I_2}}}{{{I_0}}} \cdot \frac{{{I_0}}}{{{I_1}}}} \right) = (10\,dB)\log \left( {\frac{{{I_2}}}{{{I_1}}}} \right)$
Thus, $\dfrac{I_{2}}{I_{1}}=10^{\left(L_{2}-L_{1}\right) / 10}$
or $I_{2}=I_{1} \times 10^{\left(\beta_{2}-\beta_{1}\right) 10}$
If $L_{2}-L_{1}=30 {~dB}$
and $I_{1}=3.0 \times 10^{-11} {~W} / {m}^{2}$,
then ${I_2} = \left( {3.0 \times {{10}^{ - 11}}\,W/{m^2}} \right) \times {10^3}$
$ = 3.0 \times {10^{ - 8}}\,W/{m^2}$
subject: Physics

question: The intensity of sound increases at night due to
explanation: At night, amount of carbon dioxide in atmosphere increases which raises the density of atmosphere. Since, intensity is directly proportional to density, so intensity of sound is more at night.
subject: Physics

question: A point source emits sound equally in all directions in a non-absorbing medium. Two points $P$ and $Q$ are at distance of $2\,m$ and $3\,m$ respectively from the source. The ratio of the intensities of the waves at $P$ and $Q$ is
explanation:
The average power per unit area that is incident perpendicular to the direction of propagation is called the intensity, i.e.
$I=\dfrac{P}{4 \pi r^{2}} \Rightarrow I \propto \dfrac{1}{r^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right)^{2}$
Here, $r_{1}=2 m, r_{2}=3 m$
$\therefore \dfrac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{3}{2}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{9}{4}=9: 4$
subject: Physics

question: A bird is singing on a tree and a man is hearing at a distance ' $r$ ' from the bird. Calculate the displacement of the man towards the bird so that the loudness heard by man increases by 20 $dB$. [Assume that the motion of man is along the line joining the bird and the man]
explanation:
Loudness $\beta=10 \log _{10} \dfrac{I}{I_{0}}$
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore \quad \beta_{2}-\beta_{1}=10 \log _{10} \dfrac{I_{2}}{I_{1}} \text { and } \because \quad I=\dfrac{P}{4 \pi r^{2}} \\& \therefore \quad \dfrac{I_{2}}{I_{1}}=\dfrac{r_{1}^{2}}{r_{2}^{2}} \\& \therefore \quad(\beta+20)-\beta=10 \log _{10} \dfrac{r_{1}^{2}}{r_{2}^{2}}=20 \log _{10} \dfrac{r}{r_{2}} \\& \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}=10 \Rightarrow r_{2}=0.1 r \quad\left(r_{1}=r\right) \\& \therefore \text { shift }=r-0.1 r=0.9 r=\dfrac{9 r}{10}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A source of sound emits $200\,\pi W$ power which is uniformly distributed over a sphere of radius $10\,m$. What is the loudness of sound on the surface of the sphere?
explanation:
Intensity,
$I=\dfrac{P}{A}=\dfrac{200 \pi}{4 \pi \times(10)^{2}}=0.5$
No. of decibels is given by
$\therefore 10{\log _{10}}\frac{I}{{{I_0}}} = 10{\log _{10}}\frac{{0.5}}{{{{10}^{ - 12}}}}$
$ = 10{\log _{10}}\left( {5 \times {{10}^{11}}} \right) = 84\,dB$
subject: Physics

question: Intensity level of sound whose intensity is ${10^{ - 8}}W{m^{ - 2}}$ is____ $dB$
explanation:
Intensity level, $L = 10\log \left( {\frac{I}{{{I_O}}}} \right)dB$
where ${I_0} = $ threshold intensity $ = {10^{ - 12}}\,W\,{m^{ - 2}}$ Here, $I = {10^{ - 8}}\,W\,{m^{ - 2}}$
$\therefore L = 10\log \left( {\frac{{{{10}^{ - 8}}}}{{{{10}^{ - 12}}}}} \right)dB$
$ = 10\log \left( {{{10}^4}} \right)dB = 40\,dB$
subject: Physics

question: A stationary point source of sound emits sound uniformly in all directions in a non absorbing medium. Two points $P$ and $Q$ are at a distance of $4\,m$ and $9\,m$ respectively from the source. The ratio of amplitudes of the waves at $P$ and $Q$ is
explanation:
For an isotropic point source of power $P$, intensity $I$ at a distance $r$ from it will be
$I=\dfrac{P_{0}}{4 \pi r^{2}}$
Since power $\left( {{P_0}} \right)$ remains the same,
$\therefore \dfrac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right)^{2}=\left(\dfrac{9}{4}\right)^{2}$
$\because I \propto A^{2}$, where $A$ is the amplitude of a wave
$\therefore \dfrac{A_{1}}{A_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}}=\dfrac{9}{4}$
subject: Physics

question: The loudness and pitch of a sound note depends on
explanation: Loudness depends on the intensity of sound and pitch of sound depends on its frequency.
subject: Physics

question: How many times more intense is a $60\,dB$ sound than a $30\,dB$ sound?
explanation:
When the sound intensity level is $60\,dB$,
$10\log \left( {\frac{{{I_{60}}}}{{{I_0}}}} \right) = 60 \Rightarrow {I_{60}} = {10^6}{I_0}$,
where ${I_0} = $ reference intensity.
Similarly for $30\,dB,{I_{30}} = {10^3}{I_0}$
Hence $\dfrac{I_{60}}{I_{30}}=1000$.
subject: Physics

question: A sound level $I$ is greater by $3.0103\,dB$ from another sound of intensity $10nW\,c{m^{ - 2}}.$ The absolute value of intensity of sound level $I$ in $W{m^{ - 2}}$ is:
explanation:
$\beta_{1}-\beta_{2}=10 \log \dfrac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}$
$\begin{aligned}& 3.0103=10 \log \dfrac{I_{1}}{I_{2}} \\& 0.30103=\log \dfrac{I_{1}}{I_{2}} \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}=2\end{aligned}$
Given that ${I_2} = {10^{ - 4}}\frac{W}{{{m^2}}}$
$ \Rightarrow {I_1} = 2 \times {10^{ - 4}}W{m^{ - 2}}$
subject: Physics

question: The ratio of intensities between two coherent sound sources is $4: 1$. The difference of loudness in decibels $(\mathrm{dB})$ between maximum and minimum intensities when they interfere in space is:
explanation:
Given that $\dfrac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}=\dfrac{4}{1}$
$\therefore \dfrac{I_{\max }}{I_{\max }}=\left[\dfrac{\sqrt{I_{1} / I_{2}}+1}{\sqrt{I_{1} / I_{2}}-1}\right]^{2}=\left[\dfrac{2+1}{2-1}\right]^{2}=9$
The difference of loudness in decibles is
$\begin{array}{r}\beta_{1}-\beta_{2}=\log \dfrac{I_{\max }}{I_{0}}-10 \log \dfrac{I_{\min }}{I_{0}} \\=10 \log \dfrac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=10 \log (9)=20 \log (3)\end{array}$
subject: Physics

question: If the intensity of sound is doubled, then intensity level changes from $L_{1}$ to $L_{2}$ where
subject: Physics

question: Two identical sounds $S_{1}$ and $S_{2}$ reach at a point $O$  in phase. The resultant loudness at pont $P$ is $n\,dB$ higher than the loudness of $S_{1}$. The value of $n$ is:
explanation:
Let $a$ be the amplitude due to $S_{1}$ and $S_{2}$ individually.
Loudness due to $S_{1}=I_{1}=K a^{2}$
Loudness due to $S_{1}+S_{2}=I=K(2 a)^{2}=4 I_{1}$
$\begin{aligned}& \beta_{1}=10 \log \left(\dfrac{I}{I_{o}}\right) \\& \beta_{2}=10 \log \left(\dfrac{4 I}{I_{o}}\right) \\& n=\beta_{2}-\beta_{1}=10 \log _{10}(4)=6\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A person is talking in a small room and the sound intensity level is $60\,dB$ everywhere within the room. If there are eight people talking simultaneously in the room, what is the sound intensity level?
explanation:
Let intensity due to a single person $=I$ then $10 \log I / I_{0}=60=\beta_{1}$
$\begin{aligned}& \beta_{2}=10 \log \left(\dfrac{8 I}{I_{0}}\right)=10\left(\log \dfrac{I}{I_{0}}+\log 8\right) \\& =60+10 \log 8=60+30 \log 2=69\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: The intensity of sound increases at night due to:
explanation: At night, amount of carbon dioxide in atmosphere increases which raises the density of atmosphere. Since, intensity is directly proportional to density, intensity of sound is more at night. Option (1) is correct
subject: Physics

question: The energy per unit area associated with a progressive sound wave will be doubled if:
explanation:
$\dfrac{E}{A}=I$
$\begin{aligned}& I^{\prime}=2 I \\& A^{\prime 2}=\sqrt{2} A \\& \Delta A=\sqrt{2} A-A=A(\sqrt{2}-1) \\& \dfrac{\Delta A}{A} \%=(\sqrt{2}-1) \times 100=41 \%\end{aligned}$

options.text: The amplitude of the wave is doubled
The amplitude of the wave is increases by $50 \%$
The amplitude of the wave is increases by $41 \%$

subject: Physics

question: Sound waves are emitted uniformly in all directions from a point source. The dependence of sound level $\beta$ in decibels on the distance $r$ can be expressed as ( $a$ and $b$ are positive constants)
explanation:
$\beta=10 \log _{10} \dfrac{I}{I_{0}}$
If $P$ is power of sound source then
$\beta = 10{\log _{10}}\frac{{\left( {P/4\pi {r^2}} \right)}}{{{I_0}}}\,{\text{where }}\,{I_0} = {10^{ - 12}}w/{m^2}$
$=10 \log _{10} \dfrac{P}{4 \pi r^{2} \cdot I_{0}}=10\left[\log P-\log \left(4 \pi I_{0} \cdot r^{2}\right)\right]$
$\left.=10\left[\log P-\log 4 \pi I_{0}-2 \log r\right)\right]=a-b \log r$
subject: Physics

question: For a sound source of intensity $I\,W/{m^2}$, corresponding sound level is $B_{0}$ decibel. If the intensity is increased to $4I$, new sound level becomes:
explanation:
Given that $B_{o}=10 \log \left(\dfrac{I}{I_{o}}\right)$
$B=10 \log \left(\dfrac{4 I}{I_{o}}\right)=10\left[\log \left(\dfrac{I}{I_{o}}\right)+\log 4\right]$
$B=B_{o}+10 \log (4)=B_{o}+20 \log 2=\left(B_{o}+6\right) d B$
subject: Physics

question: In the absence of teacher a class of 50 students make a noise level of $50\,dB$. 50 more students enter the class. Assuming each student on an average produces same intensity of sound then the noise level increases by
explanation:
$\beta_{1}=10 \log \left(\dfrac{I}{I_{o}}\right)$
After adding 50 more students $\beta_{2}=10 \log \left(\dfrac{2 I}{I_{o}}\right)$
$\beta_{2}-\beta_{1}=10 \log \dfrac{2 I}{I_{0}}-10 \log \dfrac{I}{I_{0}}$
$\Delta \beta = 10\log 2 = 3\,dB$
subject: Physics

question: A person speaking normally produces a sound intensity of $40\,dB$ at a distance of $1\,m$. If the threshold intensity for reasonable audibility is $20\,dB$, the maximum distance at which he can be heard clearly is
subject: Physics

question: The intensity level of sound wave is said to be 4 decibel. If the intensity of wave is doubled, then the intensity level of sound as expressed in decibel would be
explanation:
$\beta=10 \log \dfrac{I}{I_{0}}$
$\begin{aligned}& \beta_{1}=4=10 \log \dfrac{I}{I_{0}} \\& \beta_{2}=10 \log \left(\dfrac{2 I}{I_{0}}\right)=4+10 \log (2)=7\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question:
An increase in intensity level of $1\,dB$ implies an increase in intensity of:
(given antilog $0.1=1.2589$ )
explanation:
Intensity level is decibel is given by
$\begin{gathered}L=10 \log _{10} \dfrac{I_{1}}{I_{0}} \\L+1=10 \log _{10} \dfrac{I_{2}}{I_{0}} \\\Rightarrow 1=10 \log _{10} \dfrac{I_{2}}{I_{0}}-10 \log _{10} \dfrac{I_{1}}{I_{0}}\end{gathered}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{10}} = {\log _{10}}\frac{{{I_2}}}{{{I_1}}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{I_2}}}{{{I_1}}} = 1.26 \Rightarrow {I_2} = 1.26{I_1}$
$\frac{{\Delta I}}{I} \times 100 = 26\% $
subject: Physics

question: The intensity level $1\,m$ away from a source is $60\,dB$. Threshold intensity of hearings is ${10^{ - 12}}W{m^{ - 2}}$. If there is no loss of sound power in air then intensity level at $200\,cm$ from the source is
explanation:
$I=\dfrac{P}{4 \pi r^{2}} \Rightarrow I \alpha \dfrac{1}{r^{2}}$
$\frac{{{I_1}}}{{{I_2}}} = \frac{{r_2^2}}{{r_1^2}} = 4 \Rightarrow {I_2} = \frac{{{I_1}}}{4}$
${\beta _1} = 60 = 10\log \left( {\frac{{{I_1}}}{{{I_o}}}} \right)$
${\beta _2} = 10\log \left( {\frac{{{I_1}}}{{4{I_o}}}} \right) = 54\,dB$
subject: Physics

question: If the pressure amplitude in a sound wave is tripled, then the intensity of sound is increased by a factor of
explanation:
Intensity $\propto(\text { Amplitude })^{2}$
$\frac{{{I_1}}}{{{I_2}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{A_1}}}{{{A_2}}}} \right)^2} = {\left( {\frac{{{A_1}}}{{3{A_1}}}} \right)^2}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{I_1}}}{{{I_2}}} = \frac{1}{9}$
$ \Rightarrow {I_2} = 9{I_1}$
subject: Physics

question: When we hear a sound, we can identify its source based on
explanation: The sounds of different source are said to differ in quality. The number of overtones and their relative intensities determines the quality of any musical sound.

question: There are 10 sound sources each producing intensity $I$ at a point independently. They are incoherent. Average intensity of sound at that point will be
explanation:
Since the source are incoherent there will be no sustained interference.
Hence at the given point
Average intensity $=I_{1}+I_{2}+I_{3}+\ldots . .+I_{10}=10 I$

question: Two sound waves travel in the same direction in a medium. The amplitude of each wave is $A$ and the phase difference between the two waves is $120^{\circ}$. The resultant amplitude will be
explanation:
Here, $A_{1}=A, A_{2}=A, \phi=120^{\circ}$
The amplitude of the resultant wave is
$\begin{aligned}& A_{R}=\sqrt{A_{1}^{2}+A_{2}^{2}+2 A_{1} A_{2} \cos \phi} \\& =\sqrt{A^{2}+A^{2}+2 A A \cos 120^{\circ}} \\& \therefore A_{R}=A .\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: The sound intensity is $0.008\,W/{m^2}$ at a distance of $10\,m$ from an isotropic point source of sound. The power of the source is
explanation:
$\dfrac{P}{4 \pi r^{2}}=I$
$ \Rightarrow P = I \cdot 4\pi {r^2}$
$ = \left( {0.008\,W/{m^2}} \right)\left( {4 \cdot \pi \cdot {{10}^2}} \right) = 10\,W$

question: A sound absorber attenuates the sound level by $20\,dB$. The intensity decreases by a factor of
explanation:
Let ${I_1}$ & ${I_2}$ are initial and final intensities
$\beta_{1}=10 \log \left(\dfrac{I_{1}}{I_{0}}\right)$
Later, $\beta_{2}=10 \log \left(\dfrac{I_{2}}{I_{0}}\right)$
Given, $\beta_{1}-\beta_{2}=20$
$\therefore 20 = 10\log \left( {\frac{{{I_1}}}{{{I_2}}}} \right)$
${I_1} = 100\,{I_2}$

${L_2} = 10\log \frac{{2I}}{{{I_o}}} \Rightarrow \Delta L = 3.01\,dB$

explanation: Reverberation time $T \propto V$.

question: The speed of a transverse wave in a stretched string '$A$' is '$v$'. Another string '$B$' of same length and same radius is subjected to same tension. If the density of the material of the string '$B$' is $2 \%$ more than that of '$A$', then the speed of the transverse wave in string '$B$' is
explanation:
$v=\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{A d}}$
where $d=$ density and $A=$ Cross sectional area of wire.
$v \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{d}}$
If the density of the material of the string $B$ is $2 \%$ more than that of $A$ then
$v^{\prime} \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{d+d \times \dfrac{2}{100}}}$ or $v^{\prime} \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{d(1+0.02)}}$
$\therefore \dfrac{\v^{\prime}}{v}=\sqrt{\dfrac{d}{d(1.02)}}$
$\Rightarrow v^{\prime}=\dfrac{v}{\sqrt{1.02}}$
subject: Physics

question: Match the following concepts related to the speed of a travelling wave and choose the correct option from the codes given below
explanation:
A - Q: The speed of the wave is measured $\dfrac{\omega}{k}$.
B - R: The position of a point with a fixed phase on the wave changes to maintain constant phase.
$\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{S}$ : The general relation for wave speed (v) is given by the product of wavelength ( $\lambda$ ) and frequency $(v)$, or alternatively $\lambda$ divided by the period ( $T$ ).
D - P : The speed of a mechanical wave is determined by the inertial properties (like mass density) and elastic properties (like Young's modulus) of the medium. Correct option is (3).

options.text: A - P, B - Q,  C - R,     D - S
A - R,  B - S,  C - P,  D - Q
A - Q,  B - R,  C - S,  D - P
A - S,   B - R, C - Q,  D - P

The speed of transverse waves on a string depends on the tension and linear mass density of the string.
The frequency of a wave is determined by the source and is independent of the tension in the string.
explanation: Statement A is true as the speed of a wave on a string is given by $v=\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$, where $T$ is the tension and $\mu$ is the linear mass density. Statement B is also true because the frequency of the wave is indeed determined by the source of the vibration. Correct option is (1)

question:
Read the ${\rm{Assertion}}$ and ${\rm{Reason}}$ carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: The speed of a mechanical wave is determined by the inertial and elastic properties of the medium.

Reason: The linear mass density and Young's modulus of a string determine the speed of transverse waves on the string.
explanation: The assertion is true because the speed of a mechanical wave is indeed determined by the properties of the medium through which it is traveling. However, the reason is also true but it only mentions transverse waves on a string, whereas the speed of a wave can be affected by other factors as well, such as the type of wave and medium (e.g., for sound waves in air, the speed is affected by temperature and humidity). Correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Assertion is false but Reason is true.

question: A wire of length $0.4\,m$ stretched at both ends vibrates 250 times per second. If the length of the wire is increased by $0.1\,m$ and the stretching force is reduced to $1 / 4$ th of its original value, then the new frequency is
explanation:
Initial length of wire, $l_{1}=0.4 {~m}$
Frequency of stretched wire,
$f_{1}=250 {vib} / {s}=250 {~Hz}$
Final length of wire after stretching,
$l_{2}=l_{1}+01 {~m}=0.4+01=0.5 {~m}$
Initial force (tension), $T_{1}=T$
Final force (tension), $T_{2}=\dfrac{T}{4}$
We know that, frequency of wave produced due to stretched string.
$f=\dfrac{l}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{m}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{f_{2}}{f_{1}}=\dfrac{l_{1}}{l_{2}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}}=\dfrac{0.4}{0.5} \sqrt{\dfrac{T / 4}{T}}=\dfrac{4}{5} \times \dfrac{1}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{f_{2}}{f_{1}}=\dfrac{2}{5}$
$\Rightarrow f_{2}=\dfrac{2}{5} f_{1}=\dfrac{2}{5} \times 250=100 {~Hz}$

question: A heavy uniform rope is suspended vertically from a ceiling and is in equilibrium. A pulse is generated at the bottom end of the rope as shown. As the pulse travels up the rope, its acceleration at any instant is https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952596/preptracker/questions/q_109252_question_1764952596244.png ($g$ is acceleration due to gravity)
explanation:
Speed of wave on the string is given by
$V = \sqrt {\frac{T}{\mu }} {\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
where $T$ is tension, $\mu$ is linear mass density. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952597/preptracker/questions/q_109252_explanation_1764952597010.png Mass of the rope $= m$
$\mu = \frac{m}{L}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
Put the value of $T$ and $\mu$ in Eq. (1),
$v=\sqrt{\dfrac{m g}{\dfrac{m}{L}}}=\sqrt{g L}$
$a=\dfrac{U^{2}}{2 s}=\dfrac{g L}{2 L} \quad($ because $\quad S=L)$
$a=\dfrac{g}{2}$
Hence, the value of acceleration is constant.

options.text: variable but equal to $\dfrac{g}{2}$ when the pulse is exactly at the middle of the string.
variable but equal to $g$ when the pulse is exactly at the middle of the string.

question: One end of a string is tied to the ceiling of a lift and a load is attached at the bottom end of the string. When the lift is moving upwards with an acceleration of $2.1 {~ms}^{-2}$, the speed of the transverse wave at the lower end of the string is $88 {~ms}^{-1}$. If the lift moves downwards with an acceleration of $1.9 {~ms}^{-2}$, the speed of the transverse wave at the lower end of the string is $\left(g=10 {~ms}^{-2}\right)$
explanation:
Speed of transverse wave when lift is going up $\left(v_{1}^{2}\right)=88 {~m} / {s}^{2}$
Using the formula for velocity
$v=\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$
where $T$ is tension in string $\mu$ is mass/length.
$T=m a_{\text {net }} \quad$ [mass of string]
Case I When lift goes up
$a_{\text {net }}=a_{1}+g=10+2.1=12.1 {~m} / {s}^{2}$
$T_{1}=m(12.1)$
Case II When lift is going down
$a_{\text {net }}=g-a_{2}=10-1.9=8.1 {~m} / {s}^{2}$
$T_{2}=m(8.1)$
Putting the value of $T_{1}, T_{2}$ in equation for $v$.
${v_1} = \sqrt {\frac{{{T_1}}}{\mu }} \Rightarrow {v_1} = \sqrt {\frac{{(12.1)m}}{\mu }} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
${v_2} = \sqrt {\frac{{{T_2}}}{\mu }} \Rightarrow {v_2} = \sqrt {\frac{{(8.1)m}}{\mu }} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From Eq. (1) and (2)
$\dfrac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}=\dfrac{\sqrt{T_{1}}}{\sqrt{T_{2}}} \quad[\mu$ is same for both as mass $]$
$\Rightarrow v_{2}=v_{1} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}} \Rightarrow 72 {~m} / {s}$
Hence, the velocity is $72 {~m} / {s}$.
So, the correct option is (4)

question: A wire of length $50\,cm$ and weighing $10\,gm$ is attached to a spring at one end and to a fixed wall at the other end. The spring has a spring constant of $50 {~N} / {m}$ and is stretched by $1\,cm$. If a wave pulse is produced on the string near the wall, then how much time will it take to reach the spring?
explanation:
Tension in wire $=k x=50 \times \dfrac{1}{100}=0.5 {~N}$
Mass per unit length of string
$\mu=\dfrac{m}{l}=\dfrac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{50 \times 10^{-2}}=\dfrac{1}{50}$
Speed of wave pulse on string.
$v=\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{0.5}{\left(\dfrac{1}{50}\right)}}=\sqrt{25} {~ms}^{-1}=5 {~ms}^{-1}$
Time required by pulse to reach other end,
$t=\dfrac{l}{v}=\dfrac{50 \times 10^{-2} m}{5 {~ms}^{-1}}=0.1 {~s}$

question: Two wires of different densities but same area of cross sections are soldered together at one end and are stretched at a tension $T$. The velocity of a transverse wave in the first wire is double of that in the second wire. Find the ratio of the density of the first wire to that of the second wire.
explanation:
Let $v_{1}=$ velocity in the $1^{st}$ string
${v_1} = \sqrt {\left( {\frac{T}{\mu }} \right)} = \sqrt {\left( {\frac{T}{{{\rho _1}a}}} \right)} \quad \quad {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} (1)$
where $a=$ area of cross-section
Let $v_{2}=$ velocity in the second string
${v_2} = \sqrt {\left( {\frac{T}{{{\mu _2}}}} \right)} = \sqrt {\left( {\frac{T}{{{\rho _2}a}}} \right)} {\text{ }}\quad \quad \quad {\text{(2)}}$
Given that ${v_1} = 2{v_1}$
$\sqrt {\left( {\frac{T}{{a{\rho _1}}}} \right)} = 2\sqrt {\left( {\frac{T}{{a{\rho _2}}}} \right)} \Rightarrow \frac{{{\rho _1}}}{{{\rho _2}}} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$

question: A uniform rope of length $12\,m$ and mass $6\,kg$ hangs vertically from a rigid support. A block of mass $2\,kg$ is attached to the free end of the rope. A transverse pulse of wavelength $0.06\,m$ is produced at the lower end of the rope. What is the wavelength of the pulse (in $m$) when it reaches the top of the rope?
explanation:
As the rope is heavy, its tension will be different at different points. The tension at the free end will be $2\,g$ and that at the upper end it will be $8\,g$.
We have ${v=n \lambda}$
or $\sqrt {\frac{T}{\mu }} = n\lambda $
$\sqrt {\frac{T}{\lambda }} = n\sqrt \mu {\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
The frequency of the wave pulse will be the same everywhere on the rope as it depends only on the frequency of the source. The mass per unit length is also the same throughout the rope as it is uniform. Thus, we have
As ${\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\lambda}}}$ is constant, we have ${\dfrac{\sqrt{2 g}}{0.06 m}=\dfrac{\sqrt{8 g}}{\lambda_{1}}}$
Solving we get ${\lambda_{1}=0.12 {~m}}$
Where ${\lambda_{1}}$ is the wavelength at the top of the rope

question: Equation of travelling wave on a stretched string of linear density $4\,kg/m$ is $y = 0.07\cos (250t - 15x)$ where distance and time are measured in SI units. The tension in the string is
explanation:
$y = 0.07\cos \left( {250t - 15x} \right)$
$v = \frac{\omega }{k} = \frac{{250}}{{15}} = \frac{{50}}{3}\,m/s$
Also $v=\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$
$\Rightarrow {T}={v}^{2} \mu=\left(\dfrac{50}{3}\right)(4)=\dfrac{200}{3}=66.67 {~N}$

question: A string has tension $T$. For tripling the frequency, the tension in string will become
explanation:
The frequency of a string,
$f=\dfrac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$ where, $\mu$ is mass perunit length of strings and $T$ is tension in the string.
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore \quad f \propto \sqrt{T} \\& \Rightarrow \quad T \propto f^{2} \\& \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}=\left(\dfrac{f_{2}}{f_{1}}\right)^{2}\end{aligned}$
Given, $f_{1}=f, f_{2}=3 f$ and $T_{1}=T$
$\therefore \quad \dfrac{T_{2}}{T}=\left(\dfrac{3 f}{f}\right)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad T_{2}=9 T$

question: The tension of a stretched string is increased by ${69 \%}$. To keep its frequency of vibration constant, its length must be increased by
explanation:
${f=\dfrac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{m}}}$
${\Rightarrow l^{2} \propto T}$ or ${l \propto T^{1 / 2}}$
${\dfrac{l^{\prime}}{l}=\left(\dfrac{T^{\prime}}{T}\right)^{1 / 2}=\left(\dfrac{T+0.69 T}{T}\right)^{1 / 2}}$
${=(1.69)^{1 / 2}=1.3}$
If the length is increased by ${x \%}$, then
${l^{\prime}=l+\dfrac{x l}{100}}$
${\therefore \dfrac{l+\dfrac{x}{100} l}{l}=1.3}$
${1+\dfrac{x}{100}=1.3}$
${x / 100=0.3}$
or ${x=30 \%}$

explanation:

question: A string wave equation is given by $y=0.002 \sin (300 t-15 x)$ and mass density is $\left( {\mu  = 0.1\,kg{\rm{/}}m} \right)$. Then, find the tension force in the string.
explanation:
Given, wave equation,
$y=0.002 \sin (300 t-15 x)$
Mass density, $\mu = 0.1\,kg{\rm{/}}m$
Comparing the given wave equation with $y=a \sin (\omega t-k x)$, we have
$\omega=300, k=15$
$\therefore$ Velocity of wave,
$v = \frac{\omega }{k} = \frac{{300}}{{15}} = 20\,m{\rm{/}}s$
Wave speed in string is also given by
$v=\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$
where, $\mathrm{T}=$ tension in the string and $\mu=$ mass per unit length of string
$\therefore 20=\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{0.1}}$
$\Rightarrow 400=\dfrac{T}{0.1}$
$\Rightarrow T=40 N$

question: Speed of a transverse wave on a straight wire (mass $6.0\,g$, length $60\,cm$ and area of crosssection $1.0\,m{m^2}$) is $90\,m{s^{ - 1}}$. If the Young's modulus of wire is $16 \times {10^{11}}N{m^{ - 2}}$, the extension of wire over its natural length is
explanation:
$v=\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{T A}{A \mu}}$
$ \Rightarrow v = \sqrt {\frac{{Y\Delta L}}{L}\frac{A}{{m/L}}} \left( {\because \mu = \frac{m}{L}} \right)$
$v = \sqrt {\frac{{Y\Delta LA}}{m}} $
$\Delta L = \frac{{{v^2}m}}{{YA}}$
$ = \frac{{90 \times 90 \times 6 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{16 \times {{10}^{11}} \times 1 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}$
$\Delta L \approx 0.03\,mm$

question: A heavy ball of mass $M$ is suspended from the ceiling of a car by a light string of mass $m(m < < $ $M)$. When the car is at rest, the speed of transverse waves in the string is $60\,m{s^{ - 1}}$. When the car has acceleration $a$, the wave speed increases to $60.5\,m{s^{ - 1}}$. The value of $a$, in terms of gravitational acceleration $g$, is closest to
explanation:
When the car is at rest${v_1} = \sqrt {\frac{{{T_1}}}{\mu }} = \sqrt {\frac{{Mg}}{\mu }} $
when the car is in acceleration
$\frac{{{v_1}}}{{{v_2}}} = \sqrt {\frac{g}{{{{\left( {{g^2} + {a^2}} \right)}^{1/2}}}}} $
$\left( {\frac{{{v_2}}}{{{v_1}}}} \right) = {\left( {\frac{{{g^2} + {a^2}}}{{\,{g^2}}}} \right)^{1/4}} \approx 1 + \frac{{{a^2}}}{{4\,{g^2}}}$
$\frac{{60.5}}{{60}} = 1 + \frac{1}{4}\frac{{{a^2}}}{{\,{g^2}}}\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {a^2}$
$\frac{{{a^2}}}{{{g^2}}} = \frac{1}{{30}} \Rightarrow a = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {30} }} \approx \frac{g}{5}$

question: A uniform string of length $l$, mass $M$ rotates about one of its end $O$ in a horizontal plane with an angular speed $\omega$. You can ignore force of gravity on the string. The time taken by a transverse wave pulse from one end of the string to another i.e., $O$ is $\dfrac{\pi}{\sqrt{x} . \omega}$. The value of $x$ is
explanation:
Consider a small element of length $dx$ at a distance $x$ from the end of the string.
The centrifugal force on the element is
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950090/preptracker/questions/q_40747_explanation_1764950089845.png
$dT = \mu \,dx\,x\,{\omega ^2}$
$T = \mu {\omega ^2}\int\limits_x^l {x\,dx = \mu {\omega ^2}} \left[ {\frac{{{x^2}}}{2}} \right]_x^l$
$T=\mu \omega^{2} \dfrac{\left[l^{2}-x^{2}\right]}{2}$
$T$ is the tension in the string at a distance $x$ from the end. The velocity of the pulse at the distance
$\begin{aligned}& x \text { is } \quad v=\dfrac{d x}{d t}=\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}=\dfrac{\omega}{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{l^{2}-x^{2}} \\& \int_{0}^{l} \dfrac{d x}{\sqrt{l^{2}-x^{2}}}=\dfrac{\omega}{\sqrt{2}} \int_{0}^{t} d t \\& {\left[\sin ^{-1} \dfrac{x}{l}\right]_{0}^{l}=\dfrac{\omega}{\sqrt{2}} t} \\& {\left[\sin ^{-1}\left(\dfrac{l}{l}\right)-\sin ^{-1}(0)\right]=\dfrac{\omega}{\sqrt{2}} t} \\& {\left[\dfrac{\pi}{2}-0\right]=\dfrac{\omega}{\sqrt{2}} t} \\& t=\dfrac{\pi}{\omega \sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow x=2\end{aligned}$

question: A string of length $l$ hangs from the rigid support $B$. The linear mass density of the string $\mu=\mu_{0}\left(\dfrac{x}{l}\right)$. Choose incorrect option from following.
explanation:
$T = g\int \mu dx = g\int\limits_0^x {{\mu _0}} \cdot \frac{x}{l} \cdot dx = {\mu _0}g \cdot \left( {\frac{{{x^2}}}{{2l}}} \right)$
$v=\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{g x}{2}} \quad\left(\mu=\mu_{o} \dfrac{x}{l}\right)$

$\begin{gathered}a=v \dfrac{d v}{d x}=\dfrac{g}{4} \\v=\dfrac{d x}{d t}=\sqrt{\dfrac{g x}{2}} \\\int_{0}^{l} \dfrac{d x}{\sqrt{x}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{2}} \int_{0}^{t} d t \\t=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 l}{a}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 l}{g / 4}}=2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 l}{g}}\end{gathered}$
If the string accelerates up with $a=g$. Then the tension in the string is
$\begin{aligned}& T=2 \mu_{0} g\left(\dfrac{x^{2}}{2 l}\right) \\& v=\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}=\sqrt{g x}\end{aligned}$
Acceleration of the wave pulse relative to the string is $a=\dfrac{g}{2}$.
Distance covered $s=v_{0} t+\dfrac{1}{2} a^{\prime} t^{2}$
$=0+\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{g}{2}+g\right) \cdot\left(2 \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}\right)^{2}=3 l$

options.text: Tension at $P$ is $\dfrac{\mu_{0} g x^{2}}{2 l}$
Acceleration of the wave pulse at $P$  is $\dfrac{g}{4}$
Time taken by the pulse to move from $A$ to $B$ is $2 \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{g}}$
The distance covered by the wave pulse during the time in which pulse reaches to $B$ from $A$ is $3 l$, if $B$ is accelerating upwards with $g$

question: A long, heavy, flexible rope with mass per unit length $\mu$ is under a constant tension $T$. A sudden movement causes a circular loop, of radius $R$ to form at one end of the rope. The loop rolls along the rope with speed $v$ as shown in the figure. Find the kinetic energy (in $J$) of this circular pulse. $\left[ {\mu  = 5 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}kg{m^{ - 1}},R = \frac{1}{{8\pi }}m,T = 16\,N} \right]$
explanation:
Velocity of the pulse is
$v = \sqrt {\frac{{16}}{{5 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}} m/s\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} = \frac{{40}}{{\sqrt 5 }}m/s$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} KE = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} + \frac{1}{2}{I_{cm}} \cdot {\omega ^2} = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} + \frac{1}{2}m{R^2}{\omega ^2}$
$ = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} + \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}$
$K.E = m{v^2} = 2\pi R\mu \cdot {v^2} = 2\pi \times \frac{1}{{8\pi }} \times 5 \times {10^{ - 2}} \times \frac{{1600}}{5}J$
$ = 4{\text{ }}Joule.{\text{ }}$

question: The string of length $l_{0}$ and circular radius varying from $r_{1}$ to $r_{2}$ uniformly is acted upon by a force $F$ at both ends. If the density of the string is $\rho$, then the time taken by a transverse wave pulse to move from $A$ to $B$ is $\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho \pi}{F}} \cdot\left[\dfrac{r_{1}+r_{2}}{4}\right] l_{0} x$. The value of $x$ is____
explanation:
The radius of the string at a distance $x$ is
$\begin{aligned}r & =r_{1}+\left(\dfrac{r_{2}-r_{1}}{l}\right) x \\A & =\pi\left(r_{1}+\dfrac{r_{2}-r_{1}}{l} x\right)^{2} \\\therefore \quad v & =\sqrt{\dfrac{F}{\mu}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{F}{\rho A}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{F}{\pi \rho}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\left(r_{1}+\dfrac{r_{2}-r_{1}}{l} x\right)} \\\therefore \quad t & =\int_{0}^{l_{0}} \dfrac{d x}{v}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi \rho}{F}}\left(\dfrac{r_{1}+r_{2}}{4}\right) 2 l_{0} .\end{aligned}$

question: A "Tsunami" that is huge tidal wave is created at $O$ of the ocean and travelled towards shore as shown. It was found that the amplitude of wave $A$ is inversely proportional to ${h^{1/4}},\,h$ is height of water surface from bed. If the wave amplitude at the open sea having depth $4.3\,km$ is $0.5\,cm$, then the amplitude at coastal shallows having depth $10\,m$ is $0.2 x$ meter. The value of $x$ is____
explanation:
$\dfrac{A_{2}}{A_{1}}=\left(\dfrac{h_{1}}{h_{2}}\right)^{1 / 4}=\left[\dfrac{4300}{10}\right]^{1 / 4}=4.55$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{0.2 x}{0.5}=4.55 \Rightarrow \quad x=11.37 m$

question: A smooth string is connected with two hanging blocks of mass $M$ & $2M$ after passing over the pulleys $P$ \& Q. Mass of the string between $P$ & $Q$ is $m( <  < M)$. If the system is released from rest at time $t = 0$, then time taken by a transverse pulse produced at $P$ at $t = 0$ to move to point $Q$ is $\sqrt{\dfrac{x l}{g}}\left[\sqrt{\dfrac{4 M}{m}+2}-\sqrt{\dfrac{4 M}{m}}\right]$. The value of $x$ is____
explanation:
The tension in the pulley system is
$\begin{aligned}& T=\left[\dfrac{2(M)(2 M)}{M+2 M}\right] g \\& v_{0}=\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}} \\& a=\dfrac{(2 M-M) g}{2 M+M}=\dfrac{g}{3}\end{aligned}$
Let $t$ is the time at which the pulse reaches $Q$.
Here $v_{o} t$ is the distance travelled by the pulse on the string. $\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}$ is the distance travelled by the string in time $t$.
$l=v_{0} t+\dfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}$
By solving the equation carefully for $t$ and keeping the values of $T, v_{o}$ and $a$ we get
$\Rightarrow t=\sqrt{\dfrac{3 l}{g}}\left[\sqrt{\dfrac{4 M}{m}+2}-\sqrt{\dfrac{4 M}{m}}\right]$
The value of $x$ is 3.

question: A rope hangs from a rigid support. A pulse is set by jiggling the bottom end. We want to design a rope in which velocity $v$ of pulse is independent of $z$, the distance of the pulse from fixed end of the rope. If the rope is very long the desired function for mass per unit length $\mu (z)$ in terms of $\mu_{0}$ [mass per unit length of the rope at the top $(z = 0)$] is given by
explanation:
Tension in string at $P$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956147/preptracker/questions/q_40690_explanation_1764956146957.png
$\therefore$ Velocity of pulse at $P$
$\begin{aligned}& v=\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}} \Rightarrow v^{2} \mu=T \\& v^{2} d \mu=d T \quad(v=\text { constant }) \\& v^{2} d \mu=-\mu d z g\end{aligned}$
As the pulse goes down $z$ increases
so $dz$ is (+)ve, where as $d \mu$ has to be (-)ve .
Since $\mu$ should decrease to maintain the pulse velocity as constant.
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow \int_{\mu_{0}}^{\mu} \dfrac{d \mu}{\mu}=-\left(\dfrac{g}{v^{2}}\right) \int_{0}^{z} d z \\& \Rightarrow \mu=\mu_{0} e^{-\left(g z / v^{2}\right)}\end{aligned}$

question: A transverse wave is propagating on the string. The linear density of a vibrating string is ${10^{ - 3}}\,kg/m$. The equation of the wave is $Y=0.05 \sin (x+15 t)$ where $X$ and $Y$ are measured in metre and time in second. The tension force in the string is
explanation:
Given that, the linear mass density
$m = {10^{ - 3}}\,kg/m$ and equation of the wave
$y = 0.05\sin (x + 15t)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Since, the general equation of wave,
$y=a \sin (k t+\omega t)$
Now, comparing the eq.(1) and (2) we get,
$k = 1,\lambda = 2\pi \left( {\because k = \frac{{2\pi }}{\lambda }} \right)$
and $\omega=15 \Rightarrow f=\dfrac{15}{2 \pi}(\because \omega=2 \pi f)$
Velocity of the wave,
$v = f\lambda = 2\pi \times \frac{{15}}{{2\pi }} = 15\,m/s$
As, we know the tension force in the string
$T=v^{2} m\left(\because v=\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{m}}\right)$
So, by substituting the values in the above relation, we get
$T = {(15)^2} \times {10^{ - 3}} = 0.225\,N$

question: A metallic wire of $1\,m$ length has a mass of $10 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,kg$. If a tension of $100\,N$ is applied to a wire, what is the speed of transverse wave ?
explanation:
Here,
Mass per unit length of the wire,
$\mu = \frac{{10 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}\,kg}}{{1\,m}} = {10^{ - 2}}kg{m^{ - 1}}$
Tension, $T = 100\,N$
The speed of transverse wave on the wire is
$v = \sqrt {\frac{T}{\mu }} = \sqrt {\frac{{100\,N}}{{{{10}^{ - 2}}kg{m^{ - 1}}}}} = 100\,m{s^{ - 1}}$

question: Two strings $A$ and $B$ of same material are stretched by same tension. The radius of the string $A$ is double the radius of string $B$. Transverse wave travels on string $A$ with speed $v_{A}$ and on string $B$ with speed $v_{B}$. The ratio $\dfrac{v_{A}}{v_{B}}$ is
explanation:
According to question, the velocity of wave travelling on string is given by
$\begin{aligned}& v=n \lambda=\dfrac{\lambda}{2 L} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}\left(\because n=\dfrac{1}{2 L} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}\right) \\& \therefore v=\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{m / l}} \Rightarrow v=\sqrt{\dfrac{T l}{m}}\end{aligned}$
and Young's modulus, $Y=\dfrac{T \times l}{A \Delta l}$
$\therefore T \times l=Y A \Delta L$
$\therefore v \propto \sqrt A \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} (\,A = {\text{ area }})$
For string $A$, radius is $2r$ and for string $B$ radius is $r$.
So, $\dfrac{v_{A}}{v_{B}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{4 r^{2}}{r^{2}}}=\sqrt{4}=2$
$[\because Y$ for both the string is same]

question: A $10\,m$ long steel wire has mass $5\,g$. If the wire is under a tension of $80\,N$, the speed of transverse waves on the wire is
explanation:
Here, Length, $L = 10\,m$
Mass, $M = 5g = 5 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,kg$
Tension, $T = 80\,N$
Mass per unit length of the wire is
$\mu = \frac{M}{L} = \frac{{5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}\,kg}}{{10\,m}} = 5 \times {10^{ - 4}}kg{m^{ - 1}}$
Speed of the transverse wave on the wire is
$\sqrt {\frac{T}{\mu }} = \sqrt {\frac{{80N}}{{5 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}kg{m^{ - 1}}}}} $
$ = 4 \times {10^2}\,m{s^{ - 1}} = 400\,m{s^{ - 1}}$

question: A uniform rope having mass \(m\) hangs vertically from rigid support. A transverse wave pulse is produced at the lower end. The speed \((v)\) of wave pulse varies with height \(h\) from the lower end as shown in figure
explanation:

i.e., $\quad T=\dfrac{m g}{l}(h)$
As $v=\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{(m / l)}}=\sqrt{g h}$
$v^{2}=g h$
Which is a parabola. Therefore, $h$ versus $v$ graph is a parabola option (2) is correct.

question:
Two uniform ropes of ratio of mass per unit length $\eta$ (first to the second rope) are hanging

from the rigid supports. The different masses are attached to the free end of the rope such that tension at point $A$ ( near to bottom) in the first rope and tension at $B$ ( near the top) in the second rope are equal. Find the ratio of wavelengths of the pulses reaching at point $A$ and $B$, If ratio of the respective frequencies of the pulses produced at lower ends is $\frac{1}{k}$
explanation:
$v=f \lambda=\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}} \quad T=\lambda^{2} f^{2} \mu$
$\begin{aligned}& \lambda_{1}^{2} f_{1}^{2} \dfrac{\mu_{1}}{\mu_{2}}=\lambda_{2}^{2} f_{2}^{2} \\& \dfrac{\lambda_{1}^{2}}{\lambda_{2}^{2}}=\dfrac{1}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{f_{2}}{f_{1}}\right)^{2} \quad\left[\text { As } \dfrac{\mu_{1}}{\mu_{2}}=\eta\right] \\& \dfrac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\eta}} \cdot \dfrac{f_{2}}{f_{1}}=\dfrac{k}{\sqrt{\eta}}\end{aligned}$

question: A string has a mass $10.0\,g$ and a length $L = 3\,m$. Its two ends are tied to two walls at a distance $D = 2\,m$ apart. Two blocks of mass $M = 2\,kg$ each are suspended from the string as shown in figure. In what time will a wave pulse sent from a point $A$ reach point $B$ ?
explanation:
$\mu = \frac{{10 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{3}\,kg/m = \frac{1}{{300}}\,kg/m$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951000/preptracker/questions/q_39967_explanation_1764951000337.png
From the given figure
$\begin{aligned}& \cos \theta=\dfrac{D / 3}{L / 4}=\dfrac{4}{3} \cdot \dfrac{2}{3}=\dfrac{8}{9} \\\therefore & \sin \theta=\dfrac{\sqrt{17}}{9}\end{aligned}$
Now, $T_{A B}=T \cos \theta$ and $T \sin \theta=20$
$\therefore \quad T_{A B}=20 \cot \theta=\dfrac{160}{\sqrt{17}} N=38.8 N$
$\therefore \quad$ Wave velocity in $AB = \sqrt {\frac{{{T_{AB}}}}{\mu }} = 107.89\,m/s$
$\therefore \,\,{\text{Time }} = \frac{{1.5}}{{107.89}}\sec = 13.9\,ms.$

question: A block of mass $M$ hangs from the lowest end of the string of mass $m$ and length $l$. A link is generated at $P$. The time taken by the kink to reach $Q$ is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950803/preptracker/questions/q_39781_explanation_1764950803239.png
The tension at a distance $\mathrm{x}$ from the block is
$\begin{aligned}& T=M g+\dfrac{m g}{l} x=\left(M+\dfrac{m x}{l}\right) g \\& \therefore \quad v=\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}=\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{M l}{m}+x\right) g} \\& \therefore \quad t=\int_{0}^{l} \dfrac{d x}{\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{M l}{m}+x\right) g}} \\& =\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{g}}\left[\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{M l}{m}+l\right) g}-\sqrt{\dfrac{M l}{m}}\right] \\& =2 \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{m g}}(\sqrt{M+m}-\sqrt{M})\end{aligned}$

question: A transverse wave propagating on a stretched string of linear density $3 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,kg - {m^{ - 1}}$ is represented by the equation $y=0.2 \sin (1.5 x+60 t)$. Where, $x$ is in meters and $t$ is in seconds. The tension in the string (in newton) is:
explanation:
Equation of wave,
$y=0.2 \sin (1.5 x+60 t)$
Comparing with standard equation, we get
$y=A \sin (k x+\omega t) \Rightarrow k=1.5 \text { and } \omega=60$
$\therefore {\text{Velocity}}\,{\text{of}}\,{\text{wave}},{\text{ }}v = \frac{\omega }{k} = \frac{{60}}{{1.5}} = 40\,m/s$
$\Rightarrow v=\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$
Where, $\mu$ is linear density and $T$ is tension in the string.
So, $T=v^{2} \mu=(40)^{2} \times 3 \times 10^{-4}=0.48 N$

question: A circular loop of string rotates about its axis on a frictionless horizontal plane at a uniform rate so that the tangential speed of any particle of the string is $v$. If a small transverse disturbance is produced at a point of the loop, speed (relative to the string) of this disturbance travel on the string is $x v$ then $x$ is
explanation:
Suppose $F$ is the tension in the string due to its rotation. Choose a small element of the string of length $l$. If $\mu$ is the mass per unit length of the string, then mass of the element,
$m=\mu l=\mu(R \theta)$

https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957617/preptracker/questions/q_39385_explanation_1764957617028.png
Using Newton's second law for the element, we have
$2 F \sin \theta / 2=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{R}$
For small $\theta, \sin \dfrac{\theta}{2} \simeq \dfrac{\theta}{2}$
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore 2 F \dfrac{\theta}{2}=\dfrac{m v^{2}}{R} \\& \therefore F=\mu v^{2}\end{aligned}$
The speed of the disturbance
$=\sqrt{\dfrac{F}{\mu}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu v^{2}}{\mu}}=v .$
So option (3) is correct.

question: A cylindrical tube open at the both ends has a fundamental frequency $f$ in air. The tube is dipped vertically in water, so that half of it is in water. The new fundamental frequency is
explanation:
We know that, fundamental frequency produced in cylindrical open tube
$f = \frac{v}{{2L}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
where $L=$ length of tube
When tube is dipped vertically in water so that half of its portion is in water, then it acts a closed pipe of length half of original length
$\therefore$ Length of closed pipe $=\dfrac{L}{2}$
So, fundamental frequency of this closed tube,
$f^{\prime}=\dfrac{v}{4\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)}=\dfrac{v}{2 L}=f \quad$ [from Eq.(1)]
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Closed Organ Pipes

question: Consider a gas with molar mass $m$. If the  sound at frequency $f$ is introduced to a tube  of this gas at temperature $T$, then an internal  acoustic standing wave is set-up with nodes   separated by $L$. The adiabatic constant $\left( {\gamma  = {C_p}/{C_V}} \right)$
explanation:
Nodes separated by distance
$L' = \lambda /2 = L$
As we know that,
$v = \sqrt {\frac{{\gamma p}}{\rho }} = \sqrt {\frac{{\gamma RT}}{m}} \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
$\& \quad v = f\lambda \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
where, $v$ is speed of sound and $\lambda $ is
wavelength $ = 2\,L.$
From Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$f\lambda = \sqrt {\frac{{\gamma RT}}{m}} $
On squaring both sides, we get
${f^2}{\lambda ^2} = \frac{{\gamma RT}}{m} \Rightarrow \gamma = \frac{{{f^2}{\lambda ^2}m}}{{RT}}$
$ = \frac{{{f^2}{{(2L)}^2}m}}{{RT}} = \frac{{4{f^2}{L^2}m}}{{RT}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Closed Organ Pipes

question: The difference between the fundamental frequencies of an open pipe and a closed pipe of same length is $100\,Hz$. The difference between the frequencies of the second harmonic of the open pipe and the third harmonic of the closed pipe is
explanation:
For open organ pipe, $f_{0}=\dfrac{v}{2 l}$
For closed organ pipe, $f_{c}=\dfrac{v}{4 l}$
$\therefore \dfrac{v}{2 l}-\dfrac{v}{4 l}=100 \Rightarrow \dfrac{v}{4 l}=100$
$\therefore {f_{2,0}} - {f_{3c}} = \frac{{2v}}{{2l}} - \frac{{3v}}{{4l}} = \frac{v}{{4l}} = 100\,Hz$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Open Organ Pipes

question: Two closed pipes when sounded simultaneously in their fundamental modes produce 6 beats per second. If the length of the shorter pipe is $150\,cm$, then the length of the longer pipe is (Speed of sound in air ${ = 336\,m{s^{ - 1}}}$)
explanation:
For closed organ pipe,
Fundamental frequency, $f=\dfrac{v}{4 l} \therefore f_{2}-f_{1}=6$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{v}{4 l_{1}}-\dfrac{v}{4 l_{2}}=6 \Rightarrow \dfrac{336}{4 \times 150 \times 10^{-2}}-\dfrac{336}{4 l_{2}}=6$
$\therefore {l_2} = \frac{{42}}{{25}}m = \frac{{42}}{{25}} \times 100 = 168\,cm$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Closed Organ Pipes

question:
Read the ${\rm{Assertion}}$ and ${\rm{Reason}}$ carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: At a non-rigid boundary, such as the open end of an organ pipe, the reflected wave undergoes a phase change of $\pi$.

Reason: A non-rigid boundary, like the open end of an organ pipe, does not fix the wave in place, allowing for a reflected wave without a phase change.
explanation: The assertion is false because, at a non-rigid boundary, the reflected wave does not undergo a phase change. The reason is actually true and it contradicts the assertion correctly, as it explains that at a non-rigid boundary like the open end of an organ pipe, the reflected wave maintains the same phase as the incident wave. Correct option is (4)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Assertion is false but Reason is true.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Open Organ Pipes

question: If the seventh harmonic of a closed pipe is in unison with fourth harmonic of an open organ pipe, then the ratio of length of closed pipe to that of open pipe is
explanation:
For closed organ pipe $v_{1}=\dfrac{v}{4 L_{1}}$
For ${7^{th{\text{ }}}}$ harmonic;
$7{v_1} = \frac{{7v}}{{4{L_1}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
For an open pipe, fundamental energy is
$v=\dfrac{v}{2 L_{2}}$
For ${7^{th{\text{ }}}}$ harmonic of open pipe
$4{v_2} = \frac{{4v}}{{2{L_2}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Therefore from Eqs. (1), and (2) we have
$7 v_{1}=4 v_{2}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{7 v}{4 L_{1}}=\dfrac{4 v}{2 L_{2}}=\dfrac{L_{1}}{L_{2}}=\dfrac{14}{16}=\dfrac{7}{8}=7: 8$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Open Organ Pipes

question: An organ pipe with both ends open has a length $L=2.5 {~cm}$. An extra hole is created at position $L / 2$. The lowest frequency of sound produced is (assume, speed of sound in air $=340 {~m} / {s}$ )
explanation:
We know that, there must be an antinode at the open end. Since, a hole has been made at the position $L / 2$, so there must be another antinode at $x=L / 2$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952600/preptracker/questions/q_109227_explanation_1764952600344.png For lowest frequency, $\lambda=L=25 {~cm}=0.15 {~m}$ Frequency of sound produced,
$f=\dfrac{v}{\lambda}=\dfrac{340}{0.25}=1360 {~Hz}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Open Organ Pipes

question: A pipe open at both ends has a fundamental frequency \(f\) in air. The pipe is now dipped vertically in a water drum to half of its length. The fundamental frequency of the air column is now equal to:
explanation:
Fundamental frequency of open pipe (at both ends)
$f = \frac{v}{{2L}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958701/preptracker/questions/q_109213_explanation_1764958700860.png Now immersed in water open pipe behaves as closed pipe.
$f' = \frac{v}{{4\left( {\frac{L}{2}} \right)}} = \frac{v}{{2L}}{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From eq's 1 & 2 $f = f'$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Open Organ Pipes

question: A closed organ pipe in ${9^{th{\text{ }}}}$ harmonic resonates with ${4^{th{\text{ }}}}$ harmonic of open organ pipe $\left[ {{L_{{\text{closed}}}} = 10\,cm} \right].$ Find length of open organ pipe.
explanation:
As ${9^{th{\text{ }}}}$ harmonic of closed organ pipe resonates with ${4^{th{\text{ }}}}$ harmonic of open organ pipe, then
$\dfrac{9 v_{s}}{4 L_{c}}=\dfrac{4}{2 L_{0}} v_{s}$
$\Rightarrow \quad L_{0}=\dfrac{8}{9} L_{c}$
$\therefore \quad {L_0} = \frac{{80}}{9}\,cm$
So, correct option is (4).
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Open Organ Pipes

question: The fundamental frequency of a closed organ pipe is equal to the first overtone frequency of an open organ pipe. If length of the open pipe is $60\,cm,$ the length of the closed pipe will be .
explanation:
Let ${l}$ and ${l^{\prime}}$ be the length of closed and open organ pipes respectively.
As given, fundamental frequency of closed organ pipe ${=}$ First overtone frequency of open organ pipe
${\Rightarrow \dfrac{v}{4 l}=\dfrac{2 v}{2 l^{\prime}}}$ where, ${v}$ is the velocity of sound.
${\therefore l=\dfrac{l^{\prime}}{4}}$
As, length of open organ pipe, ${l^{\prime}=60 {~cm}}$
${\therefore l=15 {~cm}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Open Organ Pipes

question: Two adjacent natural frequencies of an organ pipe are formed to be 550 $Hz$ and 650 $Hz$ . Calculate the length of this pipe.  (Velocity of sound in air $350 {~m} / {s}$ ).
explanation:
In case of open organ pipe we have all types of harmonic, even as well as odd. While in case of close organ pipe we have only odd harmonic.
The ratio of two adjacent frequencies
$\dfrac{f_{n}}{f_{n+1}}=\dfrac{550}{650}=\dfrac{50 \times 11}{50 \times 13}=\dfrac{11}{13}$
Hence the adjacent harmonic are 11th and 13th. Both harmonic are odd hence frequency it is the case of close organ pipe. Clearly, the fundamental frequency should be 50 Hz .
The fundamental frequency in case of close organ pipe
$f_{0}=\dfrac{v}{4 l} \Rightarrow 50=\dfrac{350}{4 \times l}$
Hence length of the pipe $l=\dfrac{350}{50 \times 4}=1.75 {~m}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Open Organ Pipes

question:
If in an experiment for determination of velocity of sound by resonance tube method using a tuning fork of $500\,Hz,$ first resonance was observed at $30.52\,cm$ and second was obtained at $64.13\,cm,$ then maximum possible error in velocity of sound is
(Take actual speed of sound $ = 332\,m/s$
explanation:
Using, $\lambda = 2\left( {{l_2} - {l_1}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow v' = 2f\left( {{l_2} - {l_1}} \right)$
$ = 2 \times 500(64.13 - 30.52)$
$ = 1000[33.61]cm/s$
$ = 336.1\,m/s$
Error in velocity,
$\Delta v = v' - v$
$ = (336.1 - 332)m/s = 4.1\,m/s$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Closed Organ Pipes

question: If the velocity of sound in air is ${300 {~m} / {s}}$, then the distance between two successive nodes of a stationary wave of frequency 1000 ${H_Z}$ is
explanation:
Here, ${v_S} = 300\,m\,{s^{ - 1}}v = 1000\,Hz$
$\therefore \quad \lambda = \frac{{{v_S}}}{{\dot v}} = \frac{{300}}{{1000}} = 0.3\,\,m$
Hence distance between two successive nodes
$ = \frac{\lambda }{2} = \frac{{0.3}}{2} = 0.15\,\,m$ or $15\,cm.$
So correct option is (3)
subject: Physics

question: A cylindrical tube open at both ends has a fundamental frequency $n$ in air. The tube is dipped vertically in water so that half of it is immersed in water. The fundamental frequency of air column is
explanation: When tube is open, $n = \frac{v}{{2\ell }}$, where $n$ is fundamental frequency of open organ pipe in air. When half of tube is dipped vertically in water, it behaves as a closed pipe of length $\dfrac{\ell}{2}$, so fundamental frequency $n$ in this case is $\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {n^\prime } = \frac{v}{{4(\ell /2)}} = \frac{v}{{2\ell }} = n$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Open Organ Pipes

question: A closed organ pipe and an open organ pipe are tuned to the same fundamental frequency. What is the ratio of their lengths?
explanation:
Avoiding end correction, the length of closed organ pipe is
$l_{1}=\dfrac{\lambda_{1}}{4}$
or$\lambda_{1}=4 l_{1}$
The length of open organ pipe is
$l_{2}=\dfrac{\lambda_{2}}{2} \quad \text { or } \quad \lambda_{2}=2 l_{2}$
Here, $\quad n_{1}=n_{2}$
(according to the question)
$\frac{v}{{{\lambda _1}}}\quad \quad \quad \quad = \frac{v}{{{\lambda _2}}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{v}{{4{l_1}}}\quad \quad \,\, = \frac{v}{{2{l_2}}}$
$\therefore \,\,\,{l_1}:{l_2}\quad \quad \, = 1:2$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Open Organ Pipes

question: A closed organ pipe of length $20\,cm$ is sounded with tuning fork in resonance. What is the frequency of tuning fork? (Take, speed of sound $ = 332\,m{s^{ - 1}}$ )
explanation:
If we adjust the length of air-column in closed organ pipe as such its any natural frequency equals to the frequency of tuning fork, then the amplitude of forced vibrations of air column increases very much. This is the state of resonance.At first resonance,
$l=\dfrac{\lambda}{4} \quad \Rightarrow \lambda=4 l$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950277/preptracker/questions/q_41784_explanation_1764950277145.png So, frequency of tuning fork,
$f=\dfrac{v}{\lambda}=\dfrac{v}{4 l}$
Given, $l = 20\,cm = 0.2\,m$
and $\quad v = 332\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
Hence, frequency,
$f = \frac{{332}}{{4 \times 0.2}} = 415\,Hz$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Closed Organ Pipes

question: A piston of filled in cylindrical pipe is pulled as shown in the figure. A tuning fork is sounded at open end and loudest sound is heard at open length $13\,cm,\,\,41\,cm$ and $69\,cm.$ The frequency of tuning fork, if velocity of sound is $350\,m{s^{ - 1}},$ is
explanation:
In a closed organ pipe in which length of air-column can be increased or decreased. The first resonance occurs at $\lambda / 4$ and second resonance occurs at $3 \lambda / 4$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950268/preptracker/questions/q_41780_explanation_1764950268291.png Thus, at first resonance
$\begin{equation*}\dfrac{\lambda}{4}=13 \tag{1}\end{equation*}$
and at second resonance
$\begin{equation*}\dfrac{3 \lambda}{4}=41 \tag{2}\end{equation*}$
Subtracting Eq. (1) from Eq. (2), we have
$\frac{{3\lambda }}{4} - \frac{\lambda }{4} = 41 - 13$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\frac{\lambda }{2} = 28$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\lambda = 56\,cm$
Hence, frequency of tuning fork
$n = \frac{v}{\lambda } = \frac{{350}}{{56 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}$
$ = 625\,Hz$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Closed Organ Pipes

question: An air column in a pipe which is closed at one end, will be in resonance with the vibrating body of frequency \[166\;Hz,\] if the length of the air column is
explanation:
When air is blown at the open end of a closed pipe, a longitudinal wave travels in the air of the pipe from open end to closed end. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950264/preptracker/questions/q_41772_explanation_1764950264088.png When $\lambda$ is the wavelength and $l$ be the length of pipe and $n$ is the frequency of note emitted and $v$ is the velocity of sound in air, then
$n=\dfrac{v}{\lambda}($ fundamental note $)$
Given, $\quad n = 166Hz,\quad v = 332\,m/s$
$\therefore \lambda=\dfrac{332}{166}=2 m$
But, $\quad \lambda=4 l$
$ \Rightarrow l = \frac{\lambda }{4} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5\,m$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Closed Organ Pipes

question: An organ pipe open on both ends in the $n$th harmonic is in resonance with a source of $1000\,Hz.$ The length of pipe is $16.6\,cm$ and speed of sound in air is  $332\,m/s.$ Find the value of $n$.
explanation:
Given, $f = 1000\,Hz,v = 332\,m/s,$
$l = 16.6\,cm = 16.6 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,m$
Frequency in an organ pipe open on both ends,
$f = \frac{{nv}}{{2l}}{\rm{ or }}\,\,\,\,n = \frac{{2fl}}{v}{\rm{ }}$
${\rm{or }}\quad n = \frac{{2 \times 1000 \times 16.6 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}{{332}} = 1$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Open Organ Pipes

question: An organ pipe of length ${L}$ is open at one end and closed at other end. The wavelengths of the three lowest resonating frequencies that can be produced by this pipe are
explanation:
${L=(2 n-1) \dfrac{\lambda}{4}}$
${\lambda=\dfrac{4 L}{(2 n-1)} ; \quad \lambda_{1}=\dfrac{4 L}{2(1)-1}=4 L}$
$\lambda_{2}=\dfrac{4 L}{4-1}=\dfrac{4 L}{3} ; \quad \lambda_{3}=\dfrac{4 L}{6-1}=\dfrac{4 L}{5}$
subject: Physics

question: The frequency of the first overtone of a closed pipe of length ${l_{c}}$ is equal to that of the third overtone of an open pipe of length ${l_{0}}$. The ratio ${l_{0} / l_{c}}$ will be
explanation:
Frequency of first overtone of closed organ pipes,
$n_{c}=\dfrac{3 v}{4 l_{c}}$
Frequency of ${3^{\text {rd }}}$ overtone of open organ pipe,
${n_{0}=\dfrac{4 v}{2 l_{0}}=\dfrac{2 v}{l_{0}}}$
${n_{c}=n_{0}}$ (Given)
${\Rightarrow \dfrac{3 v}{4 l_{c}}=\dfrac{2 v}{2 l_{0}}}$
${\Rightarrow \dfrac{l_{0}}{l_{c}}=\dfrac{8}{3}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Open Organ Pipes

question: The two nearest harmonics of a tube closed at one end and open at other end are $220\,Hz$ and $260\,Hz$. What is the fundamental frequency of the system?
explanation:
Difference between any two consecutive frequencies of
Closed organ pipe, ${\dfrac{2 v}{4 l}=260-220=40 {~Hz}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{v}{4 l}=20 {~Hz}$
So fundamental frequency ${=20 {~Hz}}$

question: An organ pipe ${P_{1}}$ closed at one end is vibrating in its first overtone, and another pipe ${P_{2}}$, open at both ends, is vibrating in its second overtone. Both organ pipes are in resonance. The ratio of length of pipes ${P_{1}}$ and ${P_{2}}$ is
explanation:
At resonance, first overtone of closed pipe ${=}$ second overtone of open pipe
${3 \times \dfrac{v}{4 L_{1}}=\dfrac{3}{2 L_{2}} v}$
$\dfrac{4}{4 L_{1}}=\dfrac{1}{2 L_{2}}$
$\dfrac{L_{1}}{L_{2}}=\dfrac{2}{4}=1: 2$

question:
Assertion: In the case of a stationary wave, a person hear a loud sound at the nodes as compared to the antinodes.

Reason: In a stationary wave all the particles of the medium vibrate in phase.
explanation: The person hears the loud sound at the nodes, not the antinodes, because sound perception is related to pressure variations. At the nodes, where displacement is zero and strain is maximum, the variation in pressure is also maximum due to the equation $P =  - E(dy/dt)$, causing in the perception of a loud sound. So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:

Reason: In the stationary wave,some particles of the medium remain permanently at rest.
explanation: The formation of stationary waves typically occurs in a bounded medium (though not too confined). At nodes,statement of reason is correct. So correct option is (2).

explanation: Sound from an open organ pipe can be richer due to the interaction with outside air entering from both ends. However, the reason does not directly explain why the sound is richer; it simply describes a characteristic of the open organ pipe. So correct option is (2).

question:
Assertion: The speed of sound in solids is maximum though their density is large.

Reason: The coefficient of elasticity of solid is large.
explanation: $v=\sqrt{\dfrac{Y}{\rho}}$ for solids. The speed of sound in a solid is indeed maximum even though their density is large, and this is because the coefficient of elasticity $Y$ of a solid is large, which is correctly explained in the reason. So correct option is (1)

question:
Assertion: When we start filling an empty bucket with water, the pitch of sound produced goes on decreasing.

Reason: The frequency of man voice is usually higher than that of woman.
explanation: A bucket, acting as a closed-end pipe, produces sound with a frequency (pitch) determined by $v / 4 \mathrm{~L}$, where $\mathrm{L}$ is the water depth. As the water level rises, $L$ decreases, and also the pitch increases. Women's voices generally have higher frequencies than men's. So correct option is (4).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: In a stationary wave, there is no transfer of energy.

Reason: There is no motion of the disturbance from one particle to adjoining particle in stationary wave.
explanation: Standing waves, unlike progressive waves, do not propagate in any specific direction. These stationary waves possess a total energy equal to twice the energy of each of the incident and reflected waves. In standing waves, a significant amount of energy is concentrated locally. So correct option is (2).

question: An open pipe and a closed pipe have same length. The ratio of $p^{\text {th }}$ overtones of two pipes is
explanation:
The frequencies of $p_{t h}$ overtones.
For open and closed pipes are
$f_{\text {open }}=\dfrac{n v}{2 L}=\dfrac{(p+1) v}{2 L}$
$f_{\text {closed }}=\dfrac{(2 n+1) v}{4 L}=\dfrac{(2 p+1) v}{4 L}$
$\dfrac{f_{\text {open }}}{f_{\text {closed }}}=\dfrac{2(p+1)}{(2 p+1)}$.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Open Organ Pipes

question: A pipe closed at one end has length $0.8\,m$. At its open end a $0.5\,m$ long uniform string is vibrating in its second harmonic and it resonates with the fundamental frequency of the pipe. If the tension in the wire in $50\,N$ and the speed of sound is $320\,m{\rm{/}}s$, the mass of the string used is
explanation:
Let, length of string, $l = 0.5\,m$
Length of pipe, $l = 0.8\,m$
As the frequency of string is vibrating in second harmonics
i.e., $v=\left(\dfrac{2}{2 l}\right) \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$
( $\mu=$ mass pen unit length of string)
Fundamental frequency of closed pipe is,
$f^{\prime}=\dfrac{v}{4 L}$. Here ' $v$ ' is speed of sound.
At resonance, $f=f^{\prime}$
$\Rightarrow\left(\dfrac{2}{2 l}\right) \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}=\dfrac{v}{4 L}$
$\Rightarrow\left(\dfrac{1}{0.5}\right) \sqrt{\dfrac{50}{\mu}}=\left(\dfrac{320}{4 \times 0.8}\right) \Rightarrow \dfrac{50}{\mu}=2500$
$ \Rightarrow \mu = \frac{1}{{50}}kg{\rm{/}}m$
mass of string, $m=\mu l=\dfrac{1}{50} \times 0.5$
$ \Rightarrow m = \left( {\frac{1}{{100}}} \right)kg = 10{\mkern 1mu} gm$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Closed Organ Pipes

question: If the speed of sound in air is $330\,m{\rm{/}}s$, then find the number of tones present in an open organ pipe of length $1\,m$ whose frequency is $ \le 1000\,Hz$.
explanation:
Given, speed of sound, $v = 330\,m{\rm{/}}s$
Length of open organ pipe, $l = 1\,m$
Frequency, $f = 1000\,Hz$
For open organ pipe,
Fundamental frequency,
${f_0} = \frac{v}{{2l}} = \frac{{330}}{{2 \times 1}} = 165\,Hz$
$\therefore$ Number of tones present in the open organ pipe
$=\dfrac{f}{f_{0}}=\dfrac{1000}{165}$
$=6.06 \simeq 6$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Open Organ Pipes

question: A closed organ pipe of length $1.2\,m$ vibrates in its first overtone mode. The pressure variation is maximum at
explanation:
In closed organ pipe, open end of the pipe behaves as an anti - node and a node is formed at the closed end in first overtone mode or third harmonics for $\mathrm{n}=2$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950166/preptracker/questions/q_41145_explanation_1764950165591.png $\Rightarrow l=\dfrac{3 \lambda}{4}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{\lambda }{4} = \frac{l}{3} = \frac{{1.2}}{3} = 0.4\,m$
Pressure variation will be maximum displacement nodes, i.e. at $0.4\,m$ from the open end.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Closed Organ Pipes

question: In the experiment to determine velocity of sound the length of air column in resonance with fork of frequency \[450\;Hz\] in fundamental mode is \[40\;cm\]. Ignoring end correction, the velocity of sound in air is
explanation:
In the first position of resonance, let length of air column is $l$,
$l_{1}=\dfrac{\lambda}{4}$
$\Rightarrow \lambda=4 l_{1}$
$\Rightarrow \lambda=4 \times(0.4)$
$ \Rightarrow \lambda = 1.6\,m$
Velocity of sound in air,
$v = f\lambda $
$ = 450 \times 1.6$
$ = 720\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Open Organ Pipes

question: The ratio of frequencies of fundamental harmonic produced by an open pipe to that of closed pipe having the same length is:

A resonance tube is old and has jagged end.
It is still used in the laboratory to determine velocity of sound in air. A tuning fork of frequency $512\,Hz$ produces first resonance. When the tube is filled with water to a mark 11 $cm$ below a reference mark, near the open end of the tube. The experiment is repeated with another fork of frequency $256\,Hz$ which produces first resonance when water reaches a mark $27\,cm$ below the reference mark. The velocity of sound in air, obtained in the experiment, is close to

question: The equation for the fundamental standing sound wave in a tube that is closed at both ends. If the tube is $80\,cm$ long and speed of the wave is 330 $m/s$ is (assume that amplitude of wave at antinode to be ${S_0})$

question: A tube of diameter $d$ and of length $l$ unit is open at both ends. Its fundamental frequency of resonance is found to be $f_{1}$. The velocity of sound in air is $330\,m/\sec $. One endof tube is now closed. The lowest frequency of resonance of tube is $f_{2}$. Taking into consideration of end correction, $\dfrac{f_{2}}{f_{1}}$ is :
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950102/preptracker/questions/q_40874_explanation_1764950101642.png
$\begin{aligned}& f_{1}=\dfrac{v}{2(l+0.6 d)}, f_{2}=\dfrac{v}{4(l+0.3 d)} \\& \dfrac{f_{2}}{f_{1}}=\dfrac{l+0.6 d}{2(l+0.3 d)}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: While measuring the speed of sound by performing a resonance column experiment a student gets the first resonance condition at a column length of \[18\;cm\] during winter. Repeating the same experiment during summer, she measures the coulmn length to be \[x\,cm\] for the, second resonance. Then
explanation:
In winter fundamental frequency
$f_{1}=\dfrac{v}{4 l_{1}}$
First overtone $f_{3}=\dfrac{3 v}{4 l_{3}}$
As $f_{1}=f_{3}$ or $\dfrac{v}{4 l_{1}}=\dfrac{3 v}{4 l_{3}}$
${l_3} = 3{l_1} = 54\,cm$
In summer $f_{3}^{\prime}=\dfrac{3 v^{\prime}}{4 x}$
As $f_{3}=f_{3}^{\prime}$
$f_3^\prime = \frac{{3v}}{{4(54\,cm)}} = \frac{{3{v^\prime }}}{{4x}}$
$x = \frac{{{v^\prime }}}{v}(54\,cm)$]
In summer ${v^\prime } > v \Rightarrow x > 54\,cm$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Closed Organ Pipes

question: A steel rod $100\,cm$ long is clamped at is middle. The fundamental frequency of longitudinal vibrations of the rod are given to be $2.53\,kHz$. What is the speed of sound in steel?
explanation:
In fundamental mode, $l=2\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4}\right)=\dfrac{\lambda}{2}$
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950093/preptracker/questions/q_40795_explanation_1764950092730.png
$\Rightarrow \lambda=2 l$
Given $l = 100\,cm,f = 2.53kHz$
Using $v=f \lambda$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} v = 2.53 \times {10^3} \times 2 \times 100 \times {10^{ - 2}}$
$v = 5.06\,km/s$
subject: Physics

question: If the end correction of an open pipe is $0.8\,cm$, then the inner radius of that pipe will be
explanation:
For an open organ pipe, the relation between end correction and inner radius of the organ pipe is given by
$\Delta l=1.2 \times r$
[ $\Delta l=$ end correction, $r = $ inner radius $]$
So, $r=\dfrac{\Delta l}{1.2}=\dfrac{0.8}{1.2}[\because \Delta l=0.8]$
$ = \frac{2}{3}\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: The closed and open organ pipes have same length. When they are vibrating simultaneously in first overtone, produce three beats. The length of open pipe is made $\dfrac{1}{3} r d$ and closed pipe is made three times the original, the number of beats produced will be
explanation:
For open pipe first overtone
$v_{1}=\dfrac{v}{L}$
For closed pipe first overtone,
${v_1} = \frac{{3v}}{{4L}}$
$\therefore {v_1} - {v_1} = \frac{v}{L} - \frac{{3v}}{{4L}} = 3$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{v}{{3L}} = 3$
$\therefore \frac{v}{L} = 9\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
When length of open pipe is made $\dfrac{L}{3}$, then fundamnetal frequency
$v=\dfrac{v}{2\left(\dfrac{L}{3}\right)}=\dfrac{3 v}{2 L}$
When length of closed pipe is made 3 times, then fundamental frequency
$v^{\prime}=\dfrac{v}{4(3 L)}=\dfrac{3 v}{12 L}$
Beats produced $v = v'$,
$=\dfrac{3 v}{2 L}-\dfrac{v}{12 L}=\dfrac{17}{12} \dfrac{v}{L}$
$=\dfrac{17}{12} \times 12 \quad \because[$ from eq. $(1)]$
$=17$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Open Organ Pipes

question: The fundamental frequency of an air column in a pipe closed at one end is \[100\;Hz\]. If the same pipe is open at both the ends, the frequencies produced in \[Hz\] are
explanation:
For a closed pipe fundamental frequency,
${v_1} = \frac{v}{{4L}} = 100\,Hz$
For an open pipe fundamental frequency
${v_1} = \frac{v}{{2L}} = 200\,Hz$
In a pipe open at both the ends, all multiples of the fundamental are produced.
So option (4) is correct
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Open Organ Pipes

question: A pipe closed at one end has length \[83\;cm\]. The number of possible natural oscillations of air column whose frequencies lie below \[1000\;Hz\] are ( take, velocity of sound in air \[ = 332\;m/s\])
explanation:
Fundamental frequency of one end closed pipe,
$v=\dfrac{n v}{4 L}$
Here, $n = 1$
$\text { or } v_{1}=\dfrac{332}{4 \times 83 \times 10^{-2}}$
or $v_{1}=100$
As odd harmonics alone are produced in closed organ pipe, therefore possible frequencies are
$3{v_1} = 300\,Hz,5{v_2} = 500\,Hz$
$7{v_2} = 700\,Hz,9{v_2} = 900\,Hz$
Hence, the number of possible natural oscillations of air column is 5
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Closed Organ Pipes

question: A pipe open at both ends and a pipe closed at one end have same length. The ratio of frequencies of their ${p^{th}}$ overtone is
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Open Organ Pipes

question: The fundamental frequency of a closed pipe is $400\,Hz.$ If $\dfrac{1}{3} r d$ pipe is filled with water, then the frequency of ${2^{nd}}$ harmonic of the pipe will be (neglect end correction)
explanation:
Fundamental frequency of closed pipe
$n=\dfrac{v}{4 L}=400$
$v=400 \times 4 L$
If $\dfrac{1}{3} r d$ of pipe is filled with water, then remaining length of air column is $L-\dfrac{L}{3}=\dfrac{2 L}{3}$
Now, fundamental frequency
$=\dfrac{v}{4\left(\dfrac{2 L}{3}\right)}=\dfrac{3 v}{8 L}$
The second harmonic of the pipe $ = 3x$ fundamental frequency
$ = 3 \times \left( {\frac{{3v}}{{8L}}} \right) = 3 \times \frac{{3 \times 400 \times 4L}}{{8L}} = 1800\,Hz$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Closed Organ Pipes

question: An open organ pipe and a closed organ pipe have the frequency of their first overtone identical. The ratio of length of open pipe to that of closed pipe is
explanation:
The frequency higher than the fundamental frequency of sound is known as overtone For open organ pipe, first overtone is
$\left(v_{0}\right)=\dfrac{2 v}{2 L_{0}}=\dfrac{v}{L_{0}}$
For closed organ pipe, first overtone is
$v_{c}=\dfrac{3 v}{4 L_{c}}$
It is given that, their first overtone are identical i.e., $v_{o}=v_{c}$
$\dfrac{v}{L_{o}}=\dfrac{3 v}{4 L_{c}}$
$\dfrac{L_{o}}{L_{c}}=\dfrac{4}{3}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Open Organ Pipes

question: First overtone frequency of a closed pipe of length $l_{1}$ is equal to the $2^{\text {nd }}$ harmonic frequency of an open pipe of length $l_{2}$. The ratio $l_{1} / l_{2}=$
explanation:
First overtone frequency (i.e., $3^{\text {rd }}$ harmonic) of the closed pipe of length $l_{1}$ is
$f_{C}=\dfrac{3 v}{4 l_{1}}$ (where $v$ is the speed of sound in air)
$2^{\text {nd }}$ harmonic frequency of the open pipe of length $l_{2}$ is
$f_{O}=\dfrac{2 v}{2 l_{2}}=\dfrac{v}{l_{2}}$
As per question $f_{C}=f_{O}$
$\therefore \dfrac{3 v}{4 l_{1}}=\dfrac{v}{l_{2}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{l_{1}}{l_{2}}=\dfrac{3}{4}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Open Organ Pipes

question:
A cylindrical tube open at both the ends has a fundamental frequency of \[390\;Hz\] in air. If
\(1 / 4^{\text {th }}\) of the tube is immersed vertically in water the fundamental frequency of air column is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950059/preptracker/questions/q_40590_explanation_1764950058912.png
For a cylindrical tube open at both ends, its fundamental frequency is
$f = \frac{v}{\lambda } = \frac{v}{{2L}} = 390\,Hz\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
where $v$ is the velocity of sound in air.
If $(1 / 4)^{\text {th }}$ of cylindrical tube is immersed in a water, it will become a closed pipe of length three fourth that of an open pipe as shown in figure (ii). Therefore, its fundamental
frequency is
${f^\prime } = \frac{v}{{{\lambda ^\prime }}} = \frac{v}{{\left( {4 \times \frac{3}{4}\,L} \right)}}\,$
$ \Rightarrow {f^\prime } = \frac{v}{{3L}} = \frac{2}{3}\left( {\frac{v}{{2L}}} \right) = \frac{2}{3} \times 390\,Hz$ [using eq. (1)]
$ = 260\,Hz.$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Standing Waves (Resonance) in Open Organ Pipes

question: The waves set up in a closed pipe are
explanation: Waves in a closed pipe are stationary longitudinal.
