question: A rod of length $L$ and negligible mass is suspended by two identical strings $A B$ and $C D$ as shown in the figure. A mass $M$ is suspended from point $O$ which is at a distance $x$ from $B$. If the frequency of the first harmonic of $A B$ is equal to the frequency of the second harmonic of $C D$, then the value of $x$ is
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952595/preptracker/questions/q_109250_explanation_1764952594798.png Frequency of ${1^{st{\text{ }}}}$ harmonics of $A B=$
Frequency of ${2^{nd{\text{ }}}}$ harmonics of $C D$.
Let $T_{A B}$ is tension in string $A B . T_{C D}$ is the tension in string $C D$.
Frequency of 1 st harmonics of $A B$ is given by
$f=\dfrac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\dfrac{T_{A B}}{m}}, l=$ length of string (1)
Frequency of 2nd harmonics of $C D$
$f = \frac{2}{{2l}}\sqrt {\frac{{{T_{CD}}}}{m}} = \frac{1}{l}\sqrt {\frac{{{T_{CD}}}}{m}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Eq. (1) $=$ Eq. (2) (given)
$\dfrac{1}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{T_{A B}}{m}}=\dfrac{1}{l} \sqrt{\dfrac{T_{C D}}{m}}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad {T_{AB}} = 4{T_{CD}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
or rotational equilibrium of massless rod, taking torque at point $O$.
$\tau=r \times F$ (Here $F=$ Tension)$T_{A B} \times(X)=T_{C D}(L-X)$
Put value of $T_{A B}$, then$X=\dfrac{L}{5}$
subject: Physics

question: Two waves of amplitudes $A_{1}$ and $A_{2}$ respectively, are superimposed. The ratio between the maximum and minimum intensities of the resultant waves is $9: 4$. The value of $A_{2} / A_{1}$ is (assume $A_{1}>A_{2}$ )
explanation:
The ratio between the maximum and minimum intensities of the resultant wave,
$I_{\text {max }}: I_{\text {min }}=9: 4$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\dfrac{9}{4} \Rightarrow \dfrac{\left(A_{1}+A_{2}\right)^{2}}{\left(A_{1}-A_{2}\right)^{2}}=\left(\dfrac{3}{2}\right)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{A_{1}^{2}\left(1+\dfrac{A_{2}}{A_{1}}\right)^{2}}{A_{1}^{2}\left(1-\dfrac{A_{2}}{A_{1}}\right)^{2}}=\left(\dfrac{3}{2}\right)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\left(1+\dfrac{A_{2}}{A_{1}}\right)^{2}}{\left(1-\dfrac{A_{2}}{A_{1}}\right)^{2}}=\left(\dfrac{3}{2}\right)^{2} \Rightarrow \dfrac{1+\dfrac{A_{2}}{A_{1}}}{1-\dfrac{A_{2}}{A_{1}}}=\dfrac{3}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{A_{1}+A_{2}}{A_{1}-A_{2}}=\dfrac{3}{2}$
$\Rightarrow 2 A_{1}+2 A_{2}=3 A_{1}-3 A_{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 5 A_{2}=A_{1}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{A_{2}}{A_{1}}=\dfrac{1}{5}=0.20$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Resultant Amplitude and Intensity in Interference of Waves

question: The amplitude of the wave resulting from the superposition of three waves given by $x_{1}=A \cos \omega t, x_{2}=2 A \sin \omega t$ and $x_{3}=\sqrt{2} A \cos \left(\omega t+\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)$ is
explanation:
Given, $x_{1}=A \cos \omega t, x_{2}=2 A \sin \omega t$
and $x_{3}=\sqrt{2} A \cos \left(\omega t+\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)$
According to superposition principle, resultant wave is given as
$x=x_{1}+x_{2}+x_{3}$
$=A \cos \omega t+2 A \sin \omega t+\sqrt{2} A \cos \left(\omega t+\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)$
$=\sqrt{5} A\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \cos \omega t+\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{5}} \sin \omega t\right)$
$+\sqrt{2} A \cos \left(\omega t+\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)$
$=\sqrt{5} A(\cos \phi \cos \omega t+\sin \phi \sin \omega t)$
$+\sqrt{2} A \cos \left(\omega t+\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)$
$x = \sqrt 5 A\cos (\omega t - \phi ) + \sqrt 2 A\cos \left( {\omega t + \frac{\pi }{4}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952593/preptracker/questions/q_109233_explanation_1764952593286.png Phase difference between above two waves,
$\phi^{\prime}=\left(\omega t+\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)-(\omega t-\phi) \Rightarrow \phi^{\prime}=\dfrac{\pi}{4}+\phi$
$\Rightarrow \quad \cos \phi^{\prime}=\cos \left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}+\phi\right)$
$=\cos \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cos \phi-\sin \dfrac{\pi}{4} \sin \phi$
$=\cos \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cos \phi-\sin \dfrac{\pi}{4} \sin \phi$
$\cos {\phi ^\prime } = - \frac{1}{{\sqrt {10} }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From Eq. (1), amplitude of resultant wave,
$A=\sqrt{(\sqrt{5} A)^{2}+(\sqrt{2} A)^{2}+2 \sqrt{5} A \times \sqrt{2} A \cos \phi^{\prime}}$
$=\sqrt{5 A^{2}+2 A^{2}+2 \sqrt{10} A^{2} \times\left(\dfrac{-1}{\sqrt{10}}\right)} \quad$
[from Eq.(2)]
$=\sqrt{5 A^{2}}=\sqrt{5} A$
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Resultant Amplitude and Intensity in Interference of Waves

question: Standing waves are produced in a string $16\,m$ long. If there are 9 nodes between the two fixed ends of the string and the speed of the wave is $32 {~m} / {s}$, what is the frequency of the wave?
explanation:
Total number of segments of vibrations between fixed ends, $p=9+l=10$
$\therefore$ frequency of the wave,
$f=\dfrac{p}{2 L} \times v=\dfrac{10}{2 \times 16} \times 32=10 {~Hz}$
subject: Physics

question: A string of length $100\,cm$ has three resonant frequencies, $120 {~Hz}, 200 {~Hz}$ and $280\,Hz$. If a node is formed at the end of the string, the speed of the transverse wave in this string is:
explanation:
$\because$ Fundamental frequency $\left(f_{0}\right)$ is equal to the greatest common factor of $f_{1}, f_{2}$ and ${f_3}.$
$\therefore {f_0} = $ greatest common factor of $120 {~Hz}, 200 {~Hz}$ and $280\,Hz$ $i.e.,$ $f_{0}=40 {~Hz}$
Now, we know that, speed of transverse wave is given by $v=2 l f_{0}=2 \times 1 \times 40=80 {~m} / {s}$.
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics

question: Two identical sinusoidal waves are moving in the same direction along a stretched string, interfere with each other. The phase difference between them is $120^{\circ}$. The amplitudes of both the waves are same. If the amplitude of the resultant wave due to interference is $2\,mm$, then the amplitude of each wave is
explanation:
$\because \quad A=\sqrt{a_{1}^{2}+a_{2}^{2}+2 a_{1} a_{2} \cos 120^{\circ}}$
$\Rightarrow 2=\sqrt{a^{2}+a^{2}+2 a^{2}\left(-\dfrac{1}{2}\right)}$
$\Rightarrow 2=\sqrt{a^{2}+a^{2}-a^{2}}=\sqrt{a^{2}}=a$ or $a=2 {~mm}$
Hence, the amplitude of each wave is $2\,mm$.
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Resultant Amplitude and Intensity in Interference of Waves

question: The speed of a transverse wave on a string is $160 {~m} / {s}$. If the three resonant frequencies of this string respectively are $160 {~Hz}, 240 {~Hz}$ and $400\,Hz$, then the length of the string is
explanation:
Resonant frequencies are 160,240 and $400\,Hz$,
so their simplest ratios are 160:240:400.
$i.e.$ $2: 3: 5$
Hence, 160 is second harmonic
$\Rightarrow 160=\dfrac{n v}{2 L}$, where $n=2$
Substituting $n=2$ and $v=160 {~ms}^{-1}$, we get
$L=\dfrac{2 \times 160}{2 \times 160}=1 {~m}$
So, string length is $1 {~m}=100 {~cm}$.
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics

question: Two wires are fixed on a sonometer wire. Their tensions are in the ratio $8: 1$, the lengths in the ratio $36: 35$, the diameters in the ratio $4: 1$ and the densities in the ratio $1: 2$. Find the frequency of the beats  produced if the note of the higher pitch has frequency of 360 $Hz$ .
explanation:
For the two wires, we have
$n_{1}=\dfrac{1}{2 l_{1}} \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{T_{2}}{\pi r_{2}^{2} \rho_{2}}\right)} \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{T_{1}}{\pi r_{1}^{2} \rho_{1}}\right)}$
and $n_{2}=\dfrac{1}{2 l_{2}} \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{T_{2}}{\pi r_{2}^{2} \rho_{2}}\right)}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{n_{1}}{n_{2}}=\dfrac{l_{2}}{l_{1}} \sqrt{\left[\dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}\left(\dfrac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right)^{2} \dfrac{\rho_{2}}{\rho_{1}}\right]}$
According to the given problem
$\dfrac{l_{3}}{l_{1}}=\dfrac{36}{35} ; \dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}=\dfrac{8}{1} ; \dfrac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}=\dfrac{4}{1}$
and $\dfrac{\rho_{1}}{\rho_{2}}=\dfrac{1}{2}$
Substituting these values in Eq. (1)
or
$\frac{{{n_1}}}{{{n_2}}} = \frac{{35}}{{36}}\sqrt {\left[ {\left( {\frac{8}{1}} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{{16}}} \right) \cdot \left( {\frac{2}{1}} \right)} \right]} = \frac{{35}}{{36}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
It is clear from Eq. (2) that, has higher pitch so, $n_{2}=360$
Now $\dfrac{n_{1}}{360}=\dfrac{35}{36}$ or $n_{1}=350 {~Hz}$
Thus beat frequency is given as
$\Delta n=360-350=10 {~Hz}$
subject: Physics

question: Two metallic strings $A$ and $B$ of different materials are connected in series forming a joint. The strings have similar cross-sectional area $a=1 {~mm}^{2}$. The length of $A$ is $l_{A}=0.3 {~m}$ and that $B$ is $l_{B}=0.75 {~m}$. One end of the combined string is tied with a support rigidly and the other end is loaded with a block of mass $m=25.2 {~kg}$ passing over a frictionless pulley. Transverse waves are set up in the combined string using an external source of variable frequency, calculate the lowest frequency for which standing waves are observed such that the joint is a node. The densities of $A$ and $B$ are $6.3 \times 10^{3} {~kg} / {m}^{3}$ and $2.8 \times 10^{3} {~kg} / {m}$ respectively.
explanation:
Let $p=$ number of loops formed in string $A$ and $q=$ number of loops formed in string $B$
Both $p$ and $q$ will be integer numbers, as the one end is fixed rigidly, the other end is loaded and the joint $P$ is a node.
For string $A, n_{A}=\dfrac{p}{2 l_{A}} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu_{A}}}=\dfrac{p}{2 l_{A}} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{a \rho_{A}}}$
For string $B, n_{B}=\dfrac{q}{2 l_{B}} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu_{B}}}=\dfrac{q}{2 l_{B}} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{a \rho_{B}}}$
As both the strings are to be exerted by the same source of variable frequency, hence $n_{A}=n_{B}$.
$\frac{p}{{2{l_A}}}\sqrt {\frac{T}{{a{\rho _A}}}} = \frac{q}{{2{l_B}}}\sqrt {\frac{T}{{a{\rho _B}}}} $
$\frac{p}{q} = \frac{{{l_A}}}{{{l_B}}}\sqrt {\frac{{{d_A}}}{{{d_B}}}} = \frac{{0.3}}{{0.75}}\sqrt {\frac{{6.3 \times {{10}^3}}}{{2.8 \times {{10}^3}}}} $
$ = \frac{2}{5}\sqrt {\frac{9}{4}} = \frac{2}{5} \times \frac{3}{2} = \frac{3}{5}$
${n_{\min }} = \frac{3}{{2{l_A}}}\sqrt {\frac{T}{{a{\rho _A}}}} $
$ = \frac{3}{{2 \times 0.3}}\sqrt {\frac{{25.2}}{{{{10}^{ - 6}} \times 6.3 \times {{10}^3}}}} = 2000\,Hz$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Reflection of Waves at the Boundary of Two Strings

question: An object of mass $m$ is hung from a thin taut string (with linear mass density $\mu=2 {~g} / {m}$ ) that passes over a light pulley as shown in figure. The string is connected to a vibrator which can be vibrated with constant frequency $f$, and the length of the string between vibrator and the pulley is $L=2.0 {~m}$. The standing waves are formed when the mass $m$ of the object is either 16.0 $kg$ or 25.0 $kg$ . It is observed that no standing waves are formed with any mass between these values, however. What is the largest object mass  for which standing waves could be observed? (Use $g=9.8 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ )
explanation:
Let $p$ be the number of loops in the standing wave resulting from the $25.0-{kg}$ mass. Then $p+1$ is the number of loops for the standing wave resulting from the $16.0-{kg}$ mass.
The wavelength for standing waves in similar end condition, $\lambda=\dfrac{2 L}{n}$ and the frequency is $f=\dfrac{v}{\lambda}$.
The frequency does not change as the masses are changed.
Thus, $f=\dfrac{p}{2 L} \sqrt{\dfrac{T_{p}}{\mu}}$ and also $f=\dfrac{p+1}{2 L} \sqrt{\dfrac{T_{n+1}}{\mu}}$
Equating the expressions for $f$, we have
$\dfrac{p+1}{p}=\sqrt{\dfrac{T_{p}}{T_{p+1}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{25.0 \times g}{16.0 \times g}}=\dfrac{5}{4}$
Therefore, $4 p+4=5 p$, or $p=4$. Using either expression for $f$,we find
$f=\dfrac{4}{2 \times 2.0} \sqrt{\dfrac{25.0 \times 9.8}{0.00200}}=350 {~Hz}$
The tension in the string for $n$ loops formed, $T_{n}=m g$
Hence frequency,
$f = \frac{n}{{2L}}\sqrt {\frac{{{T_n}}}{\mu }} = \frac{n}{{2L}}\sqrt {\frac{{mg}}{\mu }} $
$ \Rightarrow m = \frac{{4{L^2}{f^2}\mu }}{{{n^2}g}}\quad \quad \quad (1)$
For largest mass $m$, the number of loops should be least, $n=1$
Hence, $m=\dfrac{4(2.0)^{2}(350)^{2} \times 0.00200}{(1)^{2} \times 9.80}=400 {~kg}$
subject: Physics

question: A string vibrates with a frequency 200 $Hz$ . Its length is doubled and its tension is altered until it begins to vibrate with frequency 300 $Hz$ . What is the ratio of the new tension to the original tension?
explanation:
Let $f_{1}$ and $f_{2}$ be the frequencies in two cases and $L_{1}, L_{2}$ and $T_{1}, T_{2}$ be respectively the corresponding lengths of the string and tensions under which it is vibrating.
$f_{1}=\dfrac{1}{2 L_{1}} \sqrt{\dfrac{T_{1}}{\mu}}$
and $f_{2}=\dfrac{1}{2 L_{2}} \sqrt{\dfrac{T_{2}}{\mu}}$
Thus, $\dfrac{f_{1}}{f_{2}}=\dfrac{L_{2}}{L_{1}} \sqrt{\dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}}$ or $\dfrac{200}{300}=2 \sqrt{\dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}}$$\left(f_{1}=200, f_{2}=300\right.$ and $L_{2}=2 L_{1}$ or $\left.L_{2} / L_{1}=2\right)$
or $\dfrac{2}{3}=2 \sqrt{\dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}}$ or $\dfrac{4}{9}=4 \dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}$ or $\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}=9$
subject: Physics

question: A transverse harmonic wave is propagating along a taut string. Tension in the string is 50 $N$ and its linear mass density is $0.02 {~kg} {~m}^{-1}$. The string is driven by an 80 $Hz$ oscillator tied to one end oscillating with an amplitude of 1 $mm$ . The other end of the string is terminated so that all the wave energy is absorbed and there is no reflection. Calculate the average energy of the wave (in $mJ$ ) on a 1.0 $m$ long segment of the string. (Use $\pi^{2}=10$ )
explanation:
Wave speed $v = \sqrt {\frac{T}{\mu }} = \sqrt {\frac{{50}}{{0.02}}} = \sqrt {2500} = 50\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
Power of oscillator $=$ average power transmitted through a cross section $=\dfrac{1}{2} \mu \omega^{2} a^{2} v$
$\therefore P = 2{\pi ^2}\mu {f^2}{a^2}v\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
$ \Rightarrow P = 2 \times 10 \times (0.02) \times {(80)^2}$
$ \times {\left( {1 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}} \right)^2} \times 50$
$ = 0.128\,W$
Average energy on a segment of length $L$
$=($ Average power $) \times$ (time required for the wave to travel a distance $L$ )
${E_{av}} = {P_{av}}\frac{L}{v} = 0.128 \times \frac{{1.0}}{{50}}$
$ = 0.00256\,J = 2.56\,mJ$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Energy/Power transmission through a wave on string

question:
An aluminium wire of length ${l_1} = 60.0\,cm$ of cross-sectional area $1.00 \times {10^{ - 2}}\,c{m^2}$ is connected to a steel wire of the same cross-sectional area. The compound wire, loaded with a block $m$ of mass $10.0\,kg$ (as in fig), so that the distance $l_{2}$ from the joint to the supporting pulley is $86.6\,cm$. Transverse waves are set up in the wire by using an external source of variable frequency. What is the lowest frequency of excitation for which standing waves are observed such that the joint in the wire is a node?
(Take $g = 1000\,cm{\rm{/}}{s^2}\,{\rho _{Al}} = 2.6\,g/c{m^3};$ ${\rho _{steel}} = 7.8\,g{\rm{/}}c{m^3}$)
explanation:
Distance between two nodes $ = \frac{\lambda }{2}$.
Let there are ${P}_{1}$ loops in Aluminium wire and ${P}_{2}$ loops in steel part of wire.
Allowed frequencies of Aluminium part:
${f_{AI}} = \frac{{{P_1}}}{{2{l_1}}}\sqrt {\frac{T}{{{\mu _{Al}}}}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Allowed frequencies of steel part:
${f_{steel{\rm{ }}}} = \frac{{{P_2}}}{{2{l_2}}}\sqrt {\frac{T}{{{\mu _{steel{\rm{ }}}}}}} {\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Area of croos-section $=1 \times 10^{-2} {~cm}^{2}$
$\mu_{{Al}}=(2.6) \times\left(1 \times 10^{-2}\right)=0.026 {~g} / {cm}$
$[\because \mu=\rho \times A]$
$\mu_{\text {steel }}=(7.8) \times\left(1 \times 10^{-2}\right)=0.078 {~g} / {cm}$
Equating (1) and (2).
$\frac{{{P_1}}}{{2 \times 60}}\sqrt {\frac{T}{{0.026}}} = \frac{{{P_2}}}{{2 \times 86.6}}\sqrt {\frac{T}{{0.078}}} $
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{P_1}}}{{{P_2}}} = \frac{{2 \times 60 \times 2}}{{2 \times \sqrt 3 \times 100}}\sqrt {\frac{T}{{0.078}} \times \frac{{0.026}}{T}} $
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left[ {{\rm{ Take }}\,86.6 = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} \times 100} \right]$
$ = {\rm{ }}\frac{{2 \times 60}}{{\sqrt 3 \times 100 \times \sqrt 3 }} = \frac{2}{5}$
The lowest frequency of excitation causes ${P}_{1}=2, {P}_{2}=5$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956279/preptracker/questions/q_41980_explanation_1764956279405.png Lowest frequency $=\dfrac{2}{2 \times 60} \sqrt{\dfrac{1000 \times 10^{4}}{0.026}}$
$[\because T=M g]$
$ = \frac{{{{10}^5}}}{{60\sqrt {26} }} = \frac{{{{10}^5}}}{{60 \times 5.1}}$
$ = 326.8\,Hz$
subject: Physics

question: Two vibrating strings of the same material but lengths $L$ and $2 L$ have radii $2 r$ and $r$ respectively. They are stretched under the same tension. Both the strings vibrate in their fundamental modes, the one of length $L$ with frequency $v_{1}$ and the other with frequency $v_{2}$. The ratio $v_{1} / v_{2}$ is
explanation:
Fundamental frequency,
$v=\dfrac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{m}}=\dfrac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\rho r^{2}}}\left[\because \rho=\dfrac{m}{r^{2}}\right]$
$\Rightarrow v \propto \dfrac{1}{l r}$
$\therefore \dfrac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}=\dfrac{l_{2} r_{2}}{l_{1} r_{1}}$
$=\dfrac{2 L \times r}{L \times 2 r}=1$
subject: Physics

question: The ratio of intensities of two waves is 16:9. If they produce interference, then the ratio of maximum and minimum will be
explanation:
Since, $\dfrac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}=\dfrac{16}{9}=\dfrac{a_{1}^{2}}{a_{2}^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{16}{9}}=\dfrac{4}{3}$
$\Rightarrow \quad a_{1}=\dfrac{4}{3} a_{2}$
$\therefore \quad \dfrac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\dfrac{\left(a_{1}+a_{2}\right)^{2}}{\left(a_{1}-a_{2}\right)^{2}}=\dfrac{\left(\dfrac{4}{3} a_{2}+a_{2}\right)^{2}}{\left(\dfrac{4}{3} a_{2}-a_{2}\right)}$
$=49: 1$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Resultant Amplitude and Intensity in Interference of Waves

question: Two waves having intensities $25: 9$ produce interference. The ratio maximum to minimum intensity is equal to
explanation:
$\dfrac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}=\dfrac{a_{1}^{2}}{a_{2}^{2}}=\dfrac{25}{9}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{25}{9}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{a_{1}}{a_{2}}=\dfrac{5}{3}$
$\begin{aligned}\therefore \quad \dfrac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }} & =\dfrac{\left(a_{1}+a_{2}\right)^{2}}{\left(a_{1}-a_{2}\right)^{2}}=\dfrac{(5+3)^{2}}{(5-3)^{2}} \\& =\dfrac{(8)^{2}}{(2)^{2}}=\dfrac{64}{4}=\dfrac{16}{1} \\I_{\max }: I_{\min } & =16: 1\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Resultant Amplitude and Intensity in Interference of Waves

question: Two waves are passing through a region in the same direction at the same time. If the equations of these waves are $y_{1}=a \sin \dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda}(v t-x)$ and $y_{2}=b \sin \dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda}\left[(v t-x)+x_{0}\right]$, then the amplitude of the resulting wave for $x_{0}=(\lambda / 2)$ is
explanation:
Let $\phi_{1}$ and $\phi_{2}$ represent angles of the first and second waves, then
$\phi_{2}=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda}\left[(v t-x)+x_{0}\right]$
and $\quad \phi_{1}=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda}(v t-x)$
But $\quad x_{0}=\dfrac{\lambda}{2} \Rightarrow \phi_{2}-\phi_{1}=\pi$
Hence, phase difference $\phi=\pi$, so amplitude of the resultant wave is
$R = \sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + 2ab\cos \phi } $
$ = \sqrt {{a^2} + {b^2} + 2ab\cos \pi } $
$ = \sqrt {{{(a - b)}^2}} = a - b$
$\therefore \,\,R = |a - b|$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Resultant Amplitude and Intensity in Interference of Waves

question: The speed of transverse waves in a stretched string is ${700 {~cm} / {s}}$. It the string is $2\,m$ long, the frequency with which it resonates in fundamental mode is
explanation:
${v=700 {~cm} / {s}=7 {~m} / {s}}$
For one loop, ${l=\lambda / 2}$
${\lambda=2 l=2 \times 2=4 m}$
${f=\dfrac{v}{\lambda}=7 / 4 H z}$
subject: Physics

question: For stationary wave, $y=10 \sin (300 \pi t) \sin \left(\dfrac{\pi}{8} x\right)$ The distance between consecutive nodes is
explanation:
${k=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda}=\dfrac{\pi}{8}}$
${\Rightarrow \lambda=16 {~m}}$
Distance between consecutive nodes
${=\dfrac{\lambda}{2}=\dfrac{16}{2}=8 m}$
subject: Physics

question: The equation of a stationary wave is ${y=10 \sin \dfrac{\pi x}{4} \cos 20 \pi t}$. The distance between two consecutive nodes (in ${m}$ ) is
explanation:
${k=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda}=\dfrac{\pi}{4} \Rightarrow \lambda=8}$
Now, distance between nodes ${=\lambda / 2=4}$
subject: Physics

question: A string is stretched between two fixed points separated by $75\,cm$. It is observed to have resonant frequencies at $420\,Hz$ and $315\,Hz$. There are no other resonant frequencies between these two. The lowest resonant frequency for this string is
explanation:
${\left(\dfrac{n}{2 l}\right) v=315}$ and ${\dfrac{(n+1) v}{2 l}=420}$
${\Rightarrow n=3}$
Lowest frequency
${f_{0}=\dfrac{v}{2 l}=\dfrac{315}{3}=105 {~Hz}}$
subject: Physics

question:
The equation of a plane progressive wave is
$y=0.09 \sin 8 \pi\left(t-\dfrac{x}{20}\right)$
When it is reflected at rigid supports, its amplitude becomes ${(2 / 3)^{\text {rd }}}$ of its previous value. The equation of the reflected wave is
explanation:
Put ${-x}$ in place of ${x}$ in the given equation, the phase will be differ by ${\pi}$.
Amplitude ${A^{\prime} \dfrac{2}{3} \times 0.09=0.06}$
${y=0.06 \sin 8 \pi\left[t+\dfrac{x}{20}+\pi\right]}$
$y = - 0.06\sin 8\pi \left[ {t + \frac{x}{{20}}} \right]$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Reflection of Waves at Rigid and Free Boundaries

question:
Two waves represented by
${y_{1}=10 \sin (2000 \pi t+2 x)}$ and
${y_{2}=10 \sin \left(2000 \pi t+2 x+\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)}$
are superimposed at any point at a particular instant. The resultant amplitude is
subject: Physics

question: Two waves are represented by the equations ${y_{1}=a \sin (\omega t+k x+0.57) m}$ and ${y_{2}=a \cos (\omega t+k x) m}$ where ${x}$ is in meter and ${t}$ in sec. The phase difference between them is
explanation:
As ${y_{1}=a \sin (\omega t+k x+0.57)}$
and, ${y_{2}=a \cos (\omega t+k x)}$
${\Rightarrow y_{2}=a \sin \left(\omega t+k x+\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)}$
So, ${\Delta \phi=1.57-0.57=1 {rad}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Resultant Amplitude and Intensity in Interference of Waves

question: A source supplies heat to a system at the rate of $1000\,W$. If the system performs work at a rate of $200\,W$. The rate at which internal energy of the system increases is
explanation:
Rate of heat supplied $\frac{{\delta Q}}{{dt}} = 1000\,W$
$\frac{{\delta W}}{{dt}} = 200\,W$
By first law of thermodynamics,
$\delta Q=d U+\delta W$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{d U}{d t}=\dfrac{\delta Q}{d t}-\dfrac{\delta W}{d t}=(1000-200) W$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{dU}}{{dt}} = 800\,W$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Resultant Amplitude and Intensity in Interference of Waves

question:
Assertion: The phenomenon of interference is based on conservation of energy.

Reason: For interference of two waves the amplitude must be nearly equal.
explanation: In interference, energy is transferred between positions of minimum and maximum intensity, emphasizing the conservation of energy principle. So correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Resultant Amplitude and Intensity in Interference of Waves

question:
Assertion: If two waves of same amplitude, produce a resultant wave of same amplitude, then the phase difference between them will be $120^{\circ}$.

Reason: The resultant amplitude of two waves is equal to sum of amplitude of two waves.
explanation:
$\sqrt {A_1^2 + A_2^2 + 2{A_1}{A_2}\cos \phi } $
${A_1} = {A_2} \Rightarrow {A_{{\rm{net }}}} = 2A\cos \frac{\phi }{2}$
$\phi = 120^\circ \Rightarrow {A_{{\rm{net }}}} = 2A\cos 60^\circ = A$
So correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Resultant Amplitude and Intensity in Interference of Waves

question:
Assertion: The phase difference between two medium particles having a path difference $\lambda$ is $2 \pi$.

Reason: The phase difference is directly proportional to path difference of a particle.
explanation: It takes one period $T$ for path difference to be $\lambda$ and phase difference $\Delta \phi$ to be $\lambda$. Hence, So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
The length of a sonometer wire is $90\,cm$ and the stationary wave setup in the wire is represented by an equation
$y=6 \sin \left(\dfrac{\pi x}{30}\right) \cos (250 t)$ where $x, y$ are in $\mathrm{cm}$ and $t$ is in second. The number of loops is
explanation:
Comparing the given equation with standard
$\frac{{2\pi }}{\lambda } = \frac{\pi }{{30}} \Rightarrow \lambda = 60\,cm$
$\frac{\lambda }{2} = 30\,cm$
Since the length of sonometer wire is $90 \mathrm{~cm}$ number of loops $=\dfrac{90}{30}=3$.
subject: Physics

question: The length of the wire shown in Fig. between the pulley and fixed support is $1.5\,m$ and mass is $12.0\,g$ the frequency of vibration with which the wire vibrate in two loops leaving the middle point of the wire between the pulleys at rest is:
explanation:
$f=\dfrac{2 v}{2 \ell}=\dfrac{1}{\ell} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$ where $\mu=M / L$
$ \Rightarrow n = \sqrt {\frac{T}{{ml}}} $
$ = \sqrt {\frac{{189 \times 2}}{{12 \times {{10}^{ - 3}} \times 3}}} $
$ = \sqrt {{{10}^4}} = 100\,Hz$
subject: Physics

question: For a stationary wave, $Y = 10\sin \left( {\frac{{\pi x}}{{15}}} \right)\cos (48\pi t)cm$, the distance between a node and the successive antinode is
explanation:
On comparing the given equation with a standard stationary wave equation, we get
$k = \frac{\pi }{{15}} = \frac{{2\pi }}{\lambda } \Rightarrow \lambda = 30\,cm$
Distance between node and antinode is,
$\frac{\lambda }{4} = \frac{{30}}{4} = 7.5\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: A wave travelling along uniform string represented by $Y=A \sin (\omega t-k x)$ is superimposed on another wave travelling along the same string represented by $Y=A \sin (\omega t+k x)$. The resultant is
explanation:
According to principle of superposition, the resultant wave is
${Y_{res}} = A\sin (\omega t - kx) + A\sin (\omega t + kx)$
$ \Rightarrow {Y_{res}} = 2A\sin \omega t\cos kx$
It is a standing wave equation.
For nodes, amplitude${Y_{res}} = 0$
If $\cos \,kx = 0$ then $y = 0$
$ \Rightarrow kx = \left( {n - \frac{1}{2}} \right)\pi ;$
So, $n = 1,2,3, \ldots \ldots $
since, $k=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda} ;$ we get
$x = \left( {n - \frac{1}{2}} \right)\frac{\lambda }{2}$
where $x = \left( {n - \frac{1}{2}} \right)\frac{\lambda }{2}n = 1,2,3, \ldots \ldots .$

options.text: a standing wave having nodes at $x=\left(n-\dfrac{1}{2}\right) \dfrac{\lambda}{2}$, where $n=1,2,3, \ldots \ldots \ldots$.
a standing wave having nodes at $x=\dfrac{n \lambda}{2}$, where $n=0,1,2, \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots$.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Velocity of particles while crossing mean position (in stationary waves) is maximum at anti-nodes and zero at nodes.

Reason: Amplitude of vibration at anti - nodes is maximum and at nodes, the amplitude is zero and all particles between two successive nodes cross the mean position together.
explanation:
The velocity of particles in stationary waves changes as they cross the mean position.
Specifically, at the nodes (points where the displacement is always zero), the velocity of the particles is zero. At the anti-nodes (points where the displacement is maximum), the velocity of the particles is at a maximum. This is a characteristic behavior of particles in stationary waves.
Reason correctly states that the amplitude of vibration at anti-nodes is maximum, and at nodes, the amplitude is zero. It also correctly mentions that all particles between two successive nodes cross the mean position together. However, while it provides a description of these characteristics, it doesn't directly explain why the velocity varies as it does.
Option (2) is the correct choice.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question: A wave pulse in a thin string is incident on a thick string as shown in the figure. If joint is at $P$, then the form of reflected and transmitted pulses is
explanation: The reflected and transmitted pulses are as shown in Fig. (2) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956996/preptracker/questions/q_41117_explanation_1764956996546.png
subject: Physics

question: Two generators \(S_{1}\) and \(S_{2}\) produce water wave of equal frequency. A point \[P\] is located such that \(\left(S_{1} P-S_{2} P\right)\) is equal to half a wavelength. When operated alone, \(S_{1}\) produces an oscillation of amplitude \(2 a\) at \[P\] while \(S_{2}\) produces an oscillation of amplitude \(a\). If the generators are operated in phase, which graph correctly shows the resultant oscillation at \[P\] ?
explanation:
The path difference $\Delta x=\dfrac{\lambda}{2}$
$\Delta \phi=k \Delta x=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \dfrac{\lambda}{2}=\pi$
Destructive interference will occur at point $P$.
${A_{{\text{resultant }}}} = \sqrt {{{(2a)}^2} + {a^2} - 4{a^2}} = a.$
It will remain constant with time.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Resultant Amplitude and Intensity in Interference of Waves

question:
The equation of two waves are given by:
${y_1} = 5\sin 2\pi (x - vt)cm$
${y_2} = 3\sin 2\pi (x - vt + 1.5)cm$
These waves are simultaneuously passing through a string. The amplitude of the resulting wave is
explanation:
${A_1} = 5\,cm,{A_2} = 3\,cm$
The two waves differ in their phases by $\Delta \phi=2 \pi(1.5)=3 \pi$ radians
Required resulting 'amplitude' is:
$A_{\text {net }}=\sqrt{A_{1}^{2}+A_{2}^{2}+2 A_{1} A_{2} \cos (\Delta \phi)}$
$=\sqrt{5^{2}+3^{2}+2 \times 5 \times 3 \cos 3 \pi}$
$ = \sqrt {25 + 9 - 1} = \sqrt {33} \approx 5.8\,cm$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Resultant Amplitude and Intensity in Interference of Waves

question: A standing wave is formed by the superposition of two waves travelling in opposite directions. The transverse displacement is given by $y(x, t)=0.5 \sin \left(\dfrac{5 \pi}{4} x\right) \cos (200 \pi t)$ What is the speed of the travelling wave moving in the positive $x$ direction ? $x$and $t$ are in meter and second, respectively.)
subject: Physics

question: Two wires $W_{1}$ and $W_{2}$ have the same radius $r$ and respective densities $\rho_{1}$ and $\rho_{2}$ such that $\rho_{2}=4 \rho_{1}$. They are joined together at the point $O$, as shown in the figure. The combination is used as a sonometer wire and kept under tension $T$. The point $O$ is midway between the bridges. When a stationary waves is set up in the composite wire, the joint is formed to be a node. The ratio of the number of antinodes formed in $W_{1}$ to $W_{2}$ is :
explanation:
The frequencies in both wires must be same
${\text{ }}T{\text{, }}r{\text{ and }}l{\text{ are same }}{f_1} = {f_2}$]
Frequency of vibration
$f=\dfrac{n}{2 l} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\pi r^{2} \rho}}$
As $T,r$, and $l$ are same for both the wires
$\begin{aligned}& f_{1}=f_{2} \\& \dfrac{n_{1}}{\sqrt{\rho_{1}}}=\dfrac{n_{2}}{\sqrt{\rho_{2}}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{n_{1}}{n_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_{1}}{\rho_{2}}}=\dfrac{1}{2}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: The total length of a sonometer wire between fixed ends is $110\,cm$. Two bridges are placed to divide the length of wire in ratio $6: 3: 2$. The tension in the wire is $400\,N$ and the mass per unit length is $0.01\,kg/m$. What is the minimum common frequency with which three parts can vibrate?
explanation:
$l_{1}: l_{2}: l_{3}=6: 3: 2$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {l_1} = \frac{6}{{11}} \times 110\,cm = 60\,cm,{l_2} = 30\,cm,{l_3} = 20\,cm$
$v = \sqrt {\frac{T}{\mu }} = \sqrt {\frac{{400}}{{0.01}}} \,m/s = 200\,m/s$
Now common ratio of the three lengths $l_{1}, l_{2}$ and $l_{3}$ is 10 .
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \frac{\lambda }{2} = 10\,cm\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \Rightarrow \lambda = 20\,cm$
$\therefore {f_{{\text{min}}}} = \frac{v}{\lambda } = \frac{{200}}{{20 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}}Hz = 1000\,Hz$
subject: Physics

question: A sonometer wire of length $114\,cm$ fixed at both the ends. Where should the two bridges be placed so as to divide the wire into three segments whose fundamental frequencies are in the ratio $1: 3: 4$ ?
explanation:
Total length of the wire, $L = 114\,cm$
$f_{1}: f_{2}: f_{3}=1: 3: 4$
Let $L_{1}, L_{2}$ and $L_{3}$ be the lengths of the three parts
${\text{ As }}\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} n\alpha \frac{1}{L}$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {L_1}:{L_2}:{L_3} = \frac{1}{1}:\frac{1}{3}:\frac{1}{4} = 12:4:3$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {L_1} = \left( {\frac{{12}}{{12 + 4 + 3}} \times 114} \right) = 72\,cm$
${L_2} = \left( {\frac{4}{{19}} \times 114} \right) = 24\,cm$
${L_3} = \left( {\frac{3}{{19}} \times 114} \right) = 18\,cm$
Hence the bridges should be placed at $72\,cm$ and $72 + 24 = 96\,cm$ from one end.
subject: Physics

question: Two wave pulses travel to the right toward a rigid boundary as shown. After reflection of the triangular pulse, the pulses will interfere. Which one of the following pictures best represents the superposition of the pulses when the centers of the individual pulses coincide?
explanation:
When the triangular phase gets reflected, it suffers a phase change of $180^{\circ}$. Interfering pulses are shown below.

explanation:

question: The equation of standing wave is \(y=a \cos k x \sin \omega t\). The \(y-x\) graph for the wave at \(t=\dfrac{T}{4}\) is
explanation:
$y=a \cos k x \sin \omega t$
At $\quad t=\dfrac{T}{4} \quad y=a \cos k x \sin \left(\dfrac{2 \pi}{T} \times \dfrac{T}{4}\right)$
$y=a \cos k x$
so the correct graph is (4)
subject: Physics

question: A string of mass $m$ is fixed at both ends. The Fundamental mode of string is exicted and it has an angular frequency $\omega$ and the maximum displacement amplitude A.then $K.E$ for one periodic time is
explanation:
Let $y=A \sin k x \sin \omega t$ represents the standing wave fixed at both ends. The velocity of a particle is $v_{p}=\dfrac{\partial y}{\partial t}$
$v_{p}=A \omega \sin k x \cos \omega t$
The small $K.E$ of the element is
$\begin{aligned}& d k=\dfrac{1}{2} d m v_{p}^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} \mu d x v_{p}^{2} \\& d k=\dfrac{1}{2} \mu A^{2} \omega^{2} \sin ^{2} k x \cos ^{2} \omega t d x \\& k=\dfrac{1}{2} \mu A^{2} \omega^{2} \cos ^{2} \omega t \int_{0}^{L} \sin ^{2} k x d x \\& k=\dfrac{1}{4} \mu A^{2} \omega^{2} \cos ^{2} \omega t \int_{0}^{L}(1-\cos 2 k x) d x \\& k=\dfrac{1}{4} \mu A^{2} \omega^{2} \cos ^{2} \omega t\left[L-\dfrac{1}{2 k}[\sin 2 k x]_{0}^{L}\right]\end{aligned}$
given that the string is in Fundamental mode
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{\lambda}{2}=L \Rightarrow \lambda=2 L \Rightarrow k=\dfrac{2 \pi}{2 L} \\& k=\dfrac{1}{4} \mu L A^{2} \omega^{2} \cos ^{2} \omega t \\& =\dfrac{1}{4} m A^{2} \omega^{2} \cos ^{2} \omega t \quad(\mu L=m) \\& < k>=\dfrac{1}{4} m A^{2} \omega^{2} < \cos ^{2} \omega t>=\dfrac{1}{8} m A^{2} \omega^{2}\end{aligned}$
(for one time period)
$\left[ < \sin ^{2}(\omega t)>= < \cos ^{2}(\omega t)>=\dfrac{1}{2}\right]$
subject: Physics

question: The ends of a stretched wire of length $L$ are fixed at $x = 0$ and $x = L$. In one experiment, the displacement of the wire is $y_{1}=A \sin \left(\dfrac{\pi x}{L}\right) \sin$ $(\omega t)$ and energy is $E_{1}$ and in another experiment its displacement is ${y_2} = A\sin \left( {\frac{{2\pi x}}{L}} \right)\sin (2\omega t)$ and energy is $E_{2}$. Then
explanation:
Consider a standing wave
$y = A\sin kx\sin \omega t$
Consider a small element of mass $dm$ in the string. The kinetic energy of the
Element is $d k=\dfrac{1}{2} d m v_{p}^{2}$
${v_p} = \frac{{\partial y}}{{\partial t}} = A\omega \sin kx\cos \omega t,\,\,\,dm = \mu dx$
$dk = \frac{1}{2}(\mu dx){A^2}{\omega ^2}{\sin ^2}kx{\cos ^2}\omega t$
$(\mu = {\text{ linear mass density }})$
$dk = \frac{1}{2}\mu {A^2}{\omega ^2}{\sin ^2}(kx)dx\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} ({\text{ At }}t = 0)$
$k = \frac{1}{2}\mu {A^2}{\omega ^2}\int_0^L {{{\sin }^2}} kxdx$
$ = \frac{1}{4}\mu {A^2}{\omega ^2}\left[ {L - \frac{1}{{2k}}\sin 2kL} \right]{\text{ }}$
$T.E{\text{ }} = K \cdot E + P \cdot E$
$ = \frac{1}{2}\mu {A^2}{\omega ^2}\left[ {L - \frac{1}{{2k}}\sin 2kL} \right](P.E = K.E)$
For the first wave
${E_1} = \frac{1}{2}\mu {A^2}{\omega ^2}L\,\,\,\,\left( {k = \frac{\pi }{L}} \right)$
${E_2} = \frac{1}{2}\mu {A^2}{(2\omega )^2}L\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( {k = \frac{{2\pi }}{L}} \right)$
$\frac{{{E_1}}}{{{E_2}}} = \frac{1}{4} \Rightarrow {E_2} = 4{E_1}$
subject: Physics

question: two waves are propogating to the point p along a straight line produces by two sources $A$ and $B$ of sample harmonic and of equal frequency. The amplitude of every wave at $P$ is '$a$' and phase of $A$ is ahead $\frac{\pi }{3}$ than that of $B$ and the distance $AP$ is greater than $BP$ by $50\,cm$. Then the resultant amplitude at the point $P$ will be, If the wavelength is 1 meter
explanation:
Path difference $(\Delta x) = 50\,cm = \frac{1}{2}\,m$
$\therefore$ Phase difference
$\Delta \phi=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \times \Delta x \Rightarrow \phi=\dfrac{2 \pi}{1} \times \dfrac{1}{2}=\pi$
Total phase difference $=\pi-\dfrac{\pi}{3}=\dfrac{2 \pi}{3}$
$\Rightarrow A=\sqrt{a^{2}+a^{2}+2 a^{2} \cos (2 \pi / 3)}=a$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Resultant Amplitude and Intensity in Interference of Waves

question: What could be a correct expression for the speed of ocean waves in terms of its wavelength $\lambda$, the depth $h$ of the ocean, the density $\rho$ of sea water, and the acceleration of free fall $g$ ?
explanation: The dimension of option (1) $\sqrt{g \lambda}$ matches the dimension of speed.
subject: Physics

question: A surface of area $S$ is placed perpendicular to the direction of travel of a plane wave. The energy per unit time intercepted by the surface is $E$ when the amplitude of the wave is $A$. The area of the surface is reduced to $1/2\,S$ and the amplitude of the wave is increased to $2\,A$. What is the energy per unit time intercepted by this smaller surface?
explanation:
Energy $=$ Intensity $\times$ Area
$\therefore E = I \times S,\,\,{\text{Also}},{\text{ }}I\alpha {A^2},{\text{ }}S$ is the area
$\therefore E \alpha A^{2} S \quad \therefore \quad \dfrac{E^{\prime}}{E}=\dfrac{(2 A)^{2}(S / 2)}{A^{2} \cdot S}=2$
$E^{\prime}=2 E$
subject: Physics

question: The fundamental frequency of a sonometer wire of length $l$ is $f_{0}$. A bridge is now introduced at a distance of $\Delta l$ from the center of the wire $(\Delta l <  < l)$. The number of beats heard if both sides of the string are vibrating in fundamental modes are
explanation: $\begin{aligned}& f_{0}=\dfrac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}, f_{1}=\dfrac{1}{2\left(\dfrac{l}{2}-\Delta l\right)} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}=\dfrac{f_{0} l}{\left(\dfrac{l}{2}-\Delta l\right)} \\& \therefore \quad f_{1}=\dfrac{2 f_{0}}{\left(1-\dfrac{2 \Delta l}{l}\right)}=2 f_{0}\left(1+\dfrac{2 \cdot \Delta l}{l}\right) \\& \text { Again, } f_{2}=\dfrac{1}{2\left(\dfrac{l}{2}+\Delta l\right)} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}=2 f_{0}\left(1-\dfrac{2 \cdot \Delta l}{l}\right) \\& \therefore \text { No. of beats }=f_{1}-f_{2}=\dfrac{8 f_{0} \cdot \Delta l}{l}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: In a standing wave experiment, a $1.2\,kg$ horizontal rope is fixed in place at its two ends( $x$ $=0$ and $x = 2.0\,m$) and made to oscillate up and down in the fundamental mode, at a frequency of $5.0\,Hz$. At $t = 0$, the point at $x = 1.0\,m$ has zero displacement and is moving upward in the positive direction of $y$ - axis with a transverse velocity $3.14\,m/s$. The standing wave equation is
explanation:
The wave is in fundamental mode of vibration as shown in the figure
The angular frequency
$\omega = 2\pi v = 10\pi $
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956151/preptracker/questions/q_40722_explanation_1764956150664.png
$\frac{\lambda }{2} = l \Rightarrow k = \frac{{2\pi }}{\lambda } = \frac{\pi }{l}$
The velocity of the particle at the mid point of the string is ${v_p} = A\omega = 3.14\,m/s \Rightarrow A = 0.1\,m$
From the above information
$y=0.1 \sin \left(\dfrac{\pi}{2} x\right) \sin (10 \pi t)$
subject: Physics

question:
A wave equation is represented as
$r=A \sin \left[\alpha\left(\dfrac{x-y}{2}\right)\right] \cos \left[\omega t-\alpha\left(\dfrac{x+y}{2}\right)\right]$
where $x$ and $y$ are in meters and $t$ is in seconds. Then
explanation:
The given equation represents the superposition of two progressive waves travelling along $x$ and $y$ axes given by
${r_1} = - \frac{A}{2}\sin (\omega t - \alpha x)\,\& $
${r_2} = \frac{A}{2}\sin (\omega t - \alpha y)$
$r = {r_1} + {r_2}$
$=A \sin \left[\alpha \dfrac{(x-y)}{2}\right] \cos \left[\omega t-\alpha \dfrac{(x+y)}{2}\right]$
from the given equation the amplitude of the wave is $A_{R}=A \sin \left[\alpha\left(\dfrac{x-y}{2}\right)\right]$
The amplitude becomes zero
${A_R} = A\sin \left[ {\alpha \left( {\frac{{x - y}}{2}} \right)} \right] = 0$
$\alpha \left( {\frac{{x - y}}{2}} \right) = n\pi \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} (n = 0,1,2, \ldots .)$
$\alpha \left( {\frac{{x - y}}{2}} \right) = \pi \Rightarrow x - y = \frac{{2\pi }}{\alpha }$
$y = x - \frac{{2\pi }}{\alpha }$
All the points that lie on the line $y=x-\dfrac{2 \pi}{\alpha}$ are always at rest.

options.text: The wave is a stationary wave
The wave is a progressive wave propagating along $+x-a$ axis
The wave is a progressive wave propagating at right angle to the $+x$-axis
All points lying on line $y=x-\dfrac{2 \pi}{\alpha}$ are always at rest.

subject: Physics

question: The $(x, y)$ coordinates of the corners of a squareplate are $\left( {0,0} \right)$, $\left( {L,0} \right)$, $\left( {L,L} \right)$,  and $\left( {0,L} \right)$. The edges of the plates are clamped and transverse standing waves are set - up in it. If $u\left( {x,y} \right)$ denotes the displacement of the plate at the point $\left( {x,y} \right)$ at some instant of time, the possible expression for $u$ is $\left( {u\,{\text{ = }}\,{\text{positive}}\,{\text{constant}}} \right)$
explanation:
The clamped square plate is shown in the figure
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956149/preptracker/questions/q_40714_explanation_1764956149108.png
The displacement of the plate must be zero at all the edges of the plate that is
$x = 0$ (on $y - $ axis)
$x = L$ (parallel to $y - $ axis and present on the edge)
$y = L$ (parallel to $x$ - axis and present on the edge)
$y = 0$ (on $x$ axis)
For all the above four conditions $u = 0$
The equation that satisfies is
$u=a \sin \left(\dfrac{\pi x}{L}\right) \sin \left(\dfrac{\pi y}{L}\right)$
subject: Physics

question: Two waves of nearly same amplitude, same frequency travelling with same velocity are superimposing to give phenomenon of interference. If $a_{1}$ and $a_{2}$ be their amplitudes, $\omega$ be the frequency for both, $v$ be the velocity for both and $\Delta \phi$ is the phase difference between the two waves then, incorrect statement among the following
explanation:
In this case of sustained interference in which position of maxima and minima remains fixed all over the screen
$\dfrac{I_{\min }}{I_{\max }}=\left(\dfrac{a_{1}-a_{2}}{a_{1}+a_{2}}\right)^{2}$
And both waves must be travelling in the same direction with a constant phase difference (condition for coherence)

options.text: The resultant intensity varies periodically with distance
Both the waves must have been travelling in the same direction and must be coherent

$I_{R}=I_{1}+I_{2}+2 \sqrt{I_{1} I_{2}} \cos (\Delta \phi)$, where constructive interference is obtained for path difference that are odd multiple of $\dfrac{\lambda}{2}$ and destructive interference is obtained for path difference that are even multiple of $\dfrac{\lambda}{2}$

subject: Physics
subtopic: Resultant Amplitude and Intensity in Interference of Waves

question: A sonometer wire under suitable tension having specific gravity $\rho$, vibrates with frequency $n$ in air. If the load is completely immersed in water the frequency of vibration of wire will become
explanation:
The fundamental frequency of sonometer wire,
$n=\dfrac{1}{2 L} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$
As $L$ and $\mu$ are constant, so $n$ depends on $T$
$\begin{aligned}& n \propto \sqrt{T} \\& \therefore \dfrac{n_{2}}{n_{1}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{(\text { weight })_{s}-(\text { weight })_{w}}{(\text { weight })_{s}}} \\& =\sqrt{\dfrac{V \rho_{s} g-V \rho_{w} g}{V \rho_{s} g}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\dfrac{\rho_{s}}{\rho_{w}}-1}{\dfrac{\rho_{s}}{\rho_{w}}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho-1}{\rho}}\end{aligned}$
$\because \left( {{\text{specific}}\,{\text{gravity}}\,{\text{of}}\,{\text{sonometer}}{\mkern 1mu} \,{\text{wire}},{\text{ }}\rho {\text{ = }}\frac{{{\rho _s}}}{{{\rho _w}}}} \right)$
$n_{2}=n \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho-1}{\rho}}$
subject: Physics

question: A sonometer wire is unison with a tuning fork, when it is stretched by weight $w$ and the corresponding resonating length is $L_{1}$. If the weight is reduced to $\left(\dfrac{W}{4}\right)$, the corresponding resonating length becomes $L_{2}$. The ratio $\left(\dfrac{L_{1}}{L_{2}}\right)$ is
explanation:
When sonometer is strecthed by weight $w$ (tension), then frequency of vibration in the string
$v = \frac{1}{{2l}}\sqrt {\frac{T}{m}} $
$v = \frac{1}{{2{L_1}}}\sqrt {\frac{T}{m}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Where, $L_{1}$ is the resonating length and $m$ is mass per unit length of the string
Similarly, when weight reduces to $\dfrac{w}{4}$ and resonating length is $L_{2}$, then
$v = \frac{1}{{2{L_2}}}\sqrt {\frac{{\frac{w}{4}}}{m}} \Rightarrow v = \frac{1}{{4{L_2}}}\sqrt {\frac{w}{m}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From eqs.(1) and (2), we have
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{1}{2 L_{1}} \sqrt{\dfrac{w}{m}}=\dfrac{1}{4 L_{2}} \sqrt{\dfrac{w}{m}} \Rightarrow L_{1}=2 L_{2} \\& \therefore \dfrac{L_{1}}{L_{2}}=2 \\& \therefore L_{1}: L_{2}=2: 1\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: When the length of the vibrating segment of a sonometer wire is increased by $1 \%$, the percentage change in its frequency is
explanation:
The fundamental frequency of a sonometer wire is
$n=\dfrac{1}{2 L} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$
$\therefore \quad n=\dfrac{C}{L}$ (where $C$ is a constant)
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\Delta n}{n}=\left|\dfrac{\Delta L}{L}\right|$
$\therefore \%$ change in frequency for $1 \%$ increase in length $=1 \%$
subject: Physics

question: The fundamental frequency of sonometer wire increases by $9Hz$, if its tension is increased by $69 \%$ keeping the length constant. The frequency of the wire is
explanation:
we know that, frequency of vibration of a stretched string is given by $v=\dfrac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{m}}$
where, $T = $ tension in string
$m = $ mass / length
and $1=$ length of string
New frequency,
${v^\prime } = v + 9 = \frac{1}{{2l}}\sqrt {\frac{{T + (69/100)T}}{m}} $
$ \Rightarrow \frac{v}{{{v^\prime }}} = \sqrt {\frac{T}{{\frac{{169}}{{100}}T}}} \Rightarrow \frac{v}{{v + 9}} = \sqrt {\frac{{100}}{{169}}} = \frac{{10}}{{13}}$
$ \Rightarrow 13v = 10v + 90$
$v = 30\,Hz$
subject: Physics

question: In a sonometer experiment, the bridges are separated by a fixed distance. The wire which is slightly elastic, emits a tone of frequency $n$ when held by tension $T$. If the tension is inceased to 4 $T$ the tone emitted by the wire will be of frequency
explanation:
Frequency of wave produced in the wire
$n = \frac{1}{{2l}} \times \sqrt {\frac{T}{\mu }} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
where $l=$ length of the wire
$T = $ tension in the wire
$\mu=$ mass per unit length
Frequency produced when tension changes to $4T$ is
${n^\prime } = \frac{1}{{2l}} \times \sqrt {\frac{{4T}}{\mu }} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
On dividing eq.(1) by eq.(2), we get
$\dfrac{n}{n^{\prime}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{4}}=\dfrac{1}{2} \Rightarrow n^{\prime}=2 n$
subject: Physics

question: A stretched wire of length $260\,cm$ is set into vibrations. It is divided into three segments whose frequencies are in the ratio $2: 3: 4$. Their lengths must be
explanation:
The frequency produced by a stretched wire is given by
$f = \frac{p}{{2l}}\sqrt {\frac{T}{m}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
where $p = $ number of loops formed in vibration,
$l = $ length of wire
$T = $ tension of wire
and $m = $ mass per unit length of wire
So, from eq.(1) we get
$f \propto \dfrac{1}{t}$
Here, the ratio of frequencies of three segment is
$\begin{aligned}& f_{1}: f_{2}: f_{3}=2: 3: 4 \\& \Rightarrow l_{1}: l_{2}: l_{3}=\dfrac{1}{2}: \dfrac{1}{3}: \dfrac{1}{4} \\& \quad=6: 4: 3\end{aligned}$
The length of wire, $L = 260\,cm$, so
${l_1} = \frac{6}{{13}} \times 260 = 120\,cm$
${l_2} = \frac{4}{{13}} \times 260 = 80\,cm$
and ${l_3} = \frac{3}{{13}} \times 260 = 60\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: Two uniform wires of the same material are vibrating under the same tension. If the first overtone of the first wire is equal to the second overtone of the second wire and radius of the first wire is twice the radius of the second wire, then the ratio of the lengths of the first wire to second wire is
explanation:
Fundamental frequency of the first wire is given as,
$f=\dfrac{1}{2 L_{1}} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{m}}=\dfrac{1}{2 L_{1}} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\pi r_{1}^{2} \rho}}=\dfrac{1}{2 L_{1} r_{1}} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\rho \pi}}$
The first overtone,
${f_1} = 2f = \frac{2}{{2{L_1}{r_1}}}\sqrt {\frac{T}{{\rho \pi }}} = \frac{1}{{{L_1}{r_1}}}\sqrt {\frac{T}{{\pi \rho }}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
The second overtone of the second wire is given as,
${f_2} = \frac{3}{{2{L_2}{r_2}}}\sqrt {\frac{T}{{\pi \rho }}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Given, $f_{1}=f_{2}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{{L_1}{r_1}}}\sqrt {\frac{T}{{\pi \rho }}} = \frac{3}{{2{L_2}{r_2}}}\sqrt {\frac{T}{{\pi \rho }}} $
$\therefore 3{L_1}{r_1} = 2{L_2}{r_2}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{L_1}}}{{{L_2}}} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{{{r_2}}}{{{r_1}}} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{{{r_2}}}{{2{r_2}}}\,\,\,\,\left[ {{\text{given}},\,{r_1} = 2{r_2}} \right]$
$ = \frac{1}{3}$
subject: Physics

question: A string is vibrating in its fifth overtone between two rigid supports $2.4\,m$ part. The distance between successive node and antinode is
explanation:
Here, fifth overtone ${6^{th}}$ harmonic
Therefore, string will have 6 loops
If distance between a mode and an antinode is 1 , then $6 \times 2 l=2.4$ or $l=\dfrac{2.4}{12}=0.2 m$
subject: Physics

question: A wire under tension vibrates with a fundamental frequency of $600\,Hz$. If the length of the wire is doubled, the radius is halved and the wire is made to vibrate under one-ninth the tension. Then the fundamental frequency will become
explanation:
The fundamental frequency of vibration is
$f=\dfrac{1}{2 L r} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\pi \rho}}$
Where $T = $ Tension in the wire
$L= $ Length of the wire
$r=$ Radius of the wire
$\rho=$ Density of material of the wire
$\begin{aligned}& \therefore \dfrac{f_{1}}{f_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{L_{2}}{L_{1}}\right)\left(\dfrac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right) \sqrt{\dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}} \\& \therefore \dfrac{f_{1}}{f_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{2 L_{1}}{L_{1}}\right)\left(\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{2} r_{1}}{r_{1}}\right) \sqrt{\dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{1} / 9}}=2 \times \dfrac{1}{2} \times 3=3\end{aligned}$
$ \Rightarrow {f_2} = \frac{{{f_1}}}{3} = \frac{{600\,Hz}}{3} = 200\,Hz$
subject: Physics

question: A string vibrates with a frequency of $200\,Hz$. When its length is doubled and tension is altered, it begins to vibrate with a frequency of $300\,Hz.$ The ratio of the new tension to the original tension is
explanation:
Initial frequency
$f_{1}=\dfrac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\dfrac{T_{1}}{\mu}} \Rightarrow T_{1}=f_{1}^{2} \cdot 4 l^{2} \cdot \mu$
When length of string is doubled,
$T_{2}=f_{2}^{2} \cdot 4 \times 4 l^{2} \cdot \mu$
Given ${f_1} = 200\,Hz$ and ${f_2} = 300\,Hz$,
$\therefore \quad \dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}=\dfrac{(300)^{2}}{(200)^{2}} \times 4=\dfrac{9}{1}$
subject: Physics

question: A stretched string fixed at both ends has ' $m$ ' nodes then the length of the string will be
explanation:
For $p$ number of loops in a stretched string, the length is given by
$l = \frac{{p\lambda }}{2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
As, number of harmonics $=$ number of loops $=$ number of anti nodes $ = p\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Also, number of nodes $=$ number of anti nodes $+1$
Here, number of nodes $ = m$
Number of anti nodes $ = m - 1$
from eq.(2)
$p = m - 1$
Putting this value of $p$ in eq .(1), we get
$l=\dfrac{(m-1) \lambda}{2}$
subject: Physics

question: The equation of a stationary wave is $y=2 \sin \left(\dfrac{\pi x}{15}\right) \cos (48 \pi t)$. The distance between a node and its next antinode is
explanation:
Comparing the given equation with standard equation of stationary wave
$y=A \sin (k x) \cos (\omega t)$,
$\mathrm{k}=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda}=\dfrac{\pi}{15} \Rightarrow \lambda=30$
Distance between a node and next antinode $=\dfrac{\lambda}{4}=\dfrac{30}{4}=7.5$ units
subject: Physics

question: When tension $T$ is applied to sonometer wire of length $l$, it vibrates with the fundamental frequency $n$. Keeping the experimental setup same, when the tension is increased by $8\,N$ the fundamental frequency becomes three times the earlier fundamental frequency $n$. The initial tension applied to the wire (in newton) was
explanation:
The initial fundamental frequency of wire,
$n = \frac{1}{{2L}}\sqrt {\frac{T}{\mu }} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
The final fundamental frequency of wire,
$3n = \frac{1}{{2L}}\sqrt {\frac{{T + 8}}{\mu }} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
On dividing eq .(2) by eq.(1), we get
$\dfrac{3 n}{n}=\dfrac{\dfrac{1}{2 L} \sqrt{\dfrac{T+8}{\mu}}}{\dfrac{1}{2 L} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}}$
$3 = \sqrt {\frac{{T + 8}}{T}} $
$ \Rightarrow 9T = T + 8$
$T = 1\,N$
subject: Physics

question: In sonometer experiment, the string of length ' $L$ ' under tension vibrates in second overtone between two bridges. The amplitude of vibration is maximum at
explanation:
String vibrating in second overtone between two bridges can be represented as
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956140/preptracker/questions/q_40430_explanation_1764956140503.png
Therefore, the amplitude of vibration is maximum at $\dfrac{L}{6}, \dfrac{L}{2}, \dfrac{5L}{6}$
subject: Physics

question: If $n_{1}, n_{2}$ and $n_{3}$ are the fundamental frequencies of three segments into which a string is divided, then the original fundamental frequency $n$ of the string is given by
explanation:
Total length of string $l=l_{1}+l_{2}+l_{3}$
(As string is divided into three segments)
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956138/preptracker/questions/q_40422_explanation_1764956137963.png
$n_{1}=\dfrac{1}{2 l_{1}} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}} ; n_{2}=\dfrac{1}{2 l_{2}} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}} ; n_{3}=\dfrac{1}{2 l_{3}} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$
Hence frequency $\propto \dfrac{1}{\text { length }}\left(\because n=\dfrac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{m}}\right)$
$l=l_{1}+l_{2}+l_{3}$
$\dfrac{1}{2 n} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}=\dfrac{1}{2 n_{1}} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}+\dfrac{1}{2 n_{2}} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}+\dfrac{1}{2 n_{3}} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$
$\dfrac{1}{n}=\dfrac{1}{n_{1}}+\dfrac{1}{n_{2}}+\dfrac{1}{n_{3}}$
subject: Physics

question: A stretched string is vibrating in the second overtone, then the number of nodes and antinodes between the ends of the string are respectively
explanation:
The vibrations of second overtone (or third harmonic) of a stretched string is shown in the figure.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951069/preptracker/questions/q_40418_explanation_1764951068747.png
From figure, number of nodes and antinodes will be 4 and 3 (when the two ends are also included).
subject: Physics

question: A uniform wire of length $L$, diameter $D$ and density $\rho$ is stretched under a tension $T$. The correct relation between its fundamental frequency $f$, the length $L$ and the diameter $D$ is
explanation:
The fundamental frequency is
$f=\dfrac{1}{2 L} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$
where $\mu$ is the mass per unit length of the wire
$\begin{aligned}& f=\dfrac{1}{2 L} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\rho \dfrac{D^{2}}{4}}}=\dfrac{1}{2 L} \sqrt{\dfrac{4 T}{\rho D^{2}}}=\dfrac{1}{L D} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\pi \rho}} \\& \Rightarrow f \propto \dfrac{1}{L D}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: Find the wrong statement from the following about the equation of stationary wave given by $Y=0.04 \cos (\pi x) \sin (50 \pi) m$ where $t$ is in second. Then for the stationary wave
explanation:
The displacement of a wave in term of time period is given by
$y=A \sin \left[2 \pi\left(\dfrac{t}{T}-\dfrac{x}{\lambda}\right)\right]$
This equation in terms of speed of wave (v),
becomes $=y=A \sin \left[\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda}(v t-x)\right]$
The given equation of wave is
$\begin{aligned}& y=0.04 \cos (\pi x) \sin (50 \pi t) \\& y=0.02 \sin (50 \pi t+\pi x)+0.02 \sin (50 \pi t-\pi x)\end{aligned}$
Thus, the given wave is the combination of two waves,
$y_{1}=0.02 \sin (50 \pi t+\pi x)($ in - ve direction $)$
and $y_{2}=0.02 \sin (50 \pi t-\pi x)($ in + ve direction $)$ Comparing them with the general equation of wave $a \sin (\omega t+k x)$, we get
Amplitude, $a = 0.02\,m$
time period $T = \frac{{2\pi }}{{50\pi }} = \frac{1}{{25}} = 0.04\,s$
wave length $\lambda = \frac{{2\pi }}{\pi } = 2\,m$
velocity, $v = \frac{{50\pi \times \lambda }}{{2\pi }} = \frac{{100}}{2} = 50\,m{s^{ - 1}}$
So, option (1) is wrong
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: Velocity of particles, while crossing mean position (in stationary waves) varies from maximum at antinodes to zero at nodes.


Reason: Amplitude of vibration at antinodes is maximum and at nodes, the amplitude is zero, and all particles between two successive nodes cross the mean position together.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Resultant Amplitude and Intensity in Interference of Waves

question:
Assertion: A standing wave pattern is formed in a string. The power transfer through a point (other than node and antinode) is zero always.


Reason: At antinode displacement is maximum
explanation:
At node $v=0$, at antinode Tension $\perp$ to velocity
$\therefore$ at both the points power $=0(P=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{v})$. At other points $P \neq 0$. At antinode displacement is maximum. So option (4) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: In a small segment of string carrying sinusoidal wave, total energy is conserved.


Reason: The stretch in the string is maximum at the mean position
explanation: Every small segment is acted upon by forces from both sides of it hence energy is not conserved, rather it is transmitted by the element.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question:
On reflection from a rigid boundary there takes place a complete reversal of phase.
On reflection from a denser medium, both the particle velocity and wave velocity are reversed in sign.
explanation: Reflection from a rigid boundary is a case of reflection from a denser medium. In that case the particle velocity (pulse gets reversed) and wave velocity (travels in opposite direction) are reversed in sign.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Reflection of Waves at Rigid and Free Boundaries

question:
The interference of two identical waves moving in same direction produces standing waves.
Various particles of standing waves do not remain in constant phase.
explanation: Particle present between two consecutive nodes will be in same phase.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Resultant Amplitude and Intensity in Interference of Waves

question:
Two sinusoidal waves on the same string exhibit intereference.
These waves, add or cancel out according to the principle of superposition.
explanation: The principle of superposition is basic to the phenomenon of interference.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

subject: Physics
subtopic: Resultant Amplitude and Intensity in Interference of Waves

question:
I. In a standing wave, frequency of all the particle will be same.
II. In a standing wave, all the particles cross their mean position together
III. In a standing wave, energy is not transmitted across a node.
IV. All the particles are oscillating with same amplitude.
Which of the given statement(s) is/are correct about the standing wave?
explanation: Statements I, II and III are correct and IV is incorrect. Since amplitude of all particles are different at different position.
subject: Physics

question: Equation of plane progressive wave is given by $y=0.6 \sin 2 \pi\left(t-\dfrac{x}{2}\right)$. On reflection from a denser medium its amplitude becomes $2 / 3$ of the amplitude of the incident wave. The equation of the reflected wave is
explanation:
On reflection from a denser medium, there is a phase reversal of $180^{\circ}$.
New amplitude $=\dfrac{2}{3} \times 0.6=0.4$
$\therefore$ Equation of reflected wave is
$\begin{aligned}& y=0.4 \sin 2 \pi\left[t+\dfrac{x}{2}+180^{\circ}\right] \\& =-0.4 \sin 2 \pi(t+x / 2)\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Reflection of Waves at Rigid and Free Boundaries

question: The displacement-time graphs for two sound waves \[A\] and \[B\] are shown in the figure, then the ratio of their intensities \(I_{A} / I_{B}\) is equal to
explanation:
Intensity of a sound wave is $I=\dfrac{1}{2} \rho \omega^{2} a^{2}$
where $\rho$ is the density of the gas and $a$ is the amplitude of the wave.
Intensity $\propto a^{2} \omega^{2}$
Here
$\dfrac{a_{A}}{a_{B}}=\dfrac{2}{1} \text { and } \dfrac{\omega_{A}}{\omega_{B}}=\dfrac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \dfrac{I_{A}}{I_{B}}=\left(\dfrac{2}{1}\right)^{2} \times\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{1}{1}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Resultant Amplitude and Intensity in Interference of Waves

question: A traingle pulse moves towards right with a speed \(v\). After a time \(t=10 \dfrac{x}{v}\), the shape of the pulse at the non rigid boundary is given as:
explanation:
When a pulse reaches a boundary then its shape can be determined by assuming an identical pulse travelling opposite to it from right to left and its distance is present at $10 x$ as shown in the figure.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956991/preptracker/questions/q_40210_explanation_1764956991504.png
So at the boundary the wave shape is the resultant of left and right (imaginary) pulses. So the correct graph is (1)
subject: Physics
subtopic: Reflection of Waves at Rigid and Free Boundaries

question: A pulse in a rope approaches a solid wall and it gets reflected from it. The wave pulse after reflection is best represented by
explanation: If wave is reflected from fixed end the shape of reflected wave will be reversed.
subject: Physics
subtopic: Reflection of Waves at Rigid and Free Boundaries

question: The frequency of a sonometer wire is $f$, but when the weights producing the tensions are completely immersed in water the frequency becomes $f / 3$, and on immersing the weights in a certain liquid the frequency becomes $f / 4$. The specific gravity of the liquid is:
explanation:
Length of the string and linear mass density are constant. So the frequency
$f \propto \sqrt{T}, f=K \sqrt{T}$
After inserting water
$\frac{f}{3} = K\sqrt {{T_1}} \,$
$T = mg$
${T_1} = T/9$
$mg - V{\rho _w}g = mg/9\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} $
$V.{\rho _w} \cdot g = \frac{{8mg}}{9}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
After inserting in a liquid
$f/4 = K\sqrt {{T_2}} $
${T_2} = T/16$
$mg - V{\rho _l}g = mg/16$
$V{\rho _l}g = \frac{{15mg}}{{16}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From eq's (1) & (2)
$\dfrac{\rho_{l}}{\rho_{w}}=\dfrac{135}{128}$
subject: Physics

question: Two parts of sonometer wirehaving length $1\,m$, divided by a movable kinfe-edge, differ in length by $2\,cm$ and produce 1 beat/s when sounded together. Assume that each part is oscillating in fundamental mode, find fundmental frequency of each part.
subject: Physics

question:
A guitar string of length $L$ is stretched between two fixed points $P$ and $Q$ and made to vibrate transversely as shown in the figure. Two particles $A$ and $B$ on the string are separated by a distance $s$. The maximum kinetic energies of $A$ and $B$ are $K_{A}$ and $K_{B}$ respectively. Which of the following gives the correct phase difference and maximum kinetic energies of the particles?

$\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{\text{phase}}\,{\text{difference}}}&{{\text{Maximum}}\,{\text{kinetic}}\,{\text{energy}}} \end{array}$
explanation:
Phase difference $\Delta \phi=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \Delta x$ is not valid for a standing wave. The phase difference between any two points present in successive loops is $\pi$.
The $K.{E_{{\text{Max }}}}\,{\text{at}}\,B$ is more than that of $A$ since the amplitude of $B$ is more than that of $A$.
subject: Physics

question: A standing wave $y=A \sin \left(\dfrac{20}{3} \pi x\right) \cos$ $(1000 \pi t)$ is maintained in a taut string where $x$ and $y$ are expressed in meters. The distance between the successive points oscillating with the amplitude $A/2$ between two adjacent nodes is
explanation:
From the given equation the amplitude is
${A_R} = A\sin \left( {\frac{{20\pi }}{3}x} \right){A_R} = \frac{A}{2} = A\sin \left( {\frac{{20\pi }}{3}x} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \sin \left( {\frac{{20\pi }}{3}x} \right) = \frac{1}{2}$
$\frac{{20\pi }}{3}{x_1} = \frac{\pi }{6} \Rightarrow {x_1} = \frac{1}{{40}}m = 2.5\,cm$
$\frac{{20\pi }}{3}{x_2} = \frac{\pi }{2} + \frac{\pi }{3} \Rightarrow {x_2} = 12.5\,cm$
at $x = 0$ it is a node
The distance between points having amplitudes $\frac{A}{2}{\text{is }}10\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: The stationary waves set up on a string having the equation : $y = (2\,mm)\sin \left[ {\left( {6.28\,{m^{ - 1}}} \right)x} \right]\cos \omega t$ The stationary wave is created by two identical waves, of amplitude A each, moving in opposite directions along the string. Then the smallest length of the string is
explanation:
From the given wave equation the amplitude
$A = 2\,mm\sin [6.28x]$
$k = 6.28 = \frac{{2\pi }}{\lambda } \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{{2\pi }}{{6.28}} = 1\,m$
For the wave fixed at two ends
$\frac{\lambda }{2} = L \Rightarrow L = 50\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: The vibrations of a string of length $60\,cm$ fixed at both the ends are represented by the equation $y=2 \sin \left(\dfrac{4 \pi x}{15}\right) \cos (96 \pi t)$ where $x$ and $y$ are in $cm$. The maximum number of loops that can be formed in it is
subject: Physics

question: A standing wave having 3 nodes and 2 antinodes is formed between two atoms having a distance $1.21\mathop A\limits^o $ between them. The wavelength of the standing wave is :
explanation:
The given standing wave is shown in the figure.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764956118/preptracker/questions/q_40022_explanation_1764956118291.png
As length of one loop or segment is $\lambda / 2$, so length of 2 segment is
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} 2\frac{\lambda }{2} = 1.21\mathop A\limits^o \Rightarrow \lambda = 1.21\mathop A\limits^o $
subject: Physics

question: Two wires are kept tight between the same pair of supports. The ratio of tension in the two wires are $2: 1$, radii are $3: 1$ and that of densities are $1: 2$. What is the ratio of their fundamental frequencies?
explanation:
Fundamental frequency is
$\begin{aligned}& f=\dfrac{1}{2 \ell} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}=\dfrac{1}{2 \ell} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\rho A}} \\& f=\dfrac{1}{2 \ell} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\rho \pi d^{2}}} \\& \dfrac{f_{1}}{f_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}} \times \dfrac{\rho_{2} \times d_{2}^{2}}{\rho_{1} \times d_{1}^{2}}} \\& \dfrac{f_{1}}{f_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2}{1} \times \dfrac{1}{9} \times \dfrac{2}{1}}=\dfrac{2}{3}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A travelling wave represented by $y=A \sin (\omega t-k x)$ is superimposed on another wave represented by $y=A \sin (\omega t+k x)$. The resultant is
explanation:
$y=A \sin (\omega t-k x)+A \sin (\omega t+k x)$
$y=2 A \sin \omega t \cos k x$
The amplitude is $A_{R}=2 A \cos k x$
For standing wave nodes
$\begin{aligned}& \cos k x=0 \\& \dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda} \cdot x=(2 n+1) \dfrac{\pi}{2} \\& \therefore x=\dfrac{(2 n+1) \lambda}{4}, n=0,1,2,3, \ldots . .\end{aligned}$

options.text: A standing wave having nodes at $x=\dfrac{n \lambda}{2}, n=0,1,2 \ldots$
A standing wave having nodes at $x=\left(n+\dfrac{1}{2}\right) \dfrac{\lambda}{2} ; n=0,1,2 \ldots$

subject: Physics

question: A wave represented by the equation $y=a \cos (k x-\omega t)$ is superposed with another wave to form a stationary wave such that the point $x=0$ is a node. The equation of the other wave is
explanation:
$y=a \cos (k x-\omega t)-a \cos (k x+\omega t)$
$y=2 a \sin k x \sin \omega t$
subject: Physics

question: Two similar wires of frequency $n_{1}$ and $n_{2}$ are joined to make one wire. Its frequency will be:
explanation:
$n=\dfrac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}} \Rightarrow n_{1} l_{1}=n_{2} l_{2}=k$
$l_{1}+l_{2}=l_{\text {total }} \Rightarrow \dfrac{k}{n_{1}}+\dfrac{k}{n_{2}}=\dfrac{k}{n}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{n}=\dfrac{1}{n_{1}}+\dfrac{1}{n_{2}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Reflection of Waves at the Boundary of Two Strings

question: A string in a musical instrument is $50\,cm$ long and its fundamental frequency is $800\,Hz$. If a frequency of $1000\,Hz$ is to be produced, then required length of string is:
explanation:
We know that for frequency of sound in string the formula is, $f=\dfrac{1}{2 L} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}} \Rightarrow f \propto \dfrac{1}{L}$
$\frac{{{f_1}}}{{{f_2}}} = \frac{{{L_2}}}{{{L_1}}} \Rightarrow \frac{{800}}{{1000}} = \frac{{{L_2}}}{{50}}$
${L_2} = 50 \times \frac{{800}}{{1000}} = 40\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: Two stretched strings of same material are vibrating under same tension in fundamental mode. The ratio of their frequencies is $1: 2$ and ratio of the length of the vibrating segments is $1: 4$. Then, the ratio of the radii of the string is:
explanation:
Frequency of streched string
$f=\dfrac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\pi r^{2} d}}$
Given, $\dfrac{f_{1}}{f_{2}}=\dfrac{1}{2}, \dfrac{l_{1}}{l_{2}}=\dfrac{1}{4} \Rightarrow \dfrac{f_{1}}{f_{2}}=\dfrac{l_{2}}{l_{1}} \sqrt{\dfrac{r_{2}^{2}}{r_{1}^{2}}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}=\dfrac{f_{2} l_{2}}{f_{1} l_{1}}=2 \times 4=8: 1$
subject: Physics

question: A stretched string of length $l$, fixed at both ends can sustain stationary waves of wavelength $\lambda$, given by
subject: Physics

question: Three coherent waves of equal frequencies having amplitude $10 \mu \mathrm{m}, 4 \mu \mathrm{m}$ and $7 \mu \mathrm{m}$ respectively, arrive at a given point with succesive phase difference of $\pi / 2$. The amplitude of the resulting wave in $\mu m$ is given by:
explanation:
The Phasor diagram of the three waves is

question:
Equation of motion in the same direction is given by $y_{1}=A \sin (\omega t-k x), \quad y_{2}=A \sin (\omega t-k x-\theta)$.
The amplitudeof the medium particle will be
explanation:
The resultant amplitude is given by
$\begin{gathered}A_{r}=\sqrt{A^{2}+A^{2}+2 A A \cos \theta}=\sqrt{2 A^{2}(1+\cos \theta)} \\A_{r}=2 A \cos \dfrac{\theta}{2} \quad\left(\because 1+\cos \theta=2 \cos ^{2} \dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)\end{gathered}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Resultant Amplitude and Intensity in Interference of Waves

question: A plane wave $y=a \sin (k x+c t)$ is incident on a surface. Equation of the reflected wave is: $y^{\prime}=a^{\prime} \sin (c t-k x)$. Then which of the following statement is incorrect? $a^{\prime}$ is greater than $a$
explanation: From the given waves it can be observed that there is no phase inversion for the reflected wave. It means the incident wave is travelling in denser medium. $I_{\text {reflected }} < I_{\text {incident }}$ (some energy always gets transmitted) $\Rightarrow a^{\prime}$ can not be greater than $a$.

options.text: Medium, in which incident wave is travelling is denser than the other medium

subject: Physics
subtopic: Reflection of Waves at Rigid and Free Boundaries

question: $P$ is the junction of two wires $A$ and $B . B$ is made of steel and is thicker while $A$ is made of aluminium and is thinner as shown. If a wave pulse as shown in the figure approches $P$, the reflected and transmitted waves from $P$ are respectively
