question: A sonometer wire resonates with a given tuning fork forming five antinodes in the presence of mass $9\,kg$. When this mass is replaced by a mass $M$, the wire resonates with the same tuning fork forming three antinodes for the same positions of the bridges. The value of $M$ is:
explanation:
In both cases frequencies must be same as same tunning fork is used.
${f_0} = \frac{5}{{2l}}\sqrt {\frac{{9g}}{\mu }} = \frac{3}{{2l}}\sqrt {\frac{{Mg}}{\mu }} $
$\therefore M = 25\,kg$
subject: Physics

question:
Equations of a stationary and travelling waves are as follows
${y_1} = a\sin kx\cos \omega t\,{\text{and }}{y_2} = a\sin (\omega t - kx)$
The phase difference between two points $x_{1}=\dfrac{\pi}{3 k}$ and $x_{2}=\dfrac{3 \pi}{2 k}$ is $\phi_{1}$, in the standing wave $y_{1}$ and is $\phi_{2}$ in travelling wave $\left(y_{2}\right)$ then $\dfrac{\phi_{1}}{\phi_{2}}$ is
explanation:
The positions of the points
$\begin{aligned}& \text { are } x_{1}=\dfrac{\pi}{3 k}=\dfrac{\lambda}{6} \\& x_{2}=\dfrac{3 \pi}{2 k}=\dfrac{3 \lambda}{4} \\& \Delta x=x_{2}-x_{1}=\dfrac{7 \lambda}{12}\end{aligned}$
The points $x_{1}$ and $x_{2}$ are shown the figures.
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950849/preptracker/questions/q_39823_explanation_1764950849151.png
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764950851/preptracker/questions/q_39823_explanation_1764950850630.png
$\Delta {\phi _1} = \pi $
$\frac{{\Delta {\phi _1}}}{{\Delta {\phi _2}}} = \Delta x \times \frac{{2\pi }}{\lambda } = \frac{6}{7}\pi $
If two points are present in the same loop of a standing wave then $\Delta \phi=0$ and if they are present in adjacent loops then $\Delta \phi=\pi$
$\dfrac{\Delta \phi_{1}}{\Delta \phi_{2}}=\dfrac{6}{7}$
subject: Physics

question: The equation for the vibration of a string fixed at both ends vibrating in its third harmonic is given by $y = 2\,cm\sin \left[ {\left( {0.6\,c{m^{ - 1}}} \right)x} \right]\cos \left[ {\left( {500\pi {s^{ - 1}}} \right)t} \right]$ The length of the string is
explanation:
Wave number $k = \frac{{2\pi }}{\lambda } = 0.6\,c{m^{ - 1}}$
$\therefore \frac{\lambda }{2} = \frac{\pi }{{0.6}}\,cm$
$\therefore l = \frac{{3\lambda }}{2} = 3\left( {\frac{\pi }{{0.6}}} \right)cm = 15.7\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: The equation of a wave disturbance is given as $y=0.02 \sin \left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}+50 \pi t\right) \cos (10 \pi x)$, where $x$ and $y$ are in metre and $t$ is in second. Choose the correct statement.
explanation:
Displacement node occurs at
$10\pi x = \frac{\pi }{2},\frac{{3\pi }}{2}{\text{ e}}{\text{.t}}{\text{.c}}...{\text{ }}$
$x = \frac{1}{{20}},\frac{3}{{20}} \ldots $
$x = 0.05\,m{\text{ and }}0.15\,m$
Displacement anti node occurs at
$\begin{aligned}10 \pi x & =0, \pi, 2 \pi, 3 \pi, \text { e.t.c.. } \\x & =0,0.1 m, 0.2 m, 0.3 m\end{aligned}$
$\lambda=2$ (distance between two consecutive nodes (or) anti nodes.)
$\lambda=2(0.1)=0.2 m$
wave speed $ = \frac{\omega }{k} = \frac{{50\pi }}{{10\pi }} = 50\,m/\sec $

options.text: The speed of constituent waves is $0.2\,m/s$

subject: Physics

question:
The equation for the vibration of a string fixed at both ends vibrating in its third harmonic is given by $y=2 \cos (0.6 x) \sin (t)$
The length of the string is
explanation:
Wave number $k = \frac{{2\pi }}{\lambda } = 0.6\,c{m^{ - 1}}$
$\therefore \frac{\lambda }{2} = \frac{\pi }{{0.6}}\,cm$
In third harmonic the length of the string
$\therefore \ell = \frac{{3\lambda }}{2} = 3\left( {\frac{\pi }{{0.6}}} \right)cm = 15.7\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: In a stationary wave represented by $y=2 a \cos (k x) \sin (\omega t)$. The intensity at a certain point is maximum when:
explanation:
In stationary wave $y=2 a \cos k x \sin \omega t$
Amplitude of the stationary wave is
$A=2 a \cos (k x)$
Intensity at certain point is maximum when amplitude is maximum. Intensity is maximum when $\cos (k x)$ is maximum
subject: Physics

question: The fifth harmonic for vibrations of a stretched string is shown in figure. How many nodes are present here?
subject: Physics

question: Two travelling waves ${y_1} = A\sin \left[ {k\left( {x - ct} \right)} \right]$ and ${y_2} = A\sin \left[ {k\left( {x + ct} \right)} \right]$ are superimposed on string. The distance between adjacent nodes is
explanation:
$y=A \sin (k x-k c t)+A \sin (k x+k c t)$
$\begin{aligned}& =2 A \sin \left(\dfrac{k x-k c t+k x+k c t}{2}\right) \cos \left(\dfrac{k x-k c t-k x-k c t}{2}\right) \\& =2 A \sin (k x) \cos (k c t)\end{aligned}$
Thus $\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda}=k, \therefore \quad \lambda=\dfrac{2 \pi}{k}$
The distance between adjacent nodes is $\dfrac{\pi}{k}$
subject: Physics

question: The amplitude of a wave represented by displacement equation $y=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \sin \omega t \pm \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{b}} \cos \omega t$ will be
explanation:
$y=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \sin \omega t \pm \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{b}} \sin \left(\omega t+\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)$
Here phase difference $=\dfrac{\pi}{2}$
Resultant amplitude
$=\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{b}}\right)^{2}+\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{b}}\right)^{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{a}+\dfrac{1}{b}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{a+b}{a b}}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Resultant Amplitude and Intensity in Interference of Waves

question:
The superposing waves are represented by the following equations
$y_{1}=5 \sin 2 \pi(10 t-0.1 x), y_{2}=10 \sin 2 \pi(10 t-0.1 x)$
Ratio of intensities $\dfrac{I_{\max }}{I_{\text {min }}}$ will be
explanation:
$a_{1}=5, a_{2}=10$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\dfrac{\left(a_{1}+a_{2}\right)^{2}}{\left(a_{1}-a_{2}\right)^{2}}=\left(\dfrac{5+10}{5-10}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{9}{1}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Resultant Amplitude and Intensity in Interference of Waves

question: Where should the two bridges be set in a $110\,cm$ wire so that it is divided into three parts and the ratio of the frequencies are $3: 2: 1$ ?
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764949569/preptracker/questions/q_39476_explanation_1764949568805.png
${n_1}:{n_2}:{n_3} = 3:2:1$
$n\alpha \frac{1}{\ell }$ ( $T{\mkern 1mu} $ & $\mu $ are same )
${\ell _1}:{\ell _2}:{\ell _3} = \frac{1}{3}:\frac{1}{2}:1 = 2:3:6$
${\ell _1} + {\ell _2} + {\ell _3} = 110$
$ \Rightarrow 2x + 3x + 6x = 110 \Rightarrow x = 10$
$\therefore$ The two bridges should be set at $2 x$ i.e., 20 $cm$ from one end and $6x$ i.e, $60\,cm$ from the other end.

options.text: $30\,cm$ from one end and $70\,cm$ from other end
$20\,cm$ from one end and $60\,cm$ from other end
$50\,cm$ from one end and $50\,cm$ from other end
$10\,cm$ from one end and $40\,cm$ from other end

subject: Physics

question: Fundamental frequency of sonometer wire is $n$. If the length, tension and diameter of wire are tripled, the new fundamental frequency is
explanation:
$n=\dfrac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\pi r^{2} \rho}} \Rightarrow n \propto \dfrac{\sqrt{T}}{l r}$
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow \dfrac{n_{1}}{n_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}} \times \dfrac{l_{2}}{l_{1}} \times \dfrac{r_{2}}{r_{1}} \\& =\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{3 T}} \times \dfrac{3 l}{l} \times \dfrac{3 r}{r}=3 \sqrt{3} \Rightarrow n_{2}=\dfrac{n}{3 \sqrt{3}}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A brick is hung from a sonometer wire. If the brick is immersed in oil, then frequency of the wire will
explanation:
$f \alpha \sqrt{T} \alpha \sqrt{\text { effective weight of load }}$
Tension in the string decreases because of buyoancy force. So frequency decreases

options.text: Increase due to viscosity of oil

subject: Physics

question: The expression $y=a \sin b x \sin \omega t$ represents a stationary wave. The distance between the consecutive nodes is equal to:
explanation:
$y=a \sin b x \sin \omega t$
On comparing with standard equation of stationary wave
$\begin{aligned}& y=2 a \sin \dfrac{2 \pi x}{\lambda} \cdot \sin \omega t, \text { we get } \\& \dfrac{2 \pi x}{\lambda}=b x, \\& \therefore \lambda=\dfrac{2 \pi}{b}\end{aligned}$
The distance between constructive nodes
$=\dfrac{\lambda}{2}=\dfrac{\pi}{b}$
subject: Physics

question: When a string fixed at both ends vibrates in 1 loop, 2 loops, 3 loops and 4 loops, the frequenices are in the ratio
explanation:
When the string vibrates in $n$ loops, its frequency is $f_{n}=\dfrac{n v}{2 L}$
Where $L$ is the length of the string and $v$ is the velocity of the wave.
$\therefore$ When the string fixed at its both ends vibrates in 1 loop, 2 loops, 3 loops and 4 loops, the frequencies are in the ratio $1: 2: 3: 4$
subject: Physics

question: In order to double the frequency of the fundamental note emitted by a stretched string, the length is reduced to $\dfrac{3}{4}$ $th$ of the original length and the tension is changed. The factor by which the tension is to be changed is
explanation:
$f=\dfrac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}} \Rightarrow f \propto \dfrac{\sqrt{T}}{l}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}=\left(\dfrac{f_{2}}{f_{1}}\right)^{2}\left(\dfrac{l_{2}}{l_{1}}\right)^{2}=(2)^{2}\left(\dfrac{3}{4}\right)^{2}=\dfrac{9}{4}$
subject: Physics

question: The first overtone of a stretched wire of given length in $320\,Hz$. The first harmonic is
explanation:
Frequency of first overtone are second harmonic $\left( {{n_2}} \right) = 320\,Hz$. So, frequency of first harmonic
${n_1} = \frac{{{n_2}}}{2} = \frac{{320}}{2} = 160\,Hz$
subject: Physics

question: A $10\,cm$ long rubber band obeys Hooke's law. When the rubber band is stretched to a total length of $12\,cm$ the lowest resonant frequency is ${f_0}$. The rubber band is then stretched to a length of $13\,cm$. The lowest resonant frequency will now be
explanation:
$f_{0}=\dfrac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$
For elastic wire $T = Kx$
$x \rightarrow$ elongation from natural length
Hence frequency depends on $K$ and original tension

options.text: Changed, but the change (positive or negative) depends on the elastic constant and the original tension

subject: Physics

question: Equation of a standing wave is generally expressed as $y=2 A \sin \omega t \cos k x$. In the equation, quantity $\dfrac{\omega}{k}$ represents
explanation:
Equation of the component waves are;
$y=A \sin (\omega t-k x) \text { and } y=A \sin (\omega t+k x)$
For both waves the speed of component waves is $\dfrac{\omega}{k}$

options.text: The speed of either of the component waves
A quantity that is independent of the properties of the string
The transeverse speed of the particles of the string
The speed of the standing wave

subject: Physics

question: The particle displacement (in $cm$ ) in a stationary wave is given by $y(x, t)=2 \sin (0.1 \pi x) \cos (100 \pi t)$. The distance between a node and the next antiode is
explanation:
$y(x, t)=2 \sin (0.1 \pi x) \cos (100 \pi t)$
compare with $y=A \sin (k x) \cos \omega t$
$k = 0.1\pi = \frac{{2\pi }}{\lambda } \Rightarrow \lambda = 20\,cm$
The distance between a node and next antinode
$ = \frac{\lambda }{4} = \frac{{20}}{4} = 5\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: Two vibrating strings of same length, same cross section area and stretched to same tension is made of materials with densities $\rho$ and $2 \rho$. Each string is fixed at both ends. If $f_{1}$ represents the fundamental mode of vibration of the one made with density $\rho$ and $f_{2}$ for another, then $f_{1} / f_{2}$ is
explanation:
Velocity of sound is inversely proportional to the square root of density of the medium.
$v=\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\rho A}}$
i.e., $v . \rho=$ constant
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_{2}}{\rho_{1}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{2 \rho}{\rho}}=\sqrt{2} \\& \dfrac{f_{1}}{f_{2}}=\dfrac{v_{1}}{v_{1}}=\sqrt{2}\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

$f=\dfrac{(2 n+1) v}{4 l}=\dfrac{7 v}{4 l} \quad(n=3)$

On decreasing the temperature frequency of fundamental tone decreases
Frequency of fundamental tone does not vary with temperature

question: The resultant amplitude due to superposition of two waves $y_{1}=5 \sin (\omega t-k x)$ and $y_{2}=-5 \cos \left(\omega t-k x-150^{\circ}\right)$
explanation:
$y_{1}=5 \sin (\omega t-k x)$
$\begin{aligned}& y_{2}=5 \sin [-(\omega t-k x-150)] \\& y_{2}=5 \sin \left(\omega t-k x+120^{\circ}\right)\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Resultant $=\sqrt{5^{2}+5^{2}+2.5^{2} \cos 120^{\circ}}=5$

question: Two waves represented by $y=a \sin (\omega t-k x)$ and $y_{2}=\sqrt{3} a \cos (\omega t-k x)$ are superposed. The amplitude of the resultant wave is
explanation:

$\therefore \quad a_{R}=\sqrt{a^{2}+(\sqrt{3} a)^{2}+2 \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{3} a \cdot \cos \pi / 2}=2 a$.

question: Two interfering waves have intensities in the ratio $9: 1$. Then the ratio of maximum to minimum intensity is
explanation:
$\dfrac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{I_{1}}+\sqrt{I_{2}}}{\sqrt{I_{1}}-\sqrt{I_{2}}}\right)^{2}=\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{9}+1}{\sqrt{9}-1}\right)^{2}$
$ = {\left( {\frac{4}{2}} \right)^2} = \frac{{16}}{4} = 4$

question: $S_{1}$ and $S_{2}$ are two coherent sources of sound of frequency $110\,Hz$ each. they have no initial phase difference. The intensity at a point $P$ due to $S_{1}$ is $I_{0}$ and due to $S_{2}$ is $4 I_{0}$. If the velocity of sound is $330\,m/s$ then the resultant intensity at $P$ is
explanation:
The wavelength of sound source $=\dfrac{330}{110}=3$ metre.
The phase difference between interfering waves at Pis
$=\Delta x=\dfrac{2 \pi}{\lambda}\left(S_{2} P-S_{1} P\right)=\dfrac{2 \pi}{3}(5-4)=\dfrac{2 \pi}{3}$
$\therefore$ Resultant intensity at $P$ is
$I_{R}=I_{0}+4 I_{0}+2 \sqrt{I_{0}} \sqrt{4 I_{0}} \cos \dfrac{2 \pi}{3}=3 I_{0}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Resultant Amplitude and Intensity in Interference of Waves

question: Two waves having intensity $I$ and $9I$ produce interference. If the resultant intensity at a point is $7I$, what is the phase difference between the two waves?
explanation:
$I_{R}=I_{1}+I_{2}+2 \sqrt{I_{1} I_{2}} \cos \phi$
$7 I=I+9 I+2(3 I) \cos \phi$
$-3 I=6 I \cos \phi$
$\cos \phi=-\dfrac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \varphi=120^{\circ}$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Resultant Amplitude and Intensity in Interference of Waves

question: A sonometer wire supports a $4\,kg$ load and vibrates in fundamental mode with a tuning fork of frequency $416\,Hz$. The length of the wire between the bridges is now doubled. In order to maintain fundamental mode, the load should be changed to
explanation:
Tension in sonometer wire $ = 4\,g$ $\mu=$ mass per unit length
$v=\dfrac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}} \Rightarrow 416=\dfrac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\dfrac{4 g}{\mu}}$
When length is doubled, i.e.,
Let new load $ = M$
As, $v^{\prime}=v$
$\frac{1}{{2{l^\prime }}}\sqrt {\frac{{Mg}}{\mu }} = \frac{1}{{2l}}\sqrt {\frac{{4g}}{\mu }} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{4l}}\sqrt {\frac{{Mg}}{\mu }} = \frac{1}{{2l}}\sqrt {\frac{{4g}}{\mu }} $
$ \Rightarrow \sqrt M = 2 \times 2 \Rightarrow M = 16\,kg$
subject: Physics

question: When the string of a sonometer of length $L$ between the bridges vibrates in the first overtone, the amplitude of vibration is maximum at:
explanation: The string vibrates in two segments in the first overtone. Therefore, the amplitude of vibration is maximum at $(L / 4)$ and $(3 L / 4)$.

explanation:
Middle paper does not fall.

The standing wave pattern is
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957058/preptracker/questions/q_39147_explanation_1764957057803.png
$4x = 2 \times \frac{\lambda }{2} \Rightarrow \lambda = 4x.$

question: One end of string, in which a tension in maintained, is fixed to a rigid support and the free end is made to oscillate transversely by a vibrator at a frequency $f$. Then maximum velocity of propagation of wave in the string will be
explanation:
wavelength is maximum for fundamental mode $\dfrac{\lambda}{4}=l \Rightarrow \lambda_{\max }=4 l$
$\therefore \quad v_{\text {max }}=f \lambda_{\text {max }}=4 f l \text {. }$
subject: Physics

question: A standing wave pattern is formed on a string. One of the waves is given by equation $y_{1}=a \sin \left(\omega t-k x+\dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)$ then the equation of the other wave such that at $x=0$ a node is formed.
explanation:
At $x=0$ the phase difference should be $\pi$.
Thus, the correct option is (4).

question: The equation of a stationary wave is given by $y=6 \sin (\pi x / 3) \cos 40 \pi t$ where $y$ and $x$ are given in $cm$ and time $t$ in second, then the amplitude of progressive wave is
explanation:

$2A = 6{\text{ or }}A = 3\,cm$

question: The tension in a stretched string fixed at both ends is changed by $2 \%$, the fundamental frequency is found to get changed by $15\,Hz$. Select the incorrect statement.
explanation:
$f_{0}=$ fundamental frequency $=\dfrac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$
$\frac{{\Delta {f_0}}}{{{f_0}}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{{\Delta T}}{T} \Rightarrow {f_0} = \frac{{2 \times \Delta {f_0}}}{{(\Delta T/T)}} = \frac{{2 \times 15}}{{(2/100)}}$
$ = 1500\,Hz$
Also, $v=\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}} \quad \therefore \dfrac{\Delta v}{v}=\dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T}{T}=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 2 \%=1 \%$
For fundamental frequency,
$\dfrac{\lambda}{2}=l \Rightarrow \lambda=2 l=\text { constant } .$
So option (2) is correct.

options.text: Wavelength of the string of fundamental frequency does not change
Velocity of propagation of wave changes by $2 \%$
Velocity of propagation of wave changes by $1 \%$

subject: Physics

question: A string with a mass density of $4 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,kg/m$ is under tension of $360\,N$ and is fixed at both ends. One of its resonance frequencies is $375\,Hz$. The next higher resonance frequency is$450\,Hz$. The mass of the string is :-
explanation:
The frequency $f=\dfrac{n}{2 L} \sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$
Given that
$\frac{n}{{2L}}\sqrt {\frac{T}{\mu }} {\text{ }} \sim \frac{{n + 1}}{{2L}}\sqrt {\frac{T}{\mu }} = 450 - 375$
${f_1} = \frac{1}{{2L}}\sqrt {\frac{T}{\mu }} = 450 - 375 = 75$
$L = \frac{1}{{2 \times 75}} \times \sqrt {\frac{{360}}{{4 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}} = \frac{{300}}{{150}} = 2\,m$
$m = \mu \times 2 = 8 \times {10^{ - 3}}\,kg$
subject: Physics

question: A wire under tension vibrates with a fundamental frequency of $1500\,Hz$. If the length of the wire is doubled, the radius is halved and the wire is made to vibrate under one-ninth the tension. Then the fundamental frequency will become :-
explanation:
The fundamental frequency is
$1500 = \frac{{\sqrt {\frac{T}{\mu }} }}{{2\ell }} = {f_0}$
${T^\prime } = \frac{T}{9}\,\& \,{\ell ^\prime } = 2\ell $
$\mu^{\prime}=\dfrac{m^{\prime}}{l^{\prime}}=\dfrac{\rho \pi r^{\prime 2} l^{\prime}}{l^{\prime}}=\dfrac{\rho \pi r^{2}}{4}=\dfrac{\mu}{4}$
${f^\prime } = \frac{{\sqrt {\frac{{{T^\prime }}}{{{\mu ^\prime }}}} }}{{2{\ell ^\prime }}} = \frac{{\sqrt {\frac{{\frac{T}{9}}}{{\frac{\mu }{4}}}} }}{{2(2\ell )}} = \frac{1}{3}{f_0} = 500\,Hz$
subject: Physics

question: A stretched wire of length $110\,cm$ is divided into three segments whose frequencies are in ratio 1:2:3. Their lengths must be:
explanation:
Given, ${l_1} + {l_2} + {l_3} = 110\,cm$
Here tension and linear mass density must be same for all.
and $f_{1} l_{1}=f_{2} l_{2}=f_{3} l_{3}$
$f_{1}: f_{2}: f_{3}=1: 2: 3$
$\therefore \dfrac{f_{1}}{f_{2}}=\dfrac{1}{2}=\dfrac{l_{2}}{l_{1}} \Rightarrow l_{2}=\dfrac{l_{1}}{2}$
and $\frac{{{f_1}}}{{{f_3}}} = \frac{1}{3} = \frac{{{l_3}}}{{{l_1}}} \Rightarrow {l_3} = \frac{{{l_1}}}{3}$
$\therefore {l_1} + \frac{{{l_1}}}{2} + \frac{{{l_1}}}{3} = 110 \Rightarrow {l_1} = 60\,cm$
So, ${l_1} = 60\,cm,{l_2} = 30\,cm,\,{\text{and}}\,{\text{ }}{l_3} = 20\,cm$
subject: Physics

question: A string of length $240\,cm$ is connected to two light rings which can freely move along two vertical rods. The maximum three wavelengths of standing waves that can be formed in the string are $cm$
explanation:

antinodes.

$240 = \lambda \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \therefore \lambda = 240\,cm$
$240 = \frac{{3\lambda }}{2}\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} \therefore \lambda = 160\,cm$

question:
Two waves having equations
$x_{1}=a \sin \left(\omega t+\varphi_{1}\right), x_{2}=a \sin \left(\omega t+\varphi_{2}\right)$
If in the resultant wave the frequency and amplitude remain equal to those of superimposing waves. Then phase difference between them is
explanation:
Superposition of waves does not alter the frequency of resultant wave and resultant amplitude
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow a^{2}=a^{2}+a^{2}+2 a^{2} \cos \varphi=2 a^{2}(1+\cos \varphi) \\& \Rightarrow \cos \varphi=-1 / 2=\cos 2 \pi / 3 \therefore \varphi=2 \pi / 3 .\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: Two periodic waves of intensities $I_{1}$ and $I_{2}$ pass through a region at the same time in the same direction. The sum of the maximum and minimum intensities is
explanation:
${I_{\max {\text{ }}}} = {I_1} + {I_2} + 2\sqrt {{I_1}{I_2}} $
${I_{\max {\text{ }}}} = {I_1} + {I_2} - 2\sqrt {{I_1}{I_2}} $
$\therefore$ Sum of maximum and minimum intensities
$=2\left(I_{1}+I_{2}\right) \text {. }$
subject: Physics
subtopic: Resultant Amplitude and Intensity in Interference of Waves

question: A sinusoidal wave with amplitude $y_{m}$ is travelling with speed $v$ on a string with linear density $\mu$. The angular frequency of the wave is $\omega$. Mark the one which is correct (only one parameter is changed at a time).
explanation:
Rate of energy flow is given by
$P = \frac{1}{2}{a^2}{\omega ^2}\mu \cdot v$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} P\alpha v$

options.text: Doubling the frequency doubles the rate at which energy is carried along the string
If the amplitude were doubled, the rate at which energy is carried would be halved
If the amplitude were doubled, the rate at which energy is carried would be doubled
The rate at which energy is carried is directly proportional to the velocity of the wave

subject: Physics

question: A stretched wire will produce a note of very high frequency, then the nature of the wire is
explanation:
Frequency of a stretched string is

For $f$ to be high $L$ is small, $T$ is large and $\mu$ is also small.

options.text: Thin and short wire of light material under high tension
Thick and short wire of light material under high tension
Thin and long wire of light material under high tension
Thin and short wire of heavy material under high tension.

subject: Physics
